title: Abhidhammamātikā: The Matrix from the Abstract Teaching
description: A Pāli-English interlinear edition of the opening section of the Abhidhamma, consisting mostly of important lists. It lays the philosophical and psychological foundations for the rest of the Abhidhamma books.

title: Abhidhammatthasaṅgaha: A Comprehensive Manual of Abhidhamma
description: Ācariya Anuruddha's compendium, providing a concise summary of the Abhidhamma, became the standard introductory "textbook" for the Abhidhamma in the Theravāda world since it was composed some time around the 10th century.

title: AN 1.11–20 Nīvaraṇappahāna Vagga: The Chapter on Giving Up the Hindrances
description: > Monks, I do not see a single thing that gives rise to sense desire like the feature of attractiveness.

title: AN 1.296-305 Paṭhama Ekadhamma Vagga: The First Chapter on One Thing
description:
> This one thing, when developed and cultivated, leads solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace...

Ten themes the Buddha recommends for meditation.

title: AN 1.50–53: Pabhassara Suttas
description: > Luminous, monks, is the mind.

title: AN 10.1 Kimatthiya Sutta: What’s the Purpose?
description:
> And what, Bhante, is the purpose and benefit of the knowledge and vision of things as they really are?

The purpose of ethics, concentration, and wisdom are that they lead to liberation.

title: AN 10.104 Bīja Sutta: A Seed
description:
> Their intentions, aims, wishes, and choices all lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, beneficial, and pleasant. Why is that? Because their view is good.

When a person has wrong view, all their path development is wrong. But when they have right view, everything good follows.

title: AN 10.11 Senāsana Sutta: Lodgings
description: Five factors that a mendicant should have, and five factors a lodging should have, for meditation progress to be swift.

title: AN 10.117 Saṅgārava Sutta: With Saṅgārava
description:
> Few are those among humans
who cross to the far shore.

The wrong path is the near shore where most people dwell; the right path is the far shore, where few have crossed over.

title: AN 10.12 Pañcaṅga Sutta: Five Factors
description: An Arahant has overcome the five hindrances and possesses five factors which may be considered their opposites.

title: AN 10.121 Pubbaṅgama Sutta: Forerunner
description:
> … right view is the forerunner and precursor of skillful qualities

<!---->

title: AN 10.13 Saṁyojana Sutta: The Fetters
description:
> Bhikkhus, there are these ten fetters. 

Five lower and five higher.

title: AN 10.15 Appamāda Sutta: Diligence
description:
> So too, all wholesome qualities are rooted in heedfulness and converge upon heedfulness

Diligence is the foremost of all good qualities.

title: AN 10.17 Paṭhamanl Nātha Sutta: The First Discourse on [Having] a Protector
description:
> Bhikkhus, live under a protector, not without a protector.
One without a protector lives in suffering.
There are these ten qualities that serve as a protector.

title: AN 10.177 Jāṇussoṇin Sutta: To Jāṇussoṇi (On Offerings to the Dead)
description:
> But does this gift really aid departed relatives and family? Do they actually partake of it?

The Buddha informs a brahmin that gifts offered to dead ancestors can only go to ancestors reborn in the ghost realm, but assures him that the gift yields a reward for the donor no matter where they are r

title: AN 10.2 Cetanākaraṇīya Sutta: Making a Wish
description:
> It is natural that non-regret arises in a virtuous person, one whose behavior is virtuous.

There’s no need to make a wish to get enlightened; it happens naturally when the conditions are there.

title: AN 10.21 Sīhanāda Sutta: The Lion’s Roar
description: Like a lion, a Realized One roars his preeminence based on his ten, special powers, which enable him to teach the Dhamma.

title: AN 10.211 Paṭhama Nirayasagga Sutta: The First Discourse on Heaven and Hell
description: The ten kinds of bad deeds that lead you to hell and the ten good deeds that lead to heaven.

title: AN 10.216 Saṁsappanīya Sutta: Creeping
description:
> The snake, the scorpion, the centipede, the mongoose, the cat, the mouse, and the owl, or any other animals that creep away when they see people. Thus a being is reborn from a being; one is reborn through one’s deeds.

Those who do the ten kinds of bad deeds are like creepy creatures and are rebor

title: AN 10.224 An Untitled Discourse on Forty Qualities
description: > Someone with forty qualities is cast down to hell...

title: AN 10.23 Kāya Sutta: Body
description:
> Greed is to be abandoned neither by body nor by speech...

Some things are to be abandoned through wisdom.

title: AN 10.26 Kāḷī Sutta: With Kāḷī
description:
> Some ascetics and brahmins regard the attainment of the meditation on universal water to be the ultimate.

The female lay follower Kāḷī of Kuraraghara in Avantī asks Venerable Mahākaccāna about a verse spoken by the Buddha in “The Maidens’ Questions” ([SN 4.25](/content/canon/sn4.25)).
He replies

title: AN 10.29: Kosala Sutta
description:
> … even for King Pasenadi there is alteration; there is change

A sutta on using *anicca* to make the transition from *samatha* to *vipassanā*.

title: AN 10.30 Dutiyakosala Sutta: The Second Discourse at Kosala
description:
> King Pasenadi of Kosala had returned from the war front, victorious in battle, his purpose having been achieved. Then King Pasenadi of Kosala set out for the park.

Fresh from battle, King Pasenadi declares his love and devotion to the Buddha.

title: AN 10.31 Upāli Sutta: With Upāli
description:
> Bhante, on how many grounds has the Tathāgata prescribed the training rules for his disciples and recited the Pātimokkha?

The Ten Reasons the Buddha laid down the monastic rules.

title: AN 10.34 Upasampadā Sutta: Ordination
description: Ten qualities a mendicant should have to give ordination.

title: AN 10.42 Paṭhamavivādamūla Sutta: The Roots of Arguments
description:
> … when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as the teaching...

Ten roots for disputes in the Saṅgha.

title: AN 10.44 Kusināra Sutta: At Kusinārā
description:
> … a mendicant who wants to accuse another should first check five things in themselves and establish five things in themselves

<!---->

title: AN 10.48 Pabbajita Abhiṇha Sutta: Ten Regular Reflections for a Renunciate
description:
> 'I must now behave in a different manner.'
> This must be reflected upon again and again by one who has gone forth.

<!---->

title: AN 10.50 Bhaṇḍana Sutta: Arguments
description:
> There are ten warm-hearted qualities that make for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without quarreling. What ten?

<!---->

title: AN 10.51 Sa Citta Sutta: One's Own Mind
description:
> … how is a bhikkhu skilled in the ways of his own mind?

The Buddha teaches that if you can’t read anyone else’s mind, read your own by regular self-reflection.

title: AN 10.54 Samatha Sutta: Serenity
description: One imagines this sutta was delivered to a group of monks frustrated with an erratic companion. The Buddha gently encourages them to develop empathy by cultivating themselves and to recognize that, in the final analysis, some people are simply best avoided.

title: AN 10.58 Mūlaka Sutta: Rooted
description:
> Reverends, all things are rooted in desire. Attention produces them.

The Buddha gives an extraordinary ten-point summary of the path from things to the cessation of things.

title: AN 10.6 Samādhi Sutta: Immersion
description: The Buddha tells Ānanda how an Ariya can attain the so-called "ninth jhāna" by recalling the qualities of nibbāna.

title: AN 10.60 Girimānanda Sutta: The Discourse to Girimānanda
description:
> … having heard these ten perceptions, venerable Girimānanda’s afliction immediately abated

A profound discourse on Vipassana meditation in an apotropaic frame.

title: AN 10.61 Avijjā Sutta: (The Fuel for) Ignorance
description: The Buddha gives the causal chain that leads to ignorance and the chain leading to the Awakening Factors.

title: AN 10.64 Aveccappasanna Sutta: Experiential Confidence
description:
> ... the one who is extinguished without extra effort, the one who is extinguished with extra effort, and the one who heads upstream..

A rare sutta, showing ten stages of enlightenment.

title: AN 10.67 Paṭhamanaḷakapāna Sutta: At Naḷakapāna (1st)
description: > It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, light, and diameter and circumference only grow. In the same way, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect growth, not decline, in skillf

title: AN 10.68 Dutiyanaḷakapāna Sutta: At Naḷakapāna (2nd)
description:
> who wants to listen, memorizes the teachings, examines their meaning, and practices accordingly, and is diligent when it comes to skillful qualities can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night.

At Naḷakapāna the Buddha invites Sāriputta to teach. He speaks of

title: AN 10.69 Paṭhamakathāvatthu Sutta: Topics of Discussion
description:
> There are, mendicants, these ten topics of discussion...

<!---->

title: AN 10.7 Sāriputta Sutta: Sāriputta
description:
Ānanda asks Sāriputta about the perception within the enigmatic "ninth jhāna."

For the Buddha's instructions on attaining this state, see [the previous sutta](/content/canon/an10.6).

title: AN 10.71 Ākaṅkha Sutta: One Might Wish
description:
> A mendicant might wish: ‘May I be liked and approved by my spiritual companions, respected and admired.’ So let them fulfill their precepts, be committed to inner serenity of the heart, not neglect absorption, be endowed with discernment, and frequent empty huts.

If a mendicant wishes to attain s

title: AN 10.72 Kaṇṭaka Sutta: Thorns
description: When the Buddha receives noisy visitors, several monks withdraw to a nearby forest to meditate. The Buddha praises them, saying that noise is indeed a thorn to absorption.

title: AN 10.73 Iṭṭha Dhamma Sutta: Likable Things
description:
> Ten things hinder the ten likable, desirable, and agreeable things that are rare in the world.

<!---->

title: AN 10.74 Vaḍḍhi Sutta: Growth
description:
> Mendicants, a noble disciple who grows in ten ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. What ten?

What do people accumulate to be happy and successful?

title: AN 10.81 Vāhana Sutta: With Bāhuna
description:
> Though it sprouted and grew in the water, it would rise up above the water and stand with no water clinging to it. In the same way, the Realized One has escaped

Giving up ten things, the Buddha lives free of limits.

title: AN 10.84 Byākaraṇa Sutta: Declaration
description:
> When there is still more to be done, this venerable stopped half-way after achieving some insignificant distinction. But stopping half-way means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.

Ten qualities which tell you that someone isn't an arahant.

title: AN 10.88 Akkosaka Sutta: An Abuser
description:
> Any mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions, speaking ill of the noble ones, will, without a doubt, fall ...

<!---->

title: AN 10.91 Kāmabhogī Sutta: Pleasure Seekers
description:
> They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise.

The Buddha explains—and ranks!—the ten ways of seeking wealth.

title: AN 10.93 Kiṁdiṭṭhika Sutta: What Is Your View?
description: Wanderers from other sects share their views with Anāthapiṇḍika, who declares his own view--and why it's not pessimistic.

title: AN 10.94 Vajjiyamāhita Sutta: With Vajjiya
description:
> ‘This contemplative Gotama whom you praise is a nihilist, one who doesn’t declare anything.’  
‘I tell you, venerable sirs, that the Blessed One righteously declares that “This is skillful.” He declares that “This is unskillful.”’

The householder Vajjiya Māhita visits some wanderers and the Buddh

title: AN 10.95 Uttiya Sutta: With Uttiya
description:
> ‘When Master Gotama teaches in this way, is the whole world saved, or half, or a third?’ But when he said this, the Buddha kept silent.

Venerable Ānanda answers on the Buddha's behalf with the simile of the citadel.

title: AN 10.99 Upāli Sutta: With Upāli
description:
> Upāli, it’s not easy to endure isolated wilderness & forest lodgings. It’s not easy to maintain seclusion, not easy to enjoy being alone. The forests, as it were, plunder the mind of a monk who has not gained concentration.

When Upāli asks to go into retreat, the Buddha warns him that secluded wi

title: AN 11.13 Nandiya Sutta: With Nandiya
description:
> The faithful succeed, not the faithless. The ethical succeed, not the unethical. The energetic succeed, not the lazy. The mindful succeed, while the unmindful do not.

Nandiya the Sakiyan moves to Sāvatthī to be near the Buddha.
At the end of the rains, he asks the Buddha’s advice on how to live a

title: AN 11.15 Mettā Sutta: The Benefits of Love
description:
> … you can expect eleven benefits when the heart’s release by love has been cultivated, developed, and practiced, made a vehicle and a basis, kept up, consolidated, and properly implemented.

Asked by a householder to teach a path to freedom, Venerable Ānanda explains no less than eleven meditative

title: AN 11.17 Gopāla Sutta: The Cowherd
description:
> Mendicants, a cowherd with eleven factors can’t maintain and expand a herd of cattle. What eleven?

For this sutta's lengthy repetition series, see [AN 11.22–981](/content/canon/an11.22-981).

title: AN 11.2 Cetanākaraṇīya Sutta: Make a Wish
description:
> It’s only natural that joy springs up when you have no regrets.

This lovely sutta reassures us that the path to awakening is the natural result of cultivating and perfecting ethics.

title: AN 11.22-981 Gopāla Vagga: The Series on the Cowherd
description:
> In the same way, a mendicant with eleven qualities can meditate observing impermanence in the eye … meditate observing letting go.

The formulaic expansion of [AN 11.17](/content/canon/an11.17) into 960 suttas.

title: AN 11.6 Byasana Sutta: Disasters
description:
> Mendicants, any mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions, speaking ill of the noble ones, will, without a doubt, fall into one or other of these eleven disasters.

<!---->

title: AN 11.7 Saññā Sutta: Percipient
description: Ānanda asks the Buddha about a deep state of meditation where all normal perception has ceased, but there is still perception. The Buddha affirms that such a state exists. Ānanda puts the same question to Sāriputta, and gets the same answer.

title: AN 2.1 Vajja Sutta: Punishments
description: > You should train like this: ‘We will fear the fault apparent in the present life, and we will fear the fault to do with lives to come.’

title: AN 2.130-140 Āyācana Vagga: Aspiration
description: > A faithful laywoman would rightly aspire: ‘May I be like the laywomen Khujjuttarā and [Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother](/content/canon/an7.53)!’

title: AN 2.39 Balavanta Cora Sutta: The discourse on weak kings
description: > At a time when bandits are strong, kings are weak...

title: AN 2.47 Parisa Vagga (6): Two Assemblies
description: > But when discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited the mendicants do want to listen. They pay attention and apply their minds

title: AN 2.67 Sukha Vagga (4)
description:
> Defiled happiness and undefiled happiness.
These are the two kinds of happiness.

title: AN 3.100 Loṇakapalla Sutta: A Lump of Salt
description:
> What kind of person isn’t thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars? A person who is rich, affluent, and wealthy.

Karma, contrary to later oversimplifications, is not a strict formula, whereby a certain action always has the same result.

title: AN 3.101 Paṁsudhovaka Sutta: A Panner
description:
> When they’ve been given up and eliminated, there are fine corruptions: thoughts of family, country, and being looked up to. A sincere, capable mendicant gives these up, gets rid of, eliminates, and obliterates them.

Meditation is like purifying gold. A meditator should progressively eliminate mor

title: AN 3.104 Paṭhamaassāda Sutta: Gratification
description:
> I went in search of the world’s gratification, and I found it.

The Buddha became awakened by understanding gratification, as well as its danger and escape.

title: AN 3.109 Arakkhita Sutta: Unprotected
description:
> It’s like a bungalow with a bad roof. The roof peak, rafters, and walls are unprotected. They get soaked, and become rotten.

Protecting your mind is like protecting a house.

title: AN 3.113 Āpāyika Sutta: Bound for Loss
description:
> Mendicants, three kinds of people are bound for a place of loss, bound for hell...

<!---->

title: AN 3.114 Dullabha Sutta: Rare
tags: cosmology # tactfully avoid stepping on AN 5.143
description:
> … the appearance of three people is rare in the world...

<!---->

title: AN 3.118 Apaṇṇaka Sutta: Loaded Dice
description:
> Mendicants, there are three failures.

<!---->

title: AN 3.119 Kammanta Sutta: Action
description: What constitutes failure or success in life?

title: AN 3.124 Bhaṇḍana Sutta: Arguments
description:
> Mendicants, I’m not even comfortable thinking about a place where mendicants argue…

When mendicants fight, the Buddha doesn’t like it.

title: AN 3.127 Hatthaka Sutta: With Hatthaka
description:
> … he sank and melted down and wasn’t able to stay still. It’s like when ghee or oil is poured onto sand: it sinks and melts down, and can’t remain

A god from the Pure Abodes visits the Buddha and complains about how busy he is.

title: AN 3.128 Kaṭuviya Sutta: Bitter
description:
> Monk, don’t be bitter. If you’re bitter, corrupted by putrefaction, flies will, without a doubt, plague and infest you.

The Buddha helps a monk in distress by teaching of “bitterness”, “rotting flesh”, and “insects”.

title: AN 3.130 Dutiya Anuruddha Sutta: The Second Discourse With Anuruddha
description:
> Well, Reverend Anuruddha, when you say: ‘With clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, I survey the entire galaxy,’ that’s your conceit.

Anuruddha receives a sharp teaching.

title: AN 3.132 Lekha Sutta: An Inscription
description:
> And how is an individual like an inscription in rock?

<!---->

title: AN 3.133 Yodhājīva Sutta: An Archer
description: A mendicant is like a king's star archer if they are a long-distance shooter, a marksman, and one who shatters large objects.

title: AN 3.135 Mitta Sutta: A Friend
description: A good friend does the hard thing.

title: AN 3.136 Uppādā Sutta: Arising
description:
> Monks, whether Tathāgatas arise or not, this aspect of the world remains the same...

Impermanence, suffering, and not-self are natural laws discovered by the Buddha.

title: AN 3.155 Pubbaṇha Sutta: Morning
description: > have a good morning

title: AN 3.20 Dutiyapāpaṇika Sutta: The Second Discourse About A Shopkeeper
description:
> Bhikkhus, possessing three factors, a shopkeeper soon attains vast and abundant wealth...

> And how does a bhikkhu have benefactors?

Shopkeepers and mendicants both have to be clever, responsible, and well supported.

title: AN 3.25 Vajirūpama Sutta: A Diamond
description:
> … what is the person whose mind is like a diamond?

Showing the Canonical basis for the "Vajra" image which would become important in later Buddhism.

title: AN 3.26 Sevitabba Sutta: Associates
description:
> There is a person who is not to be associated with...

You should associate with people who are equal or better than you.

title: AN 3.28 Gūthabhāṇī Sutta: Speech Like Dung
description:
> … what, bhikkhus, is the person whose speech is like dung?

<!---->

title: AN 3.29 Andha Sutta: Blind
description:
> The blind person, the one-eyed person, and the two-eyed person.

In which the Buddha dismisses the possibility that one could be ethically wise but materially foolish.

title: AN 3.32 Ānanda Sutta: With Ānanda
description:
> ... could a bhikkhu obtain such a state of concentration that he would have no I-making, mine-making, and underlying tendency to conceit...?

The Buddha instructs Ānanda on taking Nibbāna as an object of meditation.

title: AN 3.34 Nidāna Sutta: Sources
description:
> ...a mendicant arousing knowledge  
of the outcome of greed, hate, and delusion,  
would cast off all bad destinies.

Greed, hatred, and delusion as planting karmic "seeds."

title: AN 3.35 Hatthaka Sutta: With Hatthaka
description:
> I am one of those in the world who sleep well.

The Buddha sleeps well, even on cold, hard ground.

title: AN 3.36 Devadūta Sutta: Divine Messengers
description:
> Good man, didn’t you see the third divine messenger that appeared among human beings?

The Buddhist "judgment day."

title: AN 3.37 Catumahārāja Sutta: The Four Great Kings (1)
description:
> On the eighth day of the fortnight, mendicants, the ministers and counselors of the Four Great Kings wander about the world...

The gods rejoice when they see people practicing well.

title: AN 3.39 Sukhumāla Sutta: A Delicate Lifestyle
description:
> There are, bhikkhus, these three kinds of intoxication.

Siddhattha’s delicate upbringing.

title: AN 3.40 Ādhipateyya Sutta: Authorities
description:
> Bhikkhus, there are these three authorities. What three? Oneself as one’s authority, the world as one’s authority, and the Dhamma as one’s authority.

<!---->

title: AN 3.41 Sammukhībhāva Sutta: Present
description: The faithful make merit when three factors are present.

title: AN 3.42 Tiṭhāna Sutta: Cases
description:
> … in three cases one may be understood to have faith and confidence

How to know a faithful person.

title: AN 3.47 Saṅkhatalakkhaṇa Sutta: Characteristics of the (Un) Conditioned
description:
> … conditioned phenomena have these three characteristics...

The difference between the conditioned and the unconditioned.

title: AN 3.52 Dutiya Dve Brāhmaṇa Sutta: The Second Discourse to Two Brahmins
description: Giving secures your wealth in the next life, like a pot lent out from a burning house.

title: AN 3.55 Nibbuta Sutta: Extinguished
description:
> In what way is extinguishment apparent in the present life ... ?

<!---->

title: AN 3.57 Vacchagotta Sutta: With Vacchagotta
description:
> one acquires merit even if one throws away dishwashing water in a refuse dump or cesspit with the thought: ‘May the living beings here sustain themselves with this!’

The Buddha is falsely accused of preventing gifts to other communities, but agrees that gifts to the Noble Ones are the most fruitf

title: AN 3.61 Titthāyatana Sutta: Sectarian Tenets
description:
> Those who fall back on God’s creative activity as the essential truth have no desire to do what should be done and to avoid doing what should not be done...

The beliefs that everything is caused by past karma, by a creator God, or by chance all lead to apathy.
The Buddha teaches us to instead ana

title: AN 3.62 Bhaya Sutta: Perils
description:
> When villages, towns, and cities are burning up, there is sometimes an occasion when the mother finds her son and the son finds his mother.

Perils that tear mothers and children apart.

title: AN 3.63 Venāgapura Sutta: Venāga
description:
> Just as a palm fruit that has just been removed from its stalk is pure and bright, so Master Gotama’s faculties are tranquil and the color of his skin is pure and bright.

What high and luxurious bed does the Buddha use?

title: AN 3.65 Kesamutti Sutta: With the Kesaputtiya Kālāmas
description:
> Kālāmas, do not go by oral tradition, by lineage of teaching, by hearsay, by a collection of scriptures, by logic...

In this famous sutta, the Buddha outlines a practical epistemology.

title: AN 3.66 Sāḷha Sutta: To Salha
description: > He understands thus: ‘Formerly there was greed which was bad, and now there is none, which is good. Formerly there was hate, which was bad, and now there is none, which is good. Formerly there was delusion, which was bad, and now there is none, which is good.’ So here and now in this very life he

title: AN 3.68 Aññatitthiya Sutta: Followers of Other Religions
description: What is the difference between greed, hatred, and delusion?

title: AN 3.70 Uposatha Sutta: Sabbath
description: The Buddha describes how a lay Buddhist should keep a sabbath day: by recollecting the triple gem together with the gods and by keeping the moral precepts beloved and kept by the noble ones.

title: AN 3.72 Ājīvaka Sutta: A Disciple of the Ājīvakas
description: The Buddha draws out his interlocutor's own wisdom to answer a tricky question.

title: AN 3.73 Mahānāmasakka Sutta: With Mahānāma the Sakyan
description: Does convergence come first, or knowledge?

title: AN 3.75 Nivesaka Sutta: Support
description:
> friends and colleagues, relatives and family should be encouraged, supported, and established in three things.

<!---->

title: AN 3.76 Paṭhamabhava Sutta: Continued Existence (1)
description:
> So, Ānanda, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture.

How consciousness, karma, and craving create and sustain future lives.

title: AN 3.78 Sīlabbata Sutta: Precepts and Observances
description:
> Ānanda, are all precepts and observances, lifestyles, and spiritual paths fruitful?

Not all paths go up the same mountain.

title: AN 3.79 Gandhajāta Sutta: Fragrances
description: One fragrance that spreads even against the wind.

title: AN 3.80 Cūḷanikā Sutta: Lesser
description: Ānanda gets the Buddha to talk about the scale of the universe.

title: AN 3.82 Gadrabha Sutta: The Donkey
description: An ass might follow the cows, but if it can't moo...

title: AN 3.84 Vajjiputta Sutta: A Vajjian
description:
> Monk, can you train in reference to the three trainings: the training in heightened virtue, the training in heightened mind, the training in heightened discernment?

What to do if you're having trouble remembering all the rules.

title: AN 3.86 Paṭhama Sikkhā Sutta: The First Discourse on the Training
description:
> ... over a hundred and fifty training rules come up for recitation, in which gentlemen who love themselves train.

Even spiritually advanced people can break the minor rules, but striving to keep them is still worthwhile.

title: AN 3.87 Dutiya Sekhin Sutta: The Second Discourse on One in Training
description:
Even the enlightened can break the minor rules.
Yet, training in the rules is still important.

title: AN 3.93 Paviveka Sutta: Seclusion
description:
> Wanderers of other religions advocate three kinds of seclusion. What three? Seclusion in robes, almsfood, and lodgings.

While other religions' monastics focus on external seclusion, the Buddha taught his monastics to be inwardly restrained.

title: AN 3.99 Potthaka Sutta: Jute
description:
> If a senior mendicant is unethical, of bad character, this is how they’re ugly, I say. … If you associate with, accompany, and attend to that person, following their example, it’ll be for your lasting harm and suffering. …

A bad mendicant is like hemp: uncomfortable to be in close contact with.

title: AN 4.105 Amba Sutta: Mangoes
description:
> One is unripe but seems ripe,  
One is ripe but seems unripe...

Four people similar to mangoes.

title: AN 4.107 Mūsika Sutta: Mice
description:
> How does a person both make a hole and live in it?

Four people similar to mice.

title: AN 4.110 Āsīvisa Sutta: Vipers
description:
> … these four people similar to vipers are found in the world

<!---->

title: AN 4.113 Patoda Sutta: The Goad
description:
> Some excellent thoroughbred people are like this

A thoroughbred responds when it sees a goad.

title: AN 4.114 Nāga Sutta: A Royal Elephant
description:
> … a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings

A mendicant like a king’s elephant.

title: AN 4.118 Saṁvejanīya Sutta: Inspiring
description:
> … four inspiring places that a faithful gentleman should go to see

<!---->

title: AN 4.12 Sīla Sutta: Ethics
description: > Having undertaken them, train in the training rules. When you have done so, what further should be done?

title: AN 4.122 Ūmibhaya Sutta: The Danger of Waves
description:
> … a gentleman who goes forth from the lay life to homelessness in this teaching and training should anticipate four dangers

The risks of (metaphorical) waves, crocodiles, whirlpools, and sharks.

title: AN 4.125 Paṭhama Mettā Sutta: The First Discourse on Loving-Kindness
description:
> Firstly, a person meditates spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth...

The Brahma Vihāras lead to rebirth in the Brahma Realm. And from there, it depends...

title: AN 4.13 Padhāna Sutta: Striving
description:
> … there are these four right strivings. What four?

A definition of Right Effort.

title: AN 4.147 Dutiyakāla Sutta: Times (2)
description:
> [There is] a time for listening to the teaching, a time for discussing the teaching, a time for serenity, and a time for discernment.

<!---->

title: AN 4.162 Vitthāra Sutta: In Detail
description:
> Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. What four?  
1. Painful practice with slow insight,
2. painful practice with swift insight,
3. pleasant practice with slow insight, and
4. pleasant practice with swift insight.

<!---->

title: AN 4.164 Khama Sutta: Tolerant
description:
> Monks, there are these four modes of practice. Which four? Intolerant practice, tolerant practice, self-controlled practice, and even practice.

<!---->

title: AN 4.17 Paṭhamaagati Sutta: The First Discourse on Wrong Courses
description:
> … there are these four ways of taking a wrong course

Making decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice will lead in a bad direction.

title: AN 4.170 Yuganaddha Sutta: In Conjunction
description:
> All of the monks and nuns who declare in my presence that they have attained perfection, did so by one or other of four paths.

Ways of practicing serenity and discernment.

title: AN 4.170 Yuganaddha Sutta: In Tandem
description: > … whoever---monk or nun---declares the attainment of arahantship in my presence, they all do it by means of one or another of four paths.

title: AN 4.174 Ānanda Sutta: With Ānanda
description:
> The scope of the six fields of contact extends as far as the scope of proliferation. When the six fields of contact fade away and cease with nothing left over, proliferation stops and is stilled.

What is there when the senses cease?

title: AN 4.176 Āyācana Sutta: Aspiration
description:
> A faithful laywoman would rightly aspire: ‘May I be like the laywomen Khujjuttarā and [Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother](/content/canon/an7.53)!’

Leading examples for the four assemblies.

title: AN 4.178 Jambālī Sutta: Billabong
description:
> They focus on the cessation of identification, and their mind is eager, confident, settled, and decided about it. You’d expect that mendicant to stop identifying.

A rare sutta about cessation attainment and the final leap.

title: AN 4.179 Nibbāna Sutta: Extinguishment
description:
> What is the cause, Reverend Sāriputta, what is the reason why some sentient beings aren’t fully extinguished in the present life?

For the Buddha's answer to this question, see [SN 35.131](/content/canon/sn35.131).

title: AN 4.180 Mahāpadesa Sutta: The Four Great References
description:
> You should neither approve nor dismiss that mendicant’s statement. Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should ...

How to determine if something is an authentic teaching of the Buddha.

title: AN 4.182 Pāṭibhoga Sutta: Guarantee
description: There are some things no-one can guarantee.

title: AN 4.184 Abhaya Sutta: Fearless
description: The Buddha assures a layman that some people, while subject to death, have truly overcome the fear of death.

title: AN 4.191 Sotānugata Sutta: Followed by Ear
description:
> … when the teachings have been followed by ear, recited by speech, examined by mind, and well penetrated by view, four rewards can be expected

The Buddha assures us that we can attain stream entry as a *deva*.

title: AN 4.192 Ṭhāna Sutta: Facts
description:
> Judging by this fish’s approach, by the ripples it makes, and by its force, it’s a big fish, not a little one.

How to assess a person’s ethics, purity, resilience, and wisdom.

title: AN 4.195 Vappa Sutta: With Vappa
description:
A Jain follower espouses a version of fatalism which the Buddha then refutes.

This somewhat confusing discourse has a parallel in [MĀ 12](/content/monographs/ma1_bdk), which I recommend reading alongside this account as it helps to clarify things somewhat.

title: AN 4.196 Sāḷha Sutta: With Sāḷha
course: imagery # Included in Hecker's similes also
description: The Buddha explains that ethics is necessary but insufficient for reaching nibbāna with the simile of the boat and the simile of the archer.

title: AN 4.200 Pema Sutta: Love and Hate
description:
> … four things are born of love and hate

And how to not be burned by them.

title: AN 4.205 Aṭṭhaṅgika Sutta: Eightfold
description:
> … a bad person and a worse person, a good person and a better person

Is it better to preach the Dhamma or to practice it?

title: AN 4.21 Paṭhamauruvela Sutta: At Uruvelā
description:
> It is painful to dwell without reverence and deference. Now what ascetic or brahmin can I honor, respect, and dwell in dependence on?

> Let me then honor, respect, and dwell in dependence only on this Dhamma to which I have become fully enlightened.

Who should a Buddha revere?

title: AN 4.22 Dutiya Uruvela Sutta: The Second Discourse at Uruvela
description: > There are, bhikkhus, these four qualities that make one an elder. What four?

title: AN 4.231 Kavi Sutta: Poets
description:
> Cintākavi, sutakavi, atthakavi, paṭibhānakavi

The four kinds of poets.

title: AN 4.233 Vitthāra Sutta: Deeds in Detail
description: Karma that’s dark, bright, both, and neither.

title: AN 4.24 Kāḷakārāma Sutta: At Kāḷaka’s Monastery
description:
> … whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, reached, sought after, examined by the mind—that I know.

The Buddha knows what can be known and thus remains poised in the midst of the world.

title: AN 4.245 Sikkhānisaṁsa Sutta: The Benefits of Training
description:
> Mindfulness is well established in oneself: ‘In this way I’ll experience through freedom the teaching that I haven’t yet experienced, or support with wisdom in every situation the teaching I’ve already experienced.’ That’s how mindfulness is its ruler.

How training benefits, wisdom oversees, free

title: AN 4.25 Brahmacariya Sutta: The Spiritual Life
description:
> Bhikkhus, this spiritual life is not lived for the sake of deceiving people ...

> ... rather, this spiritual life is lived for the sake of restraint ...

A sutta on the proper motivation for "priests" in the Buddha's religion... and for the rest of us too.

title: AN 4.28 Ariyavaṁsa Sutta: The Noble Traditions
description:
> Any bhikkhu who is skillful in this, diligent, clearly comprehending and ever mindful, is said to be standing in an ancient, primal noble lineage.

Contentment with any old robe, alms-food, lodgings, and love of meditation: these are ancient traditions of the noble ones.

title: AN 4.29 Dhammapada Sutta: Dhamma Factors
description:
> Bhikkhus, there are these four Dhamma factors, primal, of long standing, traditional, ancient...

Contentment, good will, mindfulness and convergence are basic principles of the Dhamma.

title: AN 4.3 Paṭhama Khata Sutta: The First Discourse on Being Broken
description:
> They arouse faith in things that are dubious, and they don’t arouse faith in things that are inspiring. When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has these four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged.

After reflection, you should criticize those worthy or criticism, and praise those wo

title: AN 4.33 Sīha Sutta: The Lion
AN 4.36:+ Doṇa Sutta: Doṇa
description:
> Remember me, brahmin, as a Buddha.

The brahmin Doṇa is filled with wonder when he sees the Buddha’s footprints.

title: AN 4.40 Udāyī Sutta: With Udāyī
description: The Buddha criticizes violent sacrifice, but praises giving, especially to ethical mendicants.

title: AN 4.41 Samādhibhāvanā Sutta: Ways of Developing Immersion Further
description:
> There is a way of developing immersion further

Developing convergence for pleasure, understanding, mindfulness, and for ending the defilements.

title: AN 4.45 The Rohitassa Sutta: To Rohatissa
description:
> Yet it is just within this fathom-long body, with its perception & intellect, that I declare that there is the cosmos, the origination of the cosmos, the cessation of the cosmos, and the path of practice leading to the cessation of the cosmos.

The god Rohitassa tells how he tried to go to the end

title: AN 4.5 Anusota Sutta: Along the Stream
description:
> give up sense pleasures even if it’s painful:  
they call this person “one who goes against the stream.”

This sutta defines a person who goes _with_ the stream; a person who goes _against_ the stream; a stable person; and one who has crossed over.

title: AN 4.50 Upakkilesa Sutta: Corruptions
description: Four things obscure the sun and moon, so they don’t shine and glow and radiate. And four things corrupt the holy life: alcohol, sex, money, and wrong livelihood.

title: AN 4.52 Dutiya Puññābhisanda Sutta: The Second Discourse on Overflowing Merit
description:
> ... four streams of merit, streams of the wholesome, nutriments of happiness—heavenly, ripening in happiness, conducive to heaven...

<!---->

title: AN 4.53 Paṭhama Saṁvāsa Sutta: The First Discourse on Living Together
description: Do you live with a god or a zombie?

title: AN 4.55 Paṭhama Samajīvī Sutta: The First Discourse on Equality
description: > Householders, if wife and husband want to see each other in both this life and the next, they should be equals in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom.

title: AN 4.61 Pattakamma Sutta: The Discourse about Suitable Deeds
description:
> The Noble Disciple, householder,
with the wealth he has attained through industry and effort,
accumulated through the strength of his arms, through the sweat of his brow, righteously, in accordance with the Dhamma,
performs four suitable deeds.

Four things that are desirable, but hard to get; and

title: AN 4.62 Ānaṇya Sutta: Freedom from Debt
description:
> Householder, there are these four kinds of happiness that may be achieved by a layperson

The happiness of ownership, using wealth, debtlessness, and blamelessness.

title: AN 4.68 Devadatta Sutta: Devadatta
description:
Possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall.

An "Udāna" from the AN.

title: AN 4.70 Adhammika Sutta: The Discourse on the Dishonest (along with its commentary)
description:
> At whatever time, monastics, there are dishonest kings, [...] the gods become agitated.

When the rulers of society are dishonest, that is a time of climate change

title: AN 4.73 Sappurisa Sutta: A Person of Integrity
description:
> … a person of integrity, when asked, does not reveal another person's bad points, to say nothing of when unasked

On speaking well and ill of others.

title: AN 4.76 Kusināra Sutta: At Kusinārā
description:
> … at the time of his final extinguishment. There the Buddha addressed the mendicants...

As he lay dying, the Buddha encouraged his disciples to ask any last questions.

title: AN 4.77 Acinteyya Sutta: Inconceivable
description:
> … these four things are unthinkable. They should not be thought about

If you try to think about these things you will go mad or get frustrated.

title: AN 4.79 Vaṇijja Sutta: Business
description:
> … what is the reason why for different people the same kind of business undertaking might fail, while another doesn’t meet expectations, another meets expectations, and another exceeds expectations?

<!---->

title: AN 4.83 Avaṇṇāraha Sutta: Where Criticism Takes You
description:
> … don’t arouse faith in things that are dubious

On the importance (!) of judgement.

title: AN 4.87 Putta Sutta: The Son
description:
> And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.
But they don’t have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations.
That’s how a person is a white lotus ascetic.

The confirmed ascetic, the white lotus ascetic, the pink lotus ascetic, and the refine

title: AN 4.93 Dutiya Samādhi Sutta: Immersion (2nd)
description:
> As for the person who has neither serenity nor discernment: in order to get those skillful qualities, they should apply intense enthusiasm...

How each kind of person should practice.

title: AN 4.94 Samadhi Sutta: Concentration (Tranquillity and Insight)
description:
> As for the individual who has attained neither internal tranquillity of awareness nor insight into phenomena through heightened discernment, he should approach an individual who has attained both internal tranquillity of awareness & insight into phenomena

On the importance of attending to the wis

title: AN 4.95 Chavālāta Sutta: A Firebrand
description:
> The person who practices to benefit both themselves and others is the foremost...

See [the next sutta](/content/canon/an4.96) for how...

title: AN 4.96 Rāgavinaya Sutta: Removing Greed
description:
> There is the case where a certain individual doesn’t practice for the subduing of passion within him/herself but encourages others in the subduing of passion

Four kinds of people.  See [the previous sutta](/content/canon/an4.95) for their relative ranking.

title: AN 4.99 Sikkhāpada Sutta: Training Rules
description:
> He himself abstains from lying but doesn’t encourage others in undertaking abstinence from lying.

How to practice selfishly—or not.

title: AN 5.104 Samaṇa Sukhumāla Sutta: An Exquisite Ascetic of Ascetics
description:
> A mendicant with these five qualities is an exquisite ascetic of ascetics.

> And if anyone should be rightly called an exquisite ascetic of ascetics, it’s me.

<!---->

title: AN 5.111 Kulūpaka Sutta: Visiting Families
description:
> Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities who visits families is unlikable and unlovable, not respected or admired.

<!---->

title: AN 5.114 Andhakavinda Sutta: At Andhakavinda
description:
> ... those who have not long gone forth, who are newcomers in this Dhamma & Vinaya should be encouraged, exhorted, and established in these five things.

<!---->

title: AN 5.119 Micchāvācā Sutta: Wrong Speech
description:
> A nun with five qualities is cast down to hell...

Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.

title: AN 5.12 Kūṭa Sutta: Peak
description:
> Among these five trainee’s powers, the power of wisdom is foremost, the one that holds all the others in place...

<!---->

title: AN 5.129 Parikuppa Sutta: Fatal Wounds
description: Five deeds that irredeemably condemn the perpetrator to hell.

title: AN 5.132 Dutiya Cakkā Nuvattana Sutta: The Second Discourse on Wielding Power
description: Five qualities by which a wheel-turning monarch’s son rules justly, and five corresponding qualities by which Sāriputta keeps rolling the Wheel of Dhamma.

title: AN 5.133 Dhammarājā Sutta: The Principled King
description: The Buddha outlines what principled leadership looks like.

title: AN 5.140 Sota Sutta: A Listener
description:
> Endowed with these five qualities, a king’s elephant is worthy of a king...

<!---->

title: AN 5.141 Avajānāti Sutta: Scorn
description: People of the world exhibit these five flaws which make them untrustworthy.

title: AN 5.143 Sārandada Sutta: At Sārandada
description:
> You Licchavis are so fixated on sensual pleasures!

For the conclusion, see [AN 5.195](/content/canon/an5.195).

title: AN 5.147 Asappurisa Dāna Sutta: Gifts of a Bad Person
description:
> These are the five gifts of a bad person.

Good and bad ways of offering gifts.

title: AN 5.156 Tatiya Saddhamma Sammosa Sutta: The Third Discourse on the Decline of the True Teaching
description:
> … these five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching

<!---->

title: AN 5.157 Dukkathā Sutta: Inappropriate Talk
description:
> It’s inappropriate to talk to an unfaithful person about faith...

<!---->

title: AN 5.16 Punakūṭa Sutta: The Second on the Peak
description: Wisdom is the chief of the five powers.

title: AN 5.161 Paṭhamaāghātapaṭivinaya Sutta: Getting Rid of Resentment (1)
description:
> … use these five methods to completely get rid of resentment when it has arisen towards anyone

Five reflections to eliminate enmity in brief.

title: AN 5.162 Dutiyaāghātapaṭivinaya Sutta: Getting Rid of Resentment (2)
description:
> … you should ignore that person’s impure behavior

A series of remarkable similes illustrate the lengths we should go to to remove resent.

title: AN 5.167 Codanā Sutta: Criticizing
description:
> … a monk who wishes to criticize another should first establish five resolutions

<!---->

title: AN 5.168 Sīla Sutta: Ethics
description:
> Reverends, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion.

<!---->

title: AN 5.172 Visārada Sutta: Assured
description:
> A lay follower living at home with these five qualities is self-assured.

Confidence or lack thereof in layfolk is due to their precepts.

title: AN 5.174 Vera Sutta: Threats
description:
> Unless these five dangers and threats are given up, one is said to be unethical, and is reborn in hell.


What is truly dangerous is breaking the precepts.

title: AN 5.176 Pīti Sutta: Rapture
description:
> The pain & distress dependent on sensuality do not exist at that time.

The Buddha encourages Anāthapiṇḍika to not rest short with generosity, but to practice meditation too.

title: AN 5.177 Vaṇijjā Sutta: Trades
description: Five kinds of trade that are wrong livelihood for lay people.

title: AN 5.180 Gavesī Sutta: Gavesī
description: The Buddha smiles and tells the story of a true spiritual leader.

title: AN 5.191 Soṇa Sutta: Dogs
description: The Buddha compares ancient and contemporary Brahminic practices to those of dogs.

title: AN 5.193 Saṅgārava Sutta: With Saṅgārava
description:
> When your heart is not overcome and mired in ill will … even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced.

On how the Five Hindrances cloud our judgement  and fog our memory.

title: AN 5.195 Piṅgiyānī Sutta: Piṅgiyānī
description:
> … those Licchavis clothed Piṅgiyānī with five hundred upper robes. And Piṅgiyānī clothed the Buddha with them.

<!---->

title: AN 5.196 Mahāsupina Sutta: The Great Dreams
description:
> He walked back & forth on top of a giant mountain of excrement ...

Before his awakening, the bodhisatta had five great dreams that foretold profound aspects of his dispensation.

title: AN 5.197 Vassa Sutta: Rain
description:
In which the Buddha claims that karma, the devas, and atmospheric effects can all contribute to the weather.

See also, [SN 36.21](/content/canon/sn36.21).

title: AN 5.200 Nissāraṇīya Sutta: Elements of Escape
description:
> Take a case where a mendicant focuses on sensual pleasures, but their mind isn’t eager, confident, settled, and decided about them.
But when they focus on renunciation, their mind is eager, confident, settled, and decided about it. Their mind is in a good state...

A practical method for escaping

title: AN 5.205 Cetokhila Sutta: Emotional Barrenness
description:
> Mendicants, there are these five kinds of emotional barrenness. What five? …

<!---->

title: AN 5.21 Paṭhama Agārava Sutta: The First Discourse on Irreverence
description: If your basic practice is not there, you can’t go higher.

title: AN 5.211 Akkosaka Sutta: An Abuser
description:
> … a mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions can expect...

Why a monk shouldn’t speak ill of his peers.

title: AN 5.214 Bahubhāṇi Sutta: Someone Who Talks a Lot
description:
> … five drawbacks for a person who talks a lot

And the benefits to being reserved.

title: AN 5.215 Paṭhama Akkhanti Sutta: The First Discourse on Intolerance
description:
> Most people find you unlikable and unlovable. You have lots of enmity and many faults. You feel lost when you die.

The five drawbacks of intolerance.

title: AN 5.228 Ussūra Bhatta Sutta: Eating Late
description:
> Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for a family who takes their meals late in the day.

And five benefits of eating at a reasonable hour.

title: AN 5.23 Upakkilesa Sutta: Corruptions
description:
> But when gold is freed from these five defilements, it is malleable, wieldy, and luminous, pliant and properly fit for work.

The hindrances are like corruptions in gold.

title: AN 5.235 Anukampa Sutta: A Compassionate Mendicant
description:
> Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities shows compassion to the lay people. What five?

<!---->

title: AN 5.245 Dutiya Duccarita Sutta: The Second Discourse on Bad Conduct
description:
> These are the five benefits of good conduct.

<!---->

title: AN 5.249 Sivathika Sutta: A Charnel Ground
description:
> Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks to a charnel ground...

How a person may have the same defects as a cemetery.

title: AN 5.25 Anuggahita Sutta: Supported
description:
> When right view is assisted by five factors, it has liberation of mind as its fruit...

<!---->

title: AN 5.250 Puggala Pasāda Sutta: Faith in Individuals
description:
> Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of placing faith in an individual.

<!---->

title: AN 5.26 Vimuttāyatana Sutta: Opportunities for Freedom
description:
> Mendicants, there are these five opportunities for freedom.

<!---->

title: AN 5.29 Caṅkama Sutta: Walking Meditation
description: The five benefits of walking meditation.

title: AN 5.30 Nāgita Sutta: With Nāgita
description:
> Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity.

The Buddha declares the antidote to greed.

title: AN 5.34 Sīhasenāpati Sutta: With General Sīha
description:
> … can you point out a fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life?

The Buddha teaches General Sīha the benefits of giving.

title: AN 5.38 Saddha Sutta: Faith
description:
> Having flown across the sky,  
the birds resort to this delightful base

title: AN 5.39 Putta Sutta: A Child
description: Reasons why parents want to have children.

title: AN 5.41 The Ādiya Sutta: The Discourse on the Right Use [of Wealth]
description:
> A man remembering this, a person established in Nobility,  
Is praised right here and now, and later rejoices in heaven.

The legitimate purposes of wealth.

title: AN 5.47 Dhana Sutta: Wealth
description:
> … there are these five kinds of wealth. What five?

<!---->

title: AN 5.51 Āvaraṇa Sutta: Obstructions
description:
> The river would keep flowing swiftly for a long way, carrying all before it.

A simile describing how *samādhi* depends on a momentum of practice to clear away the hindrances.

title: AN 5.52 Akusalarāsi Sutta: A Heap of the Unwholesome
description:
> Bhikkhus, saying ‘a heap of the unwholesome,’ it is about the five hindrances that one could rightly say this.

<!---->

title: AN 5.53 Padhāniyaṅga Sutta: Factors [That Support Meditation]
description: Five conditions that help meditation progress smoothly.

title: AN 5.54 Samaya Sutta: Occasions [Good for Meditation]
description: Times that are unconducive to meditation practice, and those that are conducive.

title: AN 5.57 Abhiṇha Paccavekkhitabba Thāna Sutta: Themes for Frequent Recollection
description:
> … beings are intoxicated with life and engage in misconduct by body, speech, and mind. But when one often reflects upon [death], the intoxication with life is diminished.

Topics that are worth regularly reflecting on, whether as a lay person or a renunciant.

title: AN 5.58 Licchavi Kumāraka Sutta: The Licchavi Youths
description:
> Mahānāma, why do you say that they will make it as Vajjis?

How to cimb the social ladder the Buddhist way.

title: AN 5.7 Kāma Sutta: Sensual Pleasures
description:
> However, when the boy has grown up and has enough sense, the nurse would be unconcerned about him.

The Buddha looks after mendicants like a nurse looks after a child until they’ve grown up.

title: AN 5.71 Paṭhama Cetovimutti Phala Sutta: The First Discourse on How Freedom of the Heart is the Fruit
description:
> These five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit.

Five meditations that lead to freedom and the Buddha supplies five similes which subtly illustrate five aspects of awakening.

title: AN 5.75 Paṭhamayodhājīva Sutta: Warriors (1)
description:
> … five people similar to warriors are found among the monks

Some warriors, like some monks, falter before the threat of battle, while others emerge victorious.

title: AN 5.76 Dutiya Yodhājīva Sutta: The Second Discourse about Warriors
description:
> I say that this person is like the warrior who is killed and finished off by his foes. Some people are like that.

Some warriors, like some monks, are killed or injured in battle, while others emerge victorious.

title: AN 5.80 Catuttha Anāgata Bhaya Sutta: The Fourth Discourse on Future Perils
description:
> Monks, these five future dangers, unarisen at present, will arise in the future. Be alert to them and, being alert, work to get rid of them.

The Saṅgha may forsake the simple life and indulge in luxuries.

title: AN 5.98 Āraññaka Sutta: In the Wilderness
description:
> Mendicants, a mendicant practicing mindfulness of breathing who has five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.

<!---->

title: AN 5.99 Sīha Sutta: The Lion
description:
> If he strikes an elephant, he does it carefully...

When the Buddha teaches, he respects his audience.

title: AN 6.1 Paṭhama Āhuneyya Sutta: The First Discourse on Those Worthy of Offerings
description: > It’s a mendicant who, when they see a sight with their eyes, is neither happy nor sad. They remain equanimous, mindful and aware.

title: AN 6.10 Mahānāma Sutta: With Mahānāma
description: > Mahānāma, when a noble disciple has reached the fruit and understood the instructions they frequently practice this kind of meditation.

title: AN 6.107 Rāga Sutta: Greed
description: The meditative perceptions which act as antidotes for greed, hate, and delusion.

title: AN 6.119–139 Tapussa Sutta: About Tapussa, Etc.
description: > Having these six qualities the householder Tapussa is certain

title: AN 6.12 Sārāṇīya Sutta: Conducive to Amiability
description:
> … these six are conditions that are conducive to amiability, that engender feelings of endearment and respect

Six warm-hearted qualities practiced among the mendicants.

title: AN 6.13 Nissāraṇīya Sutta: Elements of Escape
description:
> ‘I’ve developed the heart’s release by love... Yet somehow ill will still occupies my mind.’

The Buddha’s teachings are effective, so we can’t claim to have practiced them fully if we’re still afflicted by various forms of suffering.

title: AN 6.19 Maraṇassati Sutta: Mindfulness of Death
description:
> O, that I might live for the interval that it takes to swallow having chewed up one morsel of food!

Many of those who practice mindfulness of death don’t do so urgently enough.

title: AN 6.20 Maraṇassati Sutta: Mindfulness of Death
description:
> … he should put forth extra desire, effort, diligence, endeavor, relentlessness, mindfulness, & alertness for the abandoning of those very same evil, unskillful qualities. Just as when a person whose turban or hair is on fire

A method for recollecting one’s own death that leads to urgency, dilige

title: AN 6.25 Anussatiṭṭhāna Sutta: Topics for Recollection
description:
> What six? Firstly, a noble disciple recollects...

A way to escape from greed.

title: AN 6.27 Paṭhamasamaya Sutta: Proper Occasions
description:
> There are six proper occasions for going to see an esteemed mendicant...

The right time to visit a monastic is when you need guidance and support.

title: AN 6.30 Anuttariya Sutta: Unsurpassable
description:
> Mendicants, these six things are unsurpassable. What six? The unsurpassable seeing, listening, acquisition, training, service, and recollection.

But none of them compare with the Dhamma.

title: AN 6.37 Chaḷaṅgadāna Sutta: The Six Factors of Giving (along with its Commentary)
tags: pali-canon # also a PDF in this folder
description:
> Here, monastics, for the donor there are three factors, and for the receivers there are three factors.

> … together with its commentary interleaved, [...] it should give the student an idea of how the word commentaries work

title: AN 6.39 Nidāna Sutta: Sources
description:
> Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. What three? Greed, hate, and delusion are sources that give rise to deeds.

<!---->

title: AN 6.41 Dārukkhandha Sutta: A Tree Trunk
description: > Reverends, do you see this large tree trunk?

title: AN 6.42 Nāgita Sutta: With Nāgit
description:
> Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity.

In this Sutta, The Buddha emphasizes the importance of wilderness and seclusion for a meditator.

title: AN 6.43 Nāga Sutta: The Giant
description:
> Gone beyond all things,  
Even the gods revere him

When going for a bath, the Buddha encounters a giant royal elephant. But a spiritual giant is even more impressive.

title: AN 6.45 Iṇa Sutta: Debt
description: Debt in the world, debt in the training, and the highest freedom from debt.

title: AN 6.55 Soṇa Sutta: With Soṇa
description:
> When your harp’s strings were tuned too tight, was it resonant and playable?

When Venerable Soṇa thinks of disrobing, the Buddha comes and encourages him with the famous simile of the lute that is tuned neither too loose nor too tight.

title: AN 6.57 Chaḷabhijāti Sutta: The Six Classes of Rebirth
description: Ānanda asks the Buddha about the six classes of people described by Pūraṇa Kassapa. The Buddha rejects them and proposes an alternate scheme.

title: AN 6.58 Āsava Sutta: Defilements
description:
The Buddha explains that diverse methods should be used for overcoming diverse kinds of problems.
One who is skilled in this is "worthy of offerings."

title: AN 6.60 Hatthisāriputta Sutta: With Hatthisāriputta
description: The junior monk Citta Hatthisāriputta rudely interrupts his seniors, and is admonished by Mahākoṭṭhita. His friends speak up in his defense, but Mahākoṭṭhita warns them how hard it is to know another's heart (_citta_) or where they are headed (_sāreti_).

title: AN 6.61 Majjhe Sutta: In the Middle
description:
A group of monks tries to figure out the meaning of a difficult poem uttered by the Buddha. After offering several interpretations, the Buddha gives his answer.

A very famous example of poetic analysis and hermeneutics in action at the time of the Buddha, this sutta gives several subtle cues on how

title: AN 6.63 Nibbedhika Sutta: A Penetrative Discourse
description:
> The beauties remain as they are in the world,  
while, in this regard,  
the enlightened  
subdue their desire.

The Buddha gives a deep discourse on the development of wisdom.

title: AN 6.78 Sukha Somanassa Sutta: Joy and Happiness
description: Six qualities leading to happiness.

title: AN 6.85 Sītibhāva Sutta: Cooled
description:
> A monk endowed with six qualities is capable of realizing the unexcelled cooled state.

For a comparison of different translations, see [this table](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1zn6HAnAP4V2aqJOKA_K65D3qMM-FKVzi/edit?usp=drivesdk&ouid=100121264257053757190&rtpof=true&sd=true).

title: AN 6.87 Voropita Sutta: A Murderer
description:
> Someone with six qualities is able to enter the sure path

Things that enable or obstruct true understanding while listening to the teachings.

title: AN 6.96 Pātubhāva Sutta: Appearance
description: Six things rare to find in the world.

title: AN 7.1 Paṭhamapiya Sutta: Pleasing
description:
> A mendicant with seven qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions

<!---->

title: AN 7.15 Udakūpamā Sutta: A Simile With Water
description: The Buddha illustrates the seven kinds of practitioners with a simile.

title: AN 7.21 Sārandada Sutta
description:
> … seven principles that prevent decline

<!---->

title: AN 7.36 Mitta Sutta: A Friend
description: > Monks, a friend endowed with seven qualities is worth associating with.

title: AN 7.4 Vitthatabala Sutta: Powers in Detail
description: The powers—faith, energy, conscience, prudence, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom—defined.

title: AN 7.47 Dutiya Aggi Sutta: The Second Discourse on Fires
description: The Buddha talks the brahmin Uggatasarīra out of performing a great sacrifice.

title: AN 7.49 Dutiyasaññā Sutta: Perceptions in Detail
description: > Mendicants, these seven perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They culminate in the deathless and end with the deathless. What seven? The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in

title: AN 7.52 Dāna Mahapphala Sutta: Giving
description: The Buddha explains why even the same gift may result in different benefits for different people, explaining karma and giving a hint at the nature of Buddhist ethics.

title: AN 7.53 Nandamātā Sutta: Nanda’s Mother
description:
> I had an only son called Nanda who I loved dearly. The rulers forcibly abducted him on some pretext and had him executed. But I can’t recall getting upset ...

Sāriputta and Moggallāna are on tour in the southern hills. A deity informs the laywoman Veḷukaṇṭakī that they are approaching. When Sārip

title: AN 7.55 Purisagati Sutta: Places People Are Reborn
description: The Buddha outlines the possible destinies for an anāgāmī.

title: AN 7.57 Sīha Senāpati Sutta: General Sīha's Discourse
description: The Buddha explains the benefits of giving that are visible in the present life, and one that is only apparent in the next.

title: AN 7.58 Arakkheyya Sutta: Nothing to Hide
description:
> Mendicants, there are four areas where the Realized One has nothing to hide, and three ways he is irreproachable.

<!---->

title: AN 7.6 Vitthatadhana Sutta: Wealth
description:
> Mendicants, there are these seven kinds of wealth. What seven? The wealth of faith, ethical conduct, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom.

<!---->

title: AN 7.61 Pacalāyamāna Sutta: Nodding Off
description:
> … near Kallavāḷamutta Village, Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was nodding off while meditating...

Seven methods for overcoming drowsiness in your meditation.

title: AN 7.62 Metta Sutta: Don’t Fear Good Deeds
description:
> I had over a thousand sons who were valiant and heroic, crushing the armies of my enemies.

The Buddha recalls the results of his good deeds.

title: AN 7.64 Kodhana Sutta: An Angry Person
description:
> … an enemy wishes of an enemy, 'O, may this person sleep badly!'

When someone is angry, they wish ill upon their enemy and are disappointed if they do well.

title: AN 7.67 Nagaropama Sutta: The Simile of the Citadel
description: The Buddha compares *samādhi* to a fortress that cannot be overwhelmed.

title: AN 7.71 Bhāvanā Sutta: Development
description:
> When a mendicant is committed to development, they might not wish: ‘If only my mind was freed from the defilements by not grasping!’ Even so, their mind *is* freed...

Liberation doesn’t happen because you wish for it, but because you develop the factors of the path.

title: AN 7.74 Araka Sutta: About Araka
description:
> … life as a human is short, brief, and fleeting, full of suffering and distress. Be thoughtful and wake up! Do what’s good and lead the spiritual life, for no-one born can escape death.

Araka was a famous teacher long ago, when the life span was much greater than today. Nevertheless, he still tau

title: AN 8.11 Verañja Sutta: At Verañjā
description:
> There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that I’m a teacher of annihilationism. For I teach the annihilation of greed, hate, and delusion, and the many kinds of unskillful things.

The brahmin Verañja rebukes the Buddha for his lack of respect for senior brahmins. He levels a seri

title: AN 8.13 Assājānīya Sutta: A Thoroughbred
description:
> Whether or not other bhikkhus train, I will train.

With eight qualities a royal thoroughbred is worthy of a king.

title: AN 8.14 Assakhaḷuṅka Sutta: Wild Colts
description: On the eight ways that people become defensive when admonished: a useful mirror for how we handle criticism. When was the last time you were “like a wild colt?”

title: AN 8.16 Dūteyya Sutta: Going on a Mission
description:
> … possessing eight qualities, a bhikkhu is worthy of going on a mission

<!---->

title: AN 8.19 Pahārāda Sutta: With Pahārāda
description:
> Just as the great ocean has but one taste, the taste of salt, so too, this Dhamma and discipline has but one taste: the taste of liberation.
This is the sixth astounding and amazing quality...

Spirits delight in the ocean for eight reasons, and likewise the mendicants delight in the Dhamma for ei

title: AN 8.2 Paññā Sutta: Wisdom
description:
> Here, a bhikkhu lives in dependence on the Teacher or on a certain fellow monk in the position of a teacher, toward whom he has set up a keen sense of moral shame and moral dread, affection and reverence...

Eight conditions that lead to the arising of wisdom, its growth and perfection.

title: AN 8.20 Uposatha Sutta: Sabbath
description:
On a full-moon night, the Buddha was to recite the code of conduct for the monks. However, he remained silent until dawn, due to the presence of a corrupt monk.
The Buddha follows this up with a memorable set of similes on the wonderful qualities of the Sangha.

title: AN 8.22 Dutiyaugga Sutta: The Second Ugga Sutta
description:
> I do not recall any mental exultation arising because deities come to me

Ugga the Householder roars his lion's roar and the Buddha confirms him as a non-returner.

title: AN 8.24 Dutiya Hatthaka Sutta: The Second Discourse with Hatthaka
description: > I bring together such a large congregation by using the four ways of being inclusive

title: AN 8.25 Mahānāma Sutta: Mahānāma
description:
> In what way, Bhante, is one a lay follower?

Questioned by his relative Mahānāma, the Buddha explains what makes someone a Buddhist lay follower, a virtuous lay follower, and a lay follower practicing for the welfare of all.

title: AN 8.28 Dutiya Bala Sutta: The Second Discourse on the Powers
description: The eight powers of a perfected one.

title: AN 8.29 Akkhaṇa Sutta: Lost Opportunities
description:
> Mendicants, there are eight lost opportunities for spiritual practice.

On how the Human Realm at a time of a Buddha is a unique opportunity for spiritual practice.

title: AN 8.30 Anuruddha Mahā Vitakka Sutta: Anuruddha
description: The Buddha gently encourages Venerable Anuruddha to stop thinking and to delight in cessation.

title: AN 8.31 Paṭhamadāna Sutta: The First Discourse on Giving
description:
> One gives a gift for the purpose of ornamenting the mind

Eight ways of giving a gift.

title: AN 8.33 Dānavatthu Sutta: Reasons to Give
description: Eight reasons why someone might give a gift, from worst to best.

title: AN 8.34 Khetta Sutta: A Field
description:
> A seed sown in a field that possesses these eight factors brings forth abundant fruits, its fruits are delectable, and it yields a profit.

A strange simile for the Eightfold Path, illuminating something of how we might think about each *aṅga*.

title: AN 8.35 Dānūpapatti Sutta: Rebirth by Giving
description:
> The heart’s wish of one who is virtuous succeeds because of his purity.

When giving with a certain wish in mind, you can get it.

This sutta provides a canonical basis for the ubiquitous Buddhist practice of "dedicating the merit" of an offering.

title: AN 8.36 Puñña Kiriya Vatthu Sutta: Grounds for Making Merit
description: Different levels of generosity lead to different rebirths.

title: AN 8.37 Sappurisadāna Sutta: Gifts of a Good Person
description:
> He gives what is pure and excellent...

<!---->

title: AN 8.38 Sappurisa Sutta: The Good Person
description: A good person benefits eight kinds of people, like a rain-cloud showering all over the land.

title: AN 8.39 Abhisanda Sutta: Bonanzas
description:
> Monks, there are these eight bonanzas of merit, rewards of skillfulness, nourishments of happiness...

<!---->

title: AN 8.40 Duccarita Vipāka Sutta: The Results of Misconduct
description:
> … wine at minimum conduces to madness

The karmic results of breaking the five precepts.

title: AN 8.44 Vāseṭṭha Sutta: With Vāseṭṭha [on the Sabbath]
description: The Buddha teaches the layman Vāseṭṭha that when the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones. Vāseṭṭha exclaims that such a practice would be widely beneficial.

title: AN 8.5 Paṭhama Loka Dhamma Sutta: World (1)
description:
> Gain and loss, disrepute and fame,  
blame and praise, pleasure and pain:  
these conditions that people meet  
are impermanent, transient, and subject to change.

The eight worldly conditions in brief.

title: AN 8.51 Gotamī Sutta: With Gotamī
description: Mahāpajāpatī Gotamī, the Buddha’s foster mother, requests ordination from the Buddha. He declines, until urged to relent by Ānanda. He allows Mahāpajāpatī to go forth on eight conditions, in this very difficult sutta.

title: AN 8.52 Ovāda Sutta: An Adviser for Nuns
description: With eight qualities a monk may be appointed to teach the nuns.

title: AN 8.53 Saṁkhitta Sutta: In Brief
description:
> It would be good, lord, if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma in brief

This discourse is one of the few teachings in the canon (along with the teachings on mindfulness) which the Buddha declared as "categorical": always applicable and useful in any situation. This sutta gives, better than

title: AN 8.54 Dīghajāṇu Sutta: With Dīghajāṇu
description:
> This is called accomplishment in balanced finances.

Dīghajāṇu of the Koliyans asks the Buddha to teach in a way suitable for lay people who enjoy life. The Buddha teaches four practical ways to ensure success in this life, and another four ways to ensure success in the next.

title: AN 8.55 Ujjaya Sutta: With Ujjaya
description:
> Accomplishment in initiative, protection, good friendship, and balanced finances.

The brahmin Ujjaya is going abroad, and asks the Buddha to teach him. The Buddha teaches four practical ways to ensure success in this life, and another four ways to ensure success in the next.

title: AN 8.56 Bhaya Sutta: Danger
description:
> Mendicants, ‘danger’ is a term for sensual pleasures...

The Buddha explains how addiction to sensual pleasures is perilous.

title: AN 8.6 Paṭhama Loka Dhamma Sutta: World (2)
description:
> An instructed noble disciple also meets gain and loss, disrepute and fame, blame and praise, and pleasure and pain.

The eight worldly conditions in detail.

title: AN 8.63 Saṁkhitta Sutta: A Teaching in Brief
description: The Buddha instructs a monk to sequentially develop the four Brahmaviharas and the four satipaṭṭhānā.

title: AN 8.64 Gayāsīsa Sutta: At Gāyā Head
description: The Buddha describes his progressive knowledge of the devas.

title: AN 8.69 Parisā Sutta: Assemblies
description: Eight kinds of assemblies: aristocrats, brahmins, householders, ascetics, and various deities. The Buddha taught each.

title: AN 8.7 Devadatta Vipatti Sutta: Devadatta’s Failure
description:
> Bhikkhus, it is good for a bhikkhu from time to time to review his own failings. It is good for him from time to time to review the failings of others. It is good for him from time to time to review his own achievements. It is good for him from time to time to review the achievements of others.

D

title: AN 8.70 Bhūmicāla Sutta: Earthquakes
description:
> At a time when a great wind blows, it stirs the water, and the water stirs the earth.

At the end of his life, the Buddha explains to Ānanda the eight causes of earthquakes.

title: AN 8.71 Paṭhama Saddhā Sutta: The First Discourse on Inspiring All Around
description: The qualities of a good monk.

title: AN 8.73 Paṭhama Maraṇassati Sutta: The First Discourse on Mindfulness of Death
description:
> Oh if I’d only live as long as it takes to breathe out after breathing in, or to breathe in after breathing out, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.

Various mendicants practice mindfulness of death, but do so inadequately. The Buddha explains how to do so with

title: AN 8.74 Dutiya Maraṇassati Sutta: The Second Discourse on Mindfulness of Death
description:
> Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply intense enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply intense enthusiasm …

A

title: AN 8.79 Parihāna Sutta: Decline
description: Eight things for the decline or success of a mendicant in the training.

title: AN 8.80 Kusītārambhavatthu Sutta: Grounds for Laziness
description:
> Mendicants, there are eight grounds for laziness.

And the corresponding eight grounds for energy.

title: AN 8.82 Puṇṇiya Sutta: With Puṇṇiya
description:
> When a mendicant has faith, approaches, pays homage, asks questions, actively listens to the teachings, remembers the teachings, reflects on the meaning, and practices accordingly, the Realized One feels inspired to teach [them].

How we should approach the Dhamma and Dhamma teachers.

title: AN 8.83 Mūlaka Sutta: Rooted
description:
> Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: Reverends, all things have what as their root? ...

<!---->

title: AN 8.87 Pattanikujjana Sutta: Turning the Bowl Upside Down
description:
> … the Saṅgha may, if it wishes, turn the bowl upside down for a lay follower on eight grounds.

The Buddha allowed the monks to protest in a peculiar way which has actually been used, for example [by the Burmese](/content/articles/burmese-alms-boycott_kovan-martin).

title: AN 9.1 Sambodhi Sutta: Awakening
description:
> Reverends, what is the vital condition for the development of the awakening factors?

> A mendicant grounded on these five things should develop four further things.

How spiritual friendship forms the foundation of the holy life.

title: AN 9.11 Sīhanāda Sutta: Sāriputta’s Lion’s Roar
description:
> Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry.

When another monk falsely accuses Sāriputta of hitting him, the Buddha calls Sāriputta to respond to the allegation.

title: AN 9.12 Saupādisesa Sutta: With Something Left Over
description:
> There are these nine people who, dying with something left over, are exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm.

Sāriputta visits some wanderers, who claim that only perfected ones are free from bad rebirth. Sāriputta has no opinion on this, but asks the Buddha, who replies that even

title: AN 9.17 Kula Sutta: Families
description:
> Visiting a family with nine factors is worthwhile

Nine factors in how a family should relate to the Saṅgha.

title: AN 9.19 Devatā Sutta: A Deity
description:
> … having fulfilled our duty, free of remorse and regret, we were reborn in a superior realm

Some deities come to the Buddha and speak of their practice in their past life and, in so doing, explain the conduct expected of lay people towards monastics.

title: AN 9.20 Velāma Sutta: About Velāma
description:
> Once upon a time, householder, there was a brahmin named Velāma...

> [...but] it would be more fruitful to develop the perception of impermanence—even for as long as a finger-snap—than to do all of these things

The wealthy and devoted lay supporter Anāthapiṇḍika rather curiously says that only p

title: AN 9.23 The Taṇhā Mūlaka Sutta: Things Rooted in Craving
description:
> Craving is a cause of seeking. Seeking is a cause of gaining...

Nine things that are rooted in craving.

title: AN 9.25 Paññā Sutta: Wisdom
description:
> Mendicants, when a mendicant’s mind has been well consolidated with wisdom it’s appropriate for them to say: ‘I understand...’

Nine reflections by which a mendicant knows their mind has wisdom.

title: AN 9.3 Meghiya Sutta: With Meghiya
description:
> But then, a mendicant grounded on these five things should develop four further things. They should develop the perception of ugliness to give up greed, love to give up hate, mindfulness of breathing to cut off thinking, and perception of impermanence to uproot the conceit ‘I am’.

Venerable Meghi

title: AN 9.30 Āghāta Paṭivinaya Sutta: Getting Rid of Resentment
description: Nine kinds of resentment and how to handle them.

title: AN 9.34 Nibbāna Sukha Sutta: Extinguishment is Bliss
description: How can Nibbāna be "blissful" if it's the cessation of feeling?

title: AN 9.36 Jhāna Sutta: Depending on Absorption
description:
> They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as falling apart, as empty, as not-self.
They turn their mind away from those things, and a

title: AN 9.37 Ānanda Sutta: By Ānanda
description:
> Percipient in this way, too, one is not sensitive to that dimension.

Ānanda exclaims how amazing it is that the Buddha has found a way to freedom while still experiencing the world.

Questioned by the monk Udāyī, Ānanda elucidates that he's referring to the formless attainments and then goes on t

title: AN 9.41 Tapussa Sutta: With the Householder Tapussa
description:
> Just as pain arises as an affliction for a healthy person, even so the attention to perceptions dealing with directed thought that beset me was an affliction for me.

The householder Tapussa reflects that it is renunciation that distinguishes lay from monastic. The Buddha agrees by giving a long a

title: AN 9.42 Pañcala Sutta: Cramped
description:
> … what is confinement, and what is the opening amid confinement that the Buddha spoke of?

At Udāyī’s request, Ānanda explains an obscure verse spoken (in [SN 2.7](/content/canon/sn2.7)) by a deity. The nine progressive meditations are the gradual escape from confinement.

title: AN 9.44 Paññā Vimutta Sutta: Freed by Wisdom
description:
> … having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. And they understand that with wisdom. To this extent the Buddha spoke of the one freed by wisdom


<!---->

title: AN 9.63 Sikkhādubbalya Sutta: Weaknesses in Training and Mindfulness Meditation
description:
> To give up these five weaknesses in your training you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

Meditation relies on ethics, but ethics is also supported by meditation.

title: Analysis of the Bhikkhu-Pātimokkha: A translation of the *Mahā-Vibhaṅga* from the *Vinaya-Piṭaka*
description: The canonical explication of the monastic rules.

title: The _Bhikkhuni Pātimokkha_
description: The monastic rules for Theravāda Bhikkhunis, prepared in a bilingual English-Pali edition for study and recitation.

title: _Bodhicaryāvatāra_ Chapter 1: The Benefits of Bodhicitta
description:
> In accordance with the scriptures, I shall now in brief describe  
The way to adopt the discipline of all the buddhas’ heirs.

A verse translation of chapter 1 from the
