[_Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra_](/content/canon/bodhisattvacaryavatara_santideva)
on "bodhicitta."

title: Bodhicaryāvatāra Chapter 2: Confession of Negativity
description:
> Just as I must eventually forsake this life,  
So too must I take leave of relatives and friends.  
When I must go alone on death’s uncertain journey,  
What concern to me are all these enemies and allies?

A verse translation of chapter 2 from the [_Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra_](/content/canon/bodhisa

title: _Bodhicaryāvatāra_ Chapter 3: Fully Adopting Bodhicitta
description:
> For all the beings ailing in the world,  
Until their sickness has been healed,  
May I become the doctor and the cure


A free translation of chapter 3 from the
[_Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra_](/content/canon/bodhisattvacaryavatara_santideva)
on joyfully taking hold of "bodhicitta."

description:
This epic poem on grasping firmly the intention to awaken has inspired many generations of Buddhists to live a more ethical and spiritual life and it captures beautifully the aesthetic of Buddhist ethics. Well worth reading again and again and again.

description:

description:
The first English translation of the Vinaya Pitaka, [Bhikkhu Sujato](/authors/sujato) and [Bhikkhu Brahmali](/authors/brahmali) prepared this ebook version of the PTS volumes.

A late Pāli text that has been extremely influential on the (especially monastic) meditation practices of the Theravāda world, visible especially in Sri Lanka and Thailand today.

description:

I rejoice in all goodness.  
I request and pray to all the buddhas to teach and remain in saṃsāra.  
May I attain sublime, supreme, unsurpassed wisdom!

title: The \"Sangye tenpa...\" Dedication
description: A simple, four line dedication prayer from the Nyingma Tradition.

description: A beautiful reading of some of the most famous verses in Buddhism.

description: A straightforward, annotated translation of the first chapter of the Dhammapada.

title: Dhp 365–369 Bhikhu Vagga: from The Monk Chapter
description: The Buddha illustrates letting go with the simile of a boat in need of bailing out.

title: Dhp 25 Bhikkhu Vagga: The Monk
description: This inspiring set of verses, the penultimate of the Dhammapada, outlines the contours of the holy life and encourages us to dedicate ourselves diligently to the path.

title: Dhp 21–32 Appamāda Vagga: Vigilance Chapter
description: A straightforward, annotated translation of the second chapter of the Dhammapada.

title: Dhp 33–43 Citta Vagga: Mind Chapter
description: A straightforward, annotated translation of the third chapter of the Dhammapada.

title: DN 11 Kevatta Sutta: With Kevaddha
description: The Buddha refuses to perform miracles for a layman, explaining that this is not the right way to inspire faith. He goes on to tell the story of a monk's misguided quest for spiritual answers, an answer the Buddha ultimately gives in one of the most profound poems of the Canon.

title: DN 13 Tevijja Sutta: Experts in the Three Vedas
description:
> … it is impossible that they should teach the path to that which they neither know nor see

The simile of the blind leading the blind followed by lovely similes for the chords of sensual pleasure and the hindrances, as well as for their overcoming via the limitless, divine abidings.

title: DN 14 Mahāpadāna Sutta: The Great Discourse on the Harvest of Deeds
course: buddha # only partially covered by Nyanamoli's *Life*
description:
> Ninety-one eons ago, the Buddha Vipassī arose in the world, perfected and fully awakened...

The story of the Buddha Vipassī which later came to be grafted onto Buddha Gotama's biography.

title: The Great Discourse on Causation: The *Mahānidāna Sutta* and its Commentaries
description: A translation of [DN 15](https://suttacentral.net/dn15/en/bodhi){:target="_blank" ga-event-value="{{ page.base_value }}"} together with its traditional (Theravāda) commentary and subcommentary, featuring an introduction and appendix by the translator introducing the Abhidhamma system by which the co

title: DN 16 The Mahāparinibbāna Sutta: The Great Discourse on the Buddha’s Extinguishment
description: The canonical account of the final days of the Buddha's life.

title: DN 2 Sāmaññaphala Sutta: The Fruits of Recluseship
description:
> Is it possible, venerable sir, to point out any fruit of recluseship that is visible here and now?

One of the greatest literary and spiritual texts of early Buddhism, this sutta gives a thorough account of the path and benefits of renunciation.

title: DN 21 Sakka Pañha Sutta: Sakka’s Questions
description:
> Thought is the source of desire.

A fairy sings a love song for the Buddha, and Sakka asks a few deep questions.

title: DN 22 The Mahāsatipaṭṭhāna Sutta: The Long Discourse about the Ways of Attending to Mindfulness
description: Perhaps the most important guide to meditation in the entire Pāli Canon.

title: DN 23 Pāyāsi Sutta: With Pāyāsi
description:
A long and entertaining debate with a skeptic who went to extravagant lengths to prove that there is no such thing as an afterlife.

Interesting to note: one of the methods mentioned was tried recently, with [results](https://web.archive.org/web/20200321170445if_/https://www.scientificexploration.or

title: DN 26 Cakkavatti Sutta: The Wheel-Turning Monarch
description:
> When those seven days have passed, having emerged from their hiding places and embraced each other, they will come together and cry in one voice, ‘Fantastic, dear foe, you live!’

In illustration of his dictum that one should rely on oneself, the Buddha gives a detailed account of the fall of a ki

title: DN 27 Aggañña Sutta: The Origin of the World
description: > But the single mass of water at that time was utterly dark. The moon and sun were not found, nor were stars and constellations, day and night, months and fortnights, years and seasons, or male and female. Beings were simply known as ‘beings’. After a very long period had passed, the earth’s substa

title: DN 28 Sampasādanīya Sutta: Inspiring Confidence
description:
> … there’s no other ascetic or brahmin---whether past, future, or present---whose direct knowledge is superior to the Buddha 

The Venerable Sāriputta extols the Buddha's many remarkable qualities.

For a comparison of this sutta to its parallels, see 
[DiSimone 2016](/content/articles/da16-compari

title: DN 30 Lakkhaṇa Sutta: The Marks of a Great Man
description:
The Buddha lists the 32 Marks which Brahmanical prophecy claim marked him for greatness and explains the specific causes and results that each signify, in this late addition to the Canon.

See K.R. Norman's series of articles on [The Metres of the Lakkhaṇa Sutta](/content/booklets/metres-of-the-lakk

title: DN 31 Siṅgāla Sutta: Advice to Sigālaka
description: A magisterial compendium of good advice for lay people.

title: DN 33 Saṅgīti Sutta: Reciting in Concert
description: A compendium of numerical dhammas, this sutta was perhaps a forerunner of the AN.

title: DN 5 Kūṭadanta Sutta: With Kūṭadanta
description:
> Let the king provide funding for those who work in trade.
Let the king guarantee food and wages for those in government service.
Then the people, occupied with their own work, will not harass the realm.
The king’s revenues will be great.

A brahmin wishes to undertake a great sacrifice and asks fo

title: DN 8 Mahāsīhanāda Sutta: The Longer Discourse on the Lion’s Roar
tags: renunciation # or move here when launched
description:
> I see some fervent mortifiers who takes it easy reborn in a place of loss. But I see another fervent mortifier who takes it easy reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.

The Buddha tells a naked ascetic the true meaning of austerity.

title: DN 9 Poṭṭhapāda Sutta: With Poṭṭhapāda
description:
> Potthapada—having other views, other practices, other satisfactions, other aims, other teachers—it’s hard for you to know whether perception is a person’s self or if perception is one thing and self another.

The Buddha discusses with a wanderer the nature of perception and how it evolves through

title: Dhammasangiṇi: A Buddhist Manual of Psychological Ethics
description:
The first book of the Abhidhammapiṭaka.

The book itself starts with a "matika," a list of classifications of dhammas, which are ideas or phenomena. It includes 22 three-fold and 100 two-fold classifications by the Abhidhamma method and 42 by the Sutta method. The main content is divided into four p

title: EA 12.1: The One Way In
description: The (somewhat simpler) Mahasanghika parallel to [the Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta](/content/canon/mn10).

title: Iti 1 Lobha Sutta: Greed
description:
> Greed is that one thing, bhikkhus.
Abandon that and I guarantee you non-return.

Abandoning greed is most, though not all, of the path.

title: Iti 100 Brāhmaṇa Dhamma Yāga Sutta: The Holy Offering of the Teaching
description:
> You are my children, my sons, born from my mouth, born of the Dhamma...

The Buddha compares himself and his disciples to the Brahmins, and encourages his community to be as open-handed with the Dhamma as he was.

title: Iti 105 Taṇhuppāda Sutta: The Arising of Craving
description:
> With craving his companion, a man  
wanders on a long, long time.

What can cause a monk to be reborn?

title: Iti 107 Bahukāra Sutta: Very Helpful
description:
> Householders and homeless alike,  
Each a support for the other

The reciprocal ways in which monks and lay supporters benefit each other.

title: Iti 109 Nadīsota Sutta: A River
description:
> Suppose, bhikkhus, a man was being borne along by the current of a river...

An extended metaphor for the dangers of “going with the flow.”

title: Iti 111 Sampanna Sīla Sutta: Perfect in Virtue
description:
> A bhikkhu who in such a manner is ardent and afraid of wrongdoing is called constantly energetic and resolute.

A short sutta stressing the foundation of ethics on which insight meditation must rest.

title: Iti 17 Dutiyasekha Sutta: A Trainee (2)
description:
> I do not perceive another single factor so helpful as good friendship...

Friendship with admirable people is the prime external factor to help those in training.

title: Iti 19 Saṁgha Sāmaggī Sutta: Harmony in the Saṅgha
translator: geoff # this translation strongly prefered here
description:
> Blissful is concord in the Saṅgha.

Concord in the Sangha leads to the welfare and happiness of many beings, both human and divine.

title: Iti 20 Paduṭṭhacitta Sutta: A Corrupted Mind
description:
> Beings go to a bad bourn  
Because of mind’s corruption.

<!---->

title: Iti 21 Pasanna Citta Sutta: A Confident Mind
description:
> ... if this person were to die at this time, as if carried there he would be placed in heaven.

<!---->

title: Iti 22 Metta Sutta: The Benefits of Love
description:
> Mendicants, don’t fear good deeds. For ‘good deeds’ is a term for happiness...

The Buddha recalls the results he himself has experienced from doing meritorious deeds.

title: Iti 24 Aṭṭhipuñja Sutta: A Heap of Bones
description:
> The accumulation  
of a single person’s  
bones for an eon  
would be a heap  
on a par with a mountain

<!---->

title: Iti 25 Musāvāda Sutta: On Lying
description:
> … there is no bad deed they would not do

For the full context behind this short saying, see [MN 61](/content/canon/mn61).

title: Iti 26 Dāna Sutta: Generosity
description:
> … if sentient beings only knew, as I do, the fruit of giving and sharing, they would not eat without first giving

<!---->

title: Iti 27: Mettā Bhāvanā
description:
> … of all the grounds for making worldly merit, none are worth a sixteenth part of the heart’s release by love.

Goodwill far outshines all other ways of making merit.

title: Iti 34 Ātāpī Sutta: Ardour
description:
> … a bhikkhu who is without ardour and without fear of wrongdoing is incapable of attaining enlightenment

<!---->

title: Iti 38 Vitakka Sutta: Thoughts (Often Occuring)
description:
> Bhikkhus, two thoughts often occur to the Tathāgata...

<!---->

title: Iti 41 Paññā Parihīna Sutta: Bereft of Wisdom
description:
> Bhikkhus, those beings are thoroughly deprived who are deprived of noble wisdom.

<!---->

title: Iti 43 Ajāta Sutta: Unborn
description: The existence of the unfabricated element affords us an escape from conditional reality.

title: Iti 44 Nibbāna Dhātu Sutta: The Elements of Quenching
description:
> The Nibbāna element with residue remaining (Sōpādisesa Nibbāna) and the Nibbāna element with no residue remaining (Anupādisesa Nibbāna).

<!---->

title: Iti 49 Diṭṭhigata Sutta: Held by Views
description:
> Bhikkhus, held by two kinds of views, some devas and
human beings hold back and some overreach; only those with vision see.

How craving for being or annihilation blind us to dependent origination.

title: Iti 53 Dutiyavedanā Sutta: Feelings
description:
> … by thoroughly understanding conceit, he has made an end of suffering.

How the three types of feeling should be viewed.

title: Iti 54 Paṭhamaesanā Sutta: The First on Searches
description:
> searches  
And the origin of searches,  
Where they cease and the path...

<!---->

title: Iti 55 Dutiyaesanā Sutta: The Second on Searches
description:
> Sensual search, the search for being,  
The search for a holy life ...

<!---->

title: Iti 56 Paṭhama Āsava Sutta: The First Saying on the Defilements
description:
> Monks, there are these three effluents. Which three?

A short poem on the ending of the out-flows.

title: Iti 58 Taṇhā Sutta: Craving
description:
> But those who have abandoned craving...

The three cravings and what it's like to be beyond their grasp.

title: Iti 60 Puñña Kiriya Vatthu Sutta: The Discourse on the Grounds for Making Merit
description:
> Giving and moral conduct,  
developing a mind of love...

The three grounds for meritorious activity.

title: Iti 61 Cakkhu Sutta: The Eyes
description:
> The fleshly eye, the divine eye, and the wisdom eye. These, bhikkhus, are the three eyes.

<!---->

title: Iti 63 Addhā Sutta: Times
description:
> But by fully understanding what is expressed  
One does not misconceive the speaker.

Four translations of this sutta from
[John Ireland](https://suttacentral.net/iti63/en/ireland),
[Ajahn Geoff](https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/KN/Iti/iti63.html),
[SuttaFriends](https://suttafriends.org/sutta/i

title: Iti 73 Santatara Sutta: More Peaceful
description:
> Bhikkhus, the formless is more peaceful than the form realm, and cessation is more peaceful than the formless.

<!---->

title: Iti 74 Putta Sutta: A Child
description: > One who betters their birth, one who equals their birth, and one who fails their birth.

title: Iti 75 Avuṭṭhika Sutta: A Rainless Cloud
description:
> What kind of person, bhikkhus, is like a rainless cloud?

Three types of people: one like a cloud without rain, one who rains locally, and one who rains everywhere.

title: Iti 76 Sukha Patthanā Sutta: Aspiring for Happiness
description:
> Aspiring to these three forms of bliss, a wise person should guard his virtue.

<!---->

title: Iti 83 Pañca Pubba Nimitta Sutta: The Five Prognostic Signs
description:
> When a deva is due to pass away from a company of devas, five prognostic signs appear...

How does a god die?

title: Iti 85 Asubhānupassī Sutta: Observing Ugliness
description:
> When mindfulness of breathing is well-established internally in front of you, there will be no distressing external thoughts or wishes. When you meditate observing the impermanence of all conditions, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises.

A pithy summary of the path.

title: Iti 87 Andhakaraṇa Sutta: Blindness
description:
> Bhikkhus, these three kinds of unwholesome thoughts produce blindness, lack of vision, and absence of knowledge; they obstruct wisdom, lead to vexation, and are not conducive to Nibbāna.

Along with three kinds of wholesome thoughts.

title: Iti 89 Devadatta Sutta: About Devadatta
description:
> Devadatta,  
–regarded as wise, composed,  
incandescent with honor–  
in the thrall of heedlessness  
assaulted the Tathāgata...

Conquered by three forms of false Dhamma, Devadatta was incurably doomed to hell.

title: Iti 92 Saṅghāṭikaṇṇa Sutta: The Corner of the Cloak
description:
> That bhikkhu sees the Dhamma. Seeing the Dhamma, he sees [the Tathāgata].

To see the Dhamma is to see the Buddha and to be close to him, even when physically far away.

title: Iti 93 Aggi Sutta: Fires
Iti 94 Upaparikkha Sutta: Examination
description:
> ... by not grasping anything he should remain undisturbed.

The Buddha's pithy instructions to *samma-samādhi*.

title: Iti 96 Kāmayoga Sutta: Attached to Sensual Pleasures
description:
> Tied by the yoke of sensuality & the yoke of becoming, monks, one is a returner, returning to this state...

<!---->

title: Iti 97 Kalyāṇa Sīla Sutta: Good Morals
description: Admirable virtue, admirable qualities, and admirable discernment defined.

title: Iti 98 Dāna Sutta: Giving
description:
> … there are these two kinds of gifts: a gift of material things & a gift of the Dhamma

And so too with sharing and assistance.

title: Ja 1 Apaṇṇaka Jātaka: The Story about what is Unquestionable
description: An English translation by Bhikkhu Anandajoti of the first Jātaka story, along with a commentary on the text, which has not been translated until now. This Jataka is of the Buddha as a wise caravan merchant who avoided evil yakkhas and returned home safely with all of his retinue.

title: Ja 2 Vaṇṇupatha Jātaka: The Story about a Sandy Place
description: An English translation by Bhikkhu Anandajoti of the second Jātaka story, along with a commentary on the text, which has not been translated until now. This Jātaka offers a lesson in perserverance and effort on the path of awakening.

title: Ja 273 Kacchapa Jātaka: The Tortoise (A Naughty Jātaka)
description: The Kacchapa Jataka is a humorous and risqué story, and one that has likely never been translated into English before. While it pokes fun at Rhesus Macaques and greedy Brahmanas, it also highlights the Bodhisattva's equanimity.

title: Ja 3 Seriva Vāṇija Jātaka: The Story about the Tradesman from Seriva
description: An English translation by Bhikkhu Anandajoti of the third Jātaka story, along with a commentary on the text, which has not been translated until now. This Jātaka offers a lesson in faith and honesty in leading toward a good rebirth and progress along the path of awakening.

title: Jātaka Gāthā Vaṇṇanā: Word Commentaries on the Jātaka Verses
description:
> Although the early dictionaries, like [Pali Text Society](/publishers/pts)’s *[Pali-English Dictionary](/content/reference/ped)*
and [*A Critical Pāli Dictionary*](/content/reference/critical-pali-dictionary_pts), did utilize the Jātaka word definitions
considerably, up and till now no one has tra

title: (Mahā) Karma-Vibhaṅga: The Analysis of Deeds
description:
A composite text bringing together many Buddhist stories about karma and its ripening into a comprehensive index of pedagogical snippets.

> In the original teachings deeds and their results are presented quite subtly, as everyone, of course, produces many millions of intentional deeds, both good an

title: Kathā-Vatthu (Points of Controversy) from the Abhidhamma-Pitaka
description: A book in the Abhidhamma Canon  explicitly dealing with the doctrinal controversies that arose between the Indian schools of Buddhism and the   Theravāda.

title: Khuddakapāṭha: The Short Readings
description:
The first book of the Khuddaka Nikāya, the Khuddakapāṭha was, in ancient times, a daily liturgy for novice monks.

Its selection of chants is still influential in Theravāda liturgies today.

title: Khp 5 Maṅgala Sutta: The Highest Blessings
description: A recipe for the good life, from having good friends to the realization of Nibbāna, this chant is a favorite of Theravāda Buddhists the world over, myself included.

title: Khp 6 Ratana Sutta: Jewels Discourse
description:
> May all these beings have happy minds!
Listen closely to my words...

A popular Theravāda chant and a peak into how the Sangha ritually continues the Buddha's role as "teacher of the *devas*."

title: Kp 9 Mettā Sutta: The Teaching on Love
description:
> Let him be able, and upright and straight,  
Easy to speak to, gentle, and not proud...

"What should be done" by us Buddhists.

title: Lal 26 Dharmacakrapravartana Sūtra: The Discourse that Set the Dharma-Wheel Rolling
description: A Sanskrit version of the Buddha's first sermon preserved in the Mahayana Canon.

title: Dharmāloka-Mukhaṁ: The Entrance into the Light of the Dharma
description: This is a translation of part of chapter 4 of the Lalitavistara Sūtra, in which the Bodhisattva gives a dharma teaching to the gods in Tushita just before his imminent rebirth presenting a list of the mental states and factors to be developed for awakening.

title: MA 128 Upasaka Sutra: Discourse on the White-Clad Disciple
description: The Buddha encouages lay disciples to practice the five precepts and frequently recollect their purity.

title: MA 220 Arittha Sutra: The Discourse on Knowing the Better Way to Catch a Snake
description: A Bhikkhu gives rise to a pernicious view, which the Buddha criticizes before giving a deep analysis of the aggregates.

title: MA 25 水喻: Discourse on the Five Ways of Putting an End to Anger
description: Venerable Shariputra explains five ways to quell anger through wise attention, giving five memorable similes on being determined to find the good in everyone.

title: MA 80: The Rough Cloth
description: The Venerable Aniruddha tells the monks about his practice of austerity.

title: MA 81 念身: Mindfulness of the Body
description:
> If he thus goes into seclusion and lives alone, his thoughts aren’t careless.
He cultivates diligence, stops mental disturbances, and attains a concentrated state...

For an alternative translation, see [Kuan, 2007](/content/articles/annotated-translation-of-the-chinese-version-of-the-kayagatasati

title: The Essence of Mahayana Practice (略辨大乘入道四行觀)
description:
> To enter the Great Way there are many paths, but essentially they are of two means: by Principle and by Practice.

A modern translation of and commentary on a famous, pithy discourse by the founder of the Chan school.

title: The Questions of King Malinda: An Abridgement of the _Milindapañhā_
description:
An abridged translation of the much-beloved, ancient Pāli classic of Theravāda doctrine.

> … in Burma the book has actually been included in the Sutta Piṭaka itself, as part of the _Khuddaka Nikāya_ or Miscellaneous Collection. Although the Buddhists of the other Theravāda countries have not gone q

title: MN 1 Mūlapariyāya Sutta: The Root of All Things
description:
A challenging discourse (even for those who first heard it!), this first sutta of the Majjhima Nikāya is a forceful rejection of all forms of monism, and the Samkhya philosophy in particular.

For a translation of this sutta's semicanonical commentaries, see [Bhikkhu Bodhi's *The Root of Existence*]

title: MN 10 The Mahāsatipaṭṭhāna Sutta: Mindfulness Meditation
description: Here the Buddha details the seventh factor of the noble eightfold path—right mindfulness. This collects many of the meditation teachings found throughout the canon, especially the practices focusing on the body, and is regarded as one of the most important discourses in the contemporary Theravāda tr

title: MN 100 Saṅgārava Sutta: With Saṅgārava
description: The Buddha gives an account of his struggles for—and achievement of—awakening in answer to a question about how he knows and teaches what he does.

title: MN 102 Pañcattaya Sutta: Five and Three
description: In this challenging sutta, the Buddha describes how meditators might go astray, thinking they've attained Right View when in fact they haven't.

title: MN 103 Kinti Sutta: What Do You Think About Me?
description:
> I cannot make that person emerge from the unwholesome and establish him in the wholesome.’ one should not underrate equanimity towards such a person.

The Buddha outlines when and how monks should reprove one another.

title: MN 104 Sāmagāma Sutta: At Sāmagāma
description:
> A dispute about livelihood or about the Pātimokkha would be trifling, Ānanda. But should a dispute arise in the Sangha about the path or the way, such a dispute would be for the harm and unhappiness of many

Hearing of the death of the Jain leader, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, and their subsequent disputes

title: MN 105.19: The Simile of the Field Surgeon
description: > Suppose a man were struck by an arrow thickly smeared with poison.

title: MN 105 Sunakkhatta Sutta: With Sunakkhatta
description:
> For it is death in the training of the noble one to reject the training and return to a lesser life.

The Buddha explains why some people progress on the path of meditation and why others fall short of the ultimate goal.

title: MN 106 Aneñjasappaya Sutta: Conducive to the Imperturbable
description:
> Mendicants, sensual pleasures are impermanent, hollow, false, and deceptive, made by illusion, cooed over by fools.

The Buddha outlines a number of reflections conducive to attaining imperturbable states, and ends by warning Ānanda to not become attached to even that equanimity.

title: MN 107 Ganakamoggallana Sutta: The Discourse to Ganaka-Moggallana
description: The Buddha explains how he trains his disciples and why some succeed while others fail.

title: MN 108 Gopakamoggallāna Sutta: With Moggallāna the Guardian
description:
> There is no single bhikkhu, brahmin, who possesses in each and every way all those qualities that were possessed by the Blessed One, accomplished and fully enlightened. For the Blessed One was the arouser of the unarisen path

Some time after the Buddha's Parinibbāna, Ven. Ānanda and some brahmins

title: MN 109 Mahā Puṇṇama Sutta: The Greater Discourse on the Full-moon Night
description:
> He doesn’t assume consciousness to be the self, or the self as possessing consciousness, or consciousness as in the self, or the self as in consciousness.

The Buddha gives a long discourse on the five aggregates ending in his own repudiation of the idea that not-self contradicts the law of karma.

title: MN 11: Cūḷasīhanāda Sutta: The Lesser Discourse on the Lion’s Roar
description:
> … it is only here that there is the contemplative

The Buddha tells the difference between his religion and others' and gives a clear discourse on the meaning of enlightenment.

title: MN 112 Chabbisodhana Sutta: The Sixfold Purification
description:
> Take a mendicant who declares enlightenment: ‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is no return to any state of existence.”’
You should neither approve nor dismiss that mendicant’s statement. Rather, you should question

title: MN 113 Sappurisa Sutta: A True Person
description: The Buddha reminds us to not become proud or derogatory on account of what we have---no matter how great that attainment might be.

title: MN 114 Sevitabbāsevitabba Sutta: What Should and Should Not Be Cultivated
description:
> You should not cultivate the kind of person who causes unskillful qualities to grow while skillful qualities decline. And you should cultivate the kind of person who causes unskillful qualities to decline while skillful qualities grow.

The Buddha sets up a framework on things to be cultivated or

title: MN 115 Bahudhātuka Sutta: The Many Elements
description:
> … how is a mendicant qualified to be called ‘astute, an inquirer’?

Beginning by praising a wise person, the Buddha goes on to explain that one becomes wise by inquiring into the elements, sense fields, dependent origination, and karma.

title: MN 117 Mahā Cattārīsaka Sutta: The Great Forty
description:
A thorough analysis of the Noble Eightfold Path, breaking it down into its mundane and supermundane versions.

For the more straightforward analysis of the Path, see [SN 45.8](/content/canon/sn45.8).

title: MN 118 Ānāpānasati Sutta: Mindfulness of Breathing
description: The Buddha gives a sixteen-step guided meditation on the breath and then explains how this meditation fulfills the four foundations of mindfulness and the seven factors of enlightenment.

title: MN 119 Kāyagatāsati Sutta: Mindfulness of the Body
description: The Buddha explains how mindfulness of the body should be cultivated and to what benefits it leads.

title: MN 12: Māhasīhanāda Sutta: The Greater Discourse on the Lion’s Roar
description:
A disrobed monk, Sunakkhata, attacks the Buddha’s teaching because it merely leads to the end of suffering. The Buddha counters that this is, in fact, praise, and goes on to enumerate his many profound and powerful achievements.

For a short series of lectures on this sutta by Bhikkhu Bodhi, see [Th

title: MN 121 Cūḷasuññata Sutta: The Shorter Discourse on Emptiness
description:
The Buddha describes his own meditation on emptiness and tells Ānanda how a meditator can descend into emptiness herself through seclusion and wise attention.

For a more detailed, comparative analysis including a practice guide, see [Bhikkhu Analayo'sarticle: "Gradual Entry into Emptiness"](https:/

title: MN 122 Mahāsuññata Sutta: The Longer Discourse on Emptiness
description:
> ... for a long time you have learned the teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and comprehending them theoretically. But a disciple should value following the Teacher, even if asked to go away ...

A group of mendicants have taken to socializing too much, so the B

title: MN 123 Acchariyaabbhuta Sutta: Incredible and Amazing
description:
> It’s incredible, reverends, it’s amazing, the power and might of a Realized One!

Venerable Ānanda is invited by the Buddha to speak on the Buddha’s amazing qualities, and proceeds to list the miraculous events accompanying his birth. The Buddha ends the list with what _he_ thinks is amazing.

title: MN 125 Dantabhūmi Sutta: The Level of the Tamed
description: > What did you expect, Aggivessana? For Prince Jayasena—living in the midst of sensuality, consuming sensuality, chewed on by thoughts of sensuality, burning with the fever of sensuality, intent on the search for sensuality—to know or see or realize that which is to be known through renunciation, se

title: MN 126 Bhūmija Sutta: With Bhūmija
description:
> … heaping sand in a bucket, sprinkling it thoroughly with water, and pressing it out. But by doing this, they couldn’t extract any oil, regardless of whether they made a wish

It's not wishing for *nibbāna* that leads there, but rather putting in the intelligent effort required to walk the path.

title: MN 127 Anuruddha Sutta: With Anuruddha
description: > [A meditator's] physical discomfort is not completely settled, their dullness and drowsiness is not completely eradicated, and their restlessness and remorse is not completely eliminated. Because of this they practice absorption dimly, as it were. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re re

title: MN 128 Upakkilesa Sutta: Corruptions
description:
The Buddha gives an unusually long list of the hindrances to Jhana, starting with quarreling and ending with excessive concentration on forms.

For Bhikkhu Analayo's comments on this sutta, see [Upakkilesa Sutta, 2008](/content/papers/upakkilesa-sutta_analayo).

[Bhante Sujato pointed out](https://d

title: MN 129 Bālapaṇḍita Sutta: The Foolish and the Astute
description:
> The wardens of hell punish them with the five-fold crucifixion. They drive red-hot stakes through the hands and feet, and another in the middle of the chest. And there they feel painful, sharp, severe, acute feelings—but they don’t die until that bad deed is eliminated.

A fool suffers both in thi

title: MN 13: Mahādukkhakkhanda Sutta: The Greater Discourse on the Mass of Suffering
description: Challenged to show the difference between his teaching and that of other ascetics, the Buddha points out that they speak of letting go, but do not really understand why. He then explains in great detail the suffering that arises from attachment to sensual stimulation.

title: MN 133 Mahākaccāna Bhaddekaratta Sutta: Mahākaccāna and the One Fine Night
description:
> Learn the recitation passage and analysis of One Fine Night, mendicant, memorize it, and remember it.
It is beneficial and relates to the fundamentals of the spiritual life.

The verses from [MN 131](/content/canon/mn131) are explained in a different way by Venerable Mahakaccāna.

title: MN 135 Cūḷakammavibhaṅga Sutta: The Shorter Exposition of Action
description:
> Master Gotama, what is the cause and condition why human beings are seen to be inferior and superior?

The Buddha explains to a brahmin how your deeds in past lives affect you in this life.

title: MN 136 Mahā Kamma Vibhaṅga Sutta: The Longer Analysis of Deeds
description:
> They must have done a good deed to be experienced as pleasant either previously or later, or else at the time of death they undertook Right View. And that’s why, when their body broke up, after death, they were reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.

When Samiddhi presented an poorly distilled

title: MN 137 Salāyatana Vibhanga Sutta: The Exposition of the Sixfold Base
description:
> … by depending and relying on the six kinds of joy based on renunciation, abandon and surmount the six kinds of joy based on the household life

The Buddha gives a discourse on the six sense bases, culminating in a unique statement of the Buddha's own basis of equanimity while teaching.

title: MN 139 Araṇa Vibhaṅga Sutta: The Exposition of Non-Conflict
description:
> One should know what it is to extol and what it is to disparage, and knowing both, one should neither extol nor disparage but should teach only the Dhamma.
> One should know how to define pleasure, and knowing that; one should pursue pleasure within oneself.

Achieving peace is no simple matter. T

title: MN 14 Cūḷa Dukkha Khandha Sutta: The Shorter Discourse on the Mass of Suffering
description: > Sensual pleasures give little gratification and much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks. Even though a noble disciple has clearly seen this with right wisdom, so long as they don’t achieve the rapture and bliss that are apart from sensual pleasures and unskillful q

title: MN 140 Dhātu Vibhaṅga Sutta: The Exposition of the Elements
description:
> One should not neglect wisdom, should preserve truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train for peace.

A monk spends the evening in a barn with the Buddha, who rewards the well-mannered disciple with an elaborate and profound discourse on the path and its fruit.

title: MN 141 Sacca Vibhaṅga Sutta: Analysis of the Truths
description:
> Sāriputta is able to teach, assert, establish, clarify, analyze, and reveal the four noble truths.


<!---->

title: MN 142 Dakkhiṇā Vibhaṅga Sutta: The Analysis of Religious Donations
description:
> … there is no way a personal offering can be more fruitful than one bestowed on a Saṅgha

When his step-mother Mahāpajāpatī wishes to offer him a robe for his personal use, the Buddha encourages her to offer it to the entire Saṅgha instead. He goes on to explain that the best kind of offering to t

title: MN 143 Anāthapiṇḍikovāda Sutta: Advice to Anāthapiṇḍika
description:
> … for a long time I have paid homage to the Buddha and the esteemed mendicants. Yet I have never before heard such a Dhamma talk

As the great lay disciple Anāthapiṇḍika lies dying, Venerable Sāriputta visits him and gives a powerful teaching on non-attachment.

title: MN 143 Anāthapiṇḍikovāda Sutta: Advice to Anāthapiṇḍika
description:
> … although I have long waited upon the Teacher and *bhikkhus* worthy of esteem, never before have I heard such a talk on the Dhamma

A beautiful reading of [this wonderful and profound sutta](https://suttacentral.net/mn143/en/sujato){:target="_blank" ga-event-value="0.30"}.

title: MN 145 Puṇṇovāda Sutta: Advice to Puṇṇa
description:
> … if the people of Sunāparanta abuse and threaten me, then I shall think: These people of Sunāparanta are admirable, truly admirable, in that they did not give me a blow with the fist.

An astute monk shows how to practice patience as an immigrant.

title: MN 146.15: The Lamp Simile
description: > … suppose an oil-lamp is burning: its oil is impermanent and subject to change

title: MN 147 Cūḷa Rāhulovāda Sutta: The Shorter Discourse of Advice to Rāhula
description:
> Rāhula, what do you think? Is the eye permanent or impermanent?

The Buddha takes Rāhula with him to a secluded spot in order to lead him on to liberation.

title: MN 148 Chachakka Sutta: Six by Six
description:
The Buddha analyzes the six senses from six different perspectives and encourages us to see them all as "This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self."

> Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, eye contact, feeling, and craving. Bei

title: MN 15 Anumāna Sutta: Measuring Up
description:
> Furthermore, a mendicant is attached to their own views, holding them tight, and refusing to let go. This too is a quality that makes them difficult to admonish.

Venerable Mahā Moggallāna lists 16 qualities that make someone difficult or easy to admonish.

title: MN 150 Nagaravindeyya Sutta: With the People of Nagaravinda
description:
> … ascetics and brahmins who are not free of greed, hate, and delusion for sights known by the eye, who are not peaceful inside, and who conduct themselves badly among the good by way of body, speech, and mind. They don’t deserve honor, respect, reverence, and veneration.

In discussion with a grou

title: MN 152: Indriya-Bhāvanā Sutta: The Development of the Faculties
description: The Buddha teaches Ānanda the development of the faculties for disciples at the entrance, middle and end of the path.

title: MN 16 Cetokhila Sutta: Emotional Barrenness
description:
> … a monk who is endowed with these fifteen factors including exertion, it is possible for [him to attain] breakthrough, it is possible for [him to attain] awakening, it is possible for [him to attain] arrival at unsurpassable security from bondage.

The Buddha explains various ways one can become

title: MN 17 Vanapattha Sutta: Jungle Thickets
description:
> Monks, after consideration, that monk is to leave that wilderness grove; he is not to live there.

The factors a Buddhist should consider when deciding where to stay.

title: MN 18 Madhupiṇḍika Sutta: The Honey-Ball Discourse
description: Challenged by a brahmin, the Buddha gives a coy and cryptic response about the ending of conflicts. Venerable Kaccāna draws out the detailed implications of this in one of the most insightful and difficult suttas in the canon.

title: MN 19 Dvedhāvitakka Sutta: Two Kinds of Thought
description: Recounting his own experiences developing meditation, the Buddha explains how to understand harmful and harmless thoughts, and how to go beyond thought altogether.

title: MN 2 Sabbāsava Sutta: All the Taints
description: Diverse problems demand a diverse range of responses. Rather than selling a "one size fits all" solution, in this sutta the Buddha outlines seven methods for dealing with the afflictions of life and in so doing gives us a comprehensive overview of Buddhist practices.

title: MN 20 Vitakkasaṇṭhāna Sutta: The Relaxation of Thoughts
description: In a practical meditation teaching, the Buddha describes five progressive approaches to arresting unwanted thoughts.

title: MN 21 Kakacūpama Sutta: The Simile of the Saw
description:
> ‘What the hell, Kāḷī!’

> Mendicants, there are these five ways in which others might criticize you. Their speech may be timely or untimely, true or false, gentle or harsh, beneficial or harmful, from a heart of love or from secret hate. When others criticize you, they may do so in any of these wa

title: MN 22 Alagaddūpama Sutta: The Simile of the Water Snake
description:
> I have taught the Dhamma compared to a raft, for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of holding onto. Understanding the Dhamma as taught compared to a raft, you should let go even of Dhammas, to say nothing of non-Dhammas.


In this famous and much-celebrated sutta, the Buddha teache

title: MN 23 Vammika Sutta: The Ant-Hill
description:
> Monk, monk! This ant-hill fumes by night and flames by day. The brahmin said, ‘Take up the sword and dig, O sage!’

In a curious discourse laden with evocative imagery, a deity presents a riddle to a mendicant, who seeks an answer from the Buddha.

title: MN 24 Rathavinīta Sutta: Prepared Chariots
description: Venerable Puṇṇa Mantāṇiputta teaches Venerable Sāriputta about the Buddha's path of purification, explaining that the purification of ethics and mind are not the goal, but are rather stages of the path to it.

title: MN 25: The Nivāpa Sutta: Sowing
description:
> … a trapper doesn’t cast bait for deer thinking, ‘May the deer, enjoying this bait, be healthy and in good condition. May they live long and prosper!’ 🖖

A lovely illustration of the importance of samatha jhana for living the holy life sustainably, and a memorable simile on the ways that Mara can

title: MN 26 Ariyapariyesanā Sutta: The Noble Search
description:
> Bhikkhus, before my enlightenment, while I was still only an unenlightened Bodhisatta, I too, being myself subject to birth, sought what was also subject to birth

The Buddha's own spiritual autobiography, from searching to finding true deliverance.

title: MN 27 Cūḷahatthipadopama Sutta: The Shorter Discourse on the Simile of the Elephant’s Footprint
description: The Buddha gives a rough sketch of the entire path, and encourages us to remain skeptical until the very end.

title: MN 28 Mahāhatthipadopama Sutta: The Longer Simile of the Elephant’s Footprint
description:
> When a space is enclosed by sticks, creepers, grass, and mud it becomes known as a ‘building’. In the same way, when a space is enclosed by bones, sinews, flesh, and skin it becomes known as a ‘form’.

The Venerable Sāriputta shows how all of the teachings fit inside the Four Noble Truths.

title: MN 29 Mahāsāropama Sutta: The Greater Discourse on the Simile of the Heartwood
description: > So this holy life, bhikkhus, does not have gain, honour, and renown for its benefit, or the attainment of virtue for its benefit, or the attainment of concentration for its benefit, or knowledge and vision for its benefit. But it is this unshakeable deliverance of mind that is the goal of this hol

title: MN 3 Dhammadāyāda Sutta: Heirs in the Teaching
description:
> "Be my heirs in the teaching, not in material things."

Venerable Sāriputta explains how by following the Buddha’s example we can experience the spiritual fruits of his path.

title: MN 30 Cūḷasāropama Sutta: The Shorter Simile of the Heartwood
description:
> ... he cuts its inner bark and takes it away, thinking it is heartwood; and so whatever it was he had to make with heartwood, his purpose will not be served.

After the incident with Devadatta, the Buddha cautions the mendicants against becoming complacent and points to liberation as the true hear

title: MN 31 Cūḷagosiṅga Sutta: The Shorter Discourse at Gosiṅga
description:
> Surely, venerable sir, we are living in concord, with mutual appreciation, without disputing, blending like milk and water, viewing each other with kindly eyes.

The Buddha comes across three mendicants practicing diligently and harmoniously, and asks them how they do it.
They explain how they ski

title: MN 32 Mahāgosiṅga Sutta: The Greater Discourse in Gosinga
description:
> What kind of bhikkhu, friend Ānanda, could illuminate the Gosinga Sāla-tree Wood?

A number of the Buddha's greatest disciples gather together and discuss the qualities they admire.

title: MN 33 Mahāgopālaka Sutta: The Longer Discourse on the Cowherd
description:
> And how is a mendicant not skilled in characteristics? It’s when a mendicant doesn’t understand that a fool is characterized by their deeds

For eleven reasons a cowherd is not able to properly look after a herd. The Buddha compares this to the spiritual growth of a yogi.

title: MN 34 Cūḷagopālaka Sutta: The Shorter Discourse on the Cowherd
description:
> For Māra’s stream is breasted now  
And nullified, its reeds removed;  
Rejoice then, bhikkhus, mightily  
And set your hearts where safety lies.

Drawing parallels with a cowherd guiding his herd across a dangerous river, the Buddha presents the various kinds of enlightened disciples who cross th

title: MN 35: Cūḷa Saccaka Sutta: The Shorter Discourse With Saccaka
description: Saccaka the debater challenges the Buddha. The Buddha is unimpressed.

title: MN 36 Mahāsaccaka Sutta: The Longer Discourse With Saccaka
description:
> Why am I afraid of that pleasure that has nothing to do with sensual pleasures and unwholesome states?

The Buddha responds to a follower of another religion with a long account of the various austerities he practiced before awakening, detailing the astonishing lengths he took to learn the truth o

title: MN 38 Mahātaṇhāsaṅkhaya Sutta: The Greater Craving-Destruction Discourse
description:
If there is rebirth, then what gets reborn?

In this sutta, a bhikkhu named Sāti promulgates the pernicious view that consciousness transmigrates from life to life. The Buddha reprimands him with a lengthy discourse on dependent origination, explaining that all phenomena of existence arise and cease

title: MN 39.12: The Similes on Overcoming the Hindrances
description: The Buddha compares the five hindrances to debt, a disease, a prison, slavery, and a desert.

title: MN 39 Mahā Assapura Sutta: The Greater Discourse at Assapura
description:
> What are the qualities that make one a contemplative?

The Buddha gives an overview of the path from the perspective of ethics, from the establishment of shame all the way to the realization of the highest good: Nibbāna.

title: MN 4 Bhayabherava Sutta: Fear and Dread
description:
> Remote jungle-thicket resting places in the forest are hard to endure, seclusion is hard to practise, and it is hard to enjoy solitude.

The Buddha explains the difficulties of living in the wilderness, and how they are overcome by purity of conduct and meditation.
He recounts some of the fears an

title: MN 40 Mahāassapura Sutta: The Shorter Discourse at Assapura
description: A spiritual practice doesn't come with external trappings, but with sincere inner change.

title: MN 41 Cūḷaassapura Sutta: The Brahmins of Sālā
description: One of the most detailed descriptions of morality in the early canon, this discourse lists twenty kinds of actions: unwholesome and wholesome.

title: MN 42 Verañjaka Sutta: The People of Verañja
description: Very similar to [MN 41](/content/canon/mn41), this British recording of the Buddha's words on ethics is included for your historical imagination.

title: MN 43 Mahāvedalla Sutta: The Great Classification
description:
> Wisdom and consciousness--these things are mixed, not separate. And you can never completely dissect them

Venerable Sāriputta deftly defines a bewildering array of terms.

title: MN 44 Cūḷavedalla Sutta: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers
description: A deep discussion between the Bhikkhuni Dhammadinnā and her student, the layman Visākha, on many profound topics, including the very highest meditative attainments.

title: MN 45 Cūḷa Dhamma Samādāna Sutta: The Shorter Discourse on Taking Up Practices
description: > There is a way of undertaking dhammas that is pleasant now and ripens in the future as pain. There is a way of undertaking dhammas that is painful now and ripens in the future as pain. There is a way of undertaking dhammas that is painful now and ripens in the future as pleasure. There is a way of

title: MN 46 Mahā Dhamma Samādāna Sutta: The Greater Discourse on Ways of Undertaking Things
description:
> Here, bhikkhus, someone in pain and grief abstains from killing living beings, and he experiences pain and grief that have abstention from killing living beings as condition.

In this sutta, the Buddha admits that following the ethical path isn't always pleasant. Still, he assures us it's worthwhi

title: MN 47 Vīmaṁsaka Sutta: The Inquirer
description:
> Is this venerable one restrained without fear, not restrained by fear, and does he avoid indulging in sensual pleasures because he is without lust through the destruction of lust?

The Buddha gives a thorough and exacting method for those who wish to investigate his qualifications as a spiritual t

title: MN 48 Kosambiya Sutta: The Mendicants of Kosambi
description:
> … this is the nature of a person accomplished in view. Though they might manage a diverse spectrum of duties for their spiritual companions, they still feel a keen regard for the training in higher ethics, higher mind, and higher wisdom.


The Buddha taught the reluctant, quarrelling monks of Kosa

title: MN 49 Brahmanimantanika Sutta: On the Invitation of Brahmā
description: A god gains the conceit of being eternal and omniscient and the Buddha plays a game of hide-and-seek with him to demonstrate the realms beyond that god's ken.

title: MN 5 Anaṅgaṇa Sutta: Unblemished
description:
> What is the cause, what is the reason why, of the two persons without a blemish, one is said to be worse and one better?

On the importance of mindfulness in our cultivation of virtue.

title: MN 50 Māra Tajjanīya Sutta: The Rebuke of Māra
description:
> Then Māra came up out of Moggallāna’s mouth and stood against the door

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna confronts the evil one with a surprising tale.

title: MN 51 Kandaraka Sutta: With Kandaraka
description:
> What kind of person does not torment himself, not being interested in self-torture, and does not torment others, not being interested in torturing others?

Contrasting the openness of animals with the duplicity of humans, The Buddha explains how to lead the religious life in a way that is truly ad

title: MN 52 Aṭṭhakanāgara Sutta: The Man from Aṭṭhakanagara
description:
> … while I was seeking one door to the Deathless, I have come—all at once—to hear of eleven!

Venerable Ānanda teaches a wealthy merchant how to use eleven different meditative states as gateways to enlightenment.

title: MN 53 Sekha Sutta: A Trainee
description: Venerable Ānanda discusses the qualities of a noble trainee.

title: MN 54 The Potaliya Sutta: With Potaliya the Householder
description: The Buddha gives an alternate version of "the eight precepts" which separate a layman from a renunciant and provides a series of similes about the dangers of sensual pleasures.

title: MN 55 Jīvaka Sutta: With Jīvaka
description: The Buddha’s personal doctor, Jīvaka, hears criticisms of the Buddha’s policy regarding eating meat, and asks him about it.

title: MN 56 The Upāli Sutta: With Upāli
description:
> "unintentional acts are not very blameworthy."  
"But if they are intentional?"  
"Then they are very blameworthy."  
"But where does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta say that intention is classified?"  
"In the mental rod, sir."  

The Buddha debates karma with a rich supporter of the Jains, winning him over.

title: MN 57.27: The Leper Simile
description: >  Is it only now that that fire is painful to touch, hot, and scorching, or previously too was that fire painful to touch, hot, and scorching?

title: MN 57 Kukkuravatika Sutta: The Dog-Duty Ascetic
description: > ‘By this virtue or observance or asceticism or holy life I shall become a great god or some lesser god,’ that is wrong view in his case. Now there are two destinations for one with wrong view, I say: hell or the animal realm. So, _Puṇṇa_, if his dog-duty succeeds, it will lead him to the company o

title: MN 58 The Abhaya Rājakumāra Sutta: With Prince Abhaya
description:
> I’d hold his head with my left hand, and take [the stone] out using a hooked finger of my right hand, even if it drew blood.

The leader of the Jains, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, gives his disciple Prince Abhaya a dilemma to pose to the Buddha.

title: MN 59 Bahuvedaniya Sutta: The Many Kinds of Feeling
description:
> … if someone were to say: '[Sensual pleasure] is the highest pleasure and joy that can be experienced,' I would not concede that. And why not? Because there is another kind of pleasure which surpasses that

The Buddha resolves a disagreement on the number of kinds of feelings that he taught, point

title: MN 6 Ākaṅkheyya Sutta: One Might Wish
description:
> … let them fulfill their precepts, be committed to inner serenity of the heart, not neglect absorption, be endowed with discernment, and frequent empty huts.

Careful observance of ethical precepts is the foundation of all higher achievements in the spiritual life.

title: MN 61 Ambalaṭṭhikarāhulovāda Sutta: Instructions to Rahula
description:
> Thus, Rahula, you should train yourself, 'I will not tell a deliberate lie even in jest.'

Using the “object lesson” of a cup of water, the Buddha explains to his son, Rāhula, the importance of telling the truth and reflecting on one’s motives.

title: MN 62 Mahārāhulovāda Sutta: The Longer Advice to Rāhula
description:
> … when people put clean things, unclean things, excrement, urine, saliva, pus, or blood on the earth, the earth is not bothered, humiliated, or disgusted, in the same way, Rāhula, practice ‘peacefulness of earth’ meditation.

> Then Venerable Rāhula, thinking “How could one who has been personally

title: MN 63 Cūḷamālukya Sutta: The Shorter Discourse to Mālunkyāputta
description: A monk wonders why the Buddha hasn't disclosed certain cosmological facts, and the Buddha informs him that such views are not conducive to the ending of stress.

title: MN 64 Mahāmālukya Sutta: The Greater Discourse to Mālunkyāputta
description:
> … a young tender infant lying prone does not even have the notion ‘identity,’ so how could identity view arise in him?

A little baby has no wrong views or intentions, but the underlying tendency for these things is still there. Without practicing, they will inevitably recur.

title: MN 65 Bhaddāli Sutta: With Bhaddāli
description: > Being censured in this way by the Teacher, by wise companions in the holy life, by gods, and by himself, he realises no superhuman state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. Why is that? That is how it is with one who does not fulfil the training in the Teacher’s Dispe

title: MN 66 Laṭukikopama Sutta: The Simile of the Quail
description:
> The Buddha has rid us of so many things that bring suffering and gifted us so many things that bring happiness!

> … take a person practicing to give up and let go of attachments. As they do so, every so often they lose mindfulness, and memories and thoughts connected with attachments beset them.

title: MN 67 Cātuma Sutta: At Cātumā
description:
> The Buddha said to them: "Mendicants, what’s with that dreadful racket? You’d think it was fishermen hauling in a catch!"

The Buddha describes the four dangers one can expect after going forth.

title: MN 68 Naḷakapāna Sutta: At Naḷakapāna
description:
> Recollecting that nun’s faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom, [one] applies her mind to that end. This is how a nun lives at ease.

The Buddha interrogates a group of shy monks, and explains why he reveals the attainments of his disciples.

title: MN 7 The Vatthupama Sutta: The Simile of the Cloth
description: The Vattha Sutta is a beautiful and somewhat unusual description of the path to stream entry and beyond.

title: MN 70 Kīṭāgiri Sutta: At Kīṭāgiri
description:
> But because it is known by me, seen, found, realised, contacted by wisdom thus: ‘Here, when someone feels a certain kind of pleasant feeling, unwholesome states increase in him and wholesome states diminish,’ that I therefore say: ‘Abandon such a kind of pleasant feeling.’

The Buddha admonishes a

title: MN 71 Tevijja Vacchagotta Sutta: To Vacchagotta on the Three Knowledges
description:
> Master Gotama, are there any laypeople who, without giving up the fetter of lay life, make an end of suffering when the body breaks up?

On the limits of the lay life... and the Buddha's omniscience.

title: MN 72 Aggivaccha Sutta: With Vacchagotta
description:
> A ‘position,’ Vaccha, is something that a Tathāgata has done away with.

Refusing to take a stance regarding useless metaphysical speculations, the Buddha illustrates the spiritual goal with the simile of a flame going out.

title: MN 73 Mahāvaccha Sutta: The Longer Discourse With Vacchagotta
description:
> But because, Master Gotama, monks, nuns, celibate laymen, laymen enjoying sensual pleasures, celibate laywomen, and laywomen enjoying sensual pleasures have all succeeded in this teaching, this spiritual path is complete in that respect.

Vacchagotta finally lets go of his obsession with meaningle

title: MN 74 The Dīghanakha Sutta: To LongNails
description:
> … even this view of yours, Aggivessana—‘All is not pleasing to me’—is even that not pleasing to you?

Deftly outmaneuvering an extreme skeptic, the Buddha discusses the outcomes of belief and disbelief. Rather than getting stuck in abstractions, he encourages staying close to one's experiences.

title: MN 75 Māgaṇḍiya Sutta: To Māgandiya
description:
> Indeed, I have long been tricked, cheated, and defrauded by this mind.

A fun and surprising sutta in which a bumbling but faithful Brahmin is set straight.

title: MN 76 Sandaka Sutta: The Sandaka Sutta
description:
> ‘I don’t say it’s like this. I don’t say it’s like that. I don’t say it’s otherwise. I don’t say it’s not so. And I don’t deny it’s not so.’  
> A sensible person reflects on this matter in this way: ‘This teacher is dull and stupid.’

Venerable Ānanda lists eight warning signs to avoid when choos

title: MN 77 Mahā Sakuludāyi Sutta: The Greater Discourse to Sakuludāyin
description:
> And even those disciples of his who fall out with their companions in the holy life and abandon the training to return to the low life—even they praise the Master and the Dhamma and the Sangha; they blame themselves instead of others, saying: "We were unlucky, we have little merit"

The ascetic Sa

title: MN 79 Cūḷasakuludāyi Sutta: The Shorter Discourse With Sakuludāyī
description:
> But what is that ultimate splendor compared to which no other splendor is finer?

A wanderer teaches his doctrine of the “highest splendor” but is unable to give a satisfactory account of what that means. The Buddha memorably compares him to someone who is in love with a women he has never met.

title: MN 8.61: The Mud Simile
description: The Buddha explains that only the enlightened can truly teach.

title: MN 8: Sallekha (Effacement)
description:
> ‘Others will be cruel; we shall not be cruel here’

The Buddha gives a comprehensive moral rubric we can use to assess and guide our development of self-effacement.

title: MN 81 Ghaṭikāra Sutta: With Ghaṭikāra
description: The Buddha smiles and tells Ānanda an entertaining story of a lay anāgāmī and a reluctant renunciate at the time of the Buddha Kassapa, demonstrating that the Buddha wasn't always so wise in his previous lives.

title: MN 82 Raṭṭhapāla Sutta: On Raṭṭhapāla
description:
> Then, not receiving his parents’ permission to go forth, the clansman Raṭṭhapāla lay down there on the bare floor, saying: "Right here I shall either die or receive the going forth."

This long sutta tells the story of Raṭṭhapāla's going forth: a model of monastic behavior for Theravādins even tod

title: MN 85 Bodhirājakumāra Sutta: With Prince Bodhi
description:
> Then it occurred to me, ‘I can’t achieve that pleasure with a body so excessively emaciated. Why don’t I eat some solid food, some rice and porridge?’

The Buddha tells the story of his striving to a faithful Brahmin.

title: MN 86 Aṅgulimāla Sutta: With Aṅgulimāla
description:
> It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! How the Buddha tames those who are wild

One of the most beloved stories in the Pāli Canon.

title: MN 87 Piyajātika Sutta: Born from Affection
description:
> I did not delight in the contemplative Gotama’s speech; I condemned it, rose from my seat, and left!

A grieving father is having none of the Buddha's nonsense, and King Pasenadi gets a damma talk from his wife, Queen Mallikā, on the dangers of affection in this entertaining sutta.

title: MN 89 Dhammacetiya Sutta: Monuments to the Dhamma
description:
> King Pasenadi entered the dwelling.
He prostrated himself at the Blessed One’s feet, and then he covered the Blessed One’s feet with kisses, caressing them with his hands and pronouncing his name...

King Pasenadi, near the end of his life, visits the Buddha, and pronounces the reasons for his dev

title: MN 9.17: The Four Noble Truths
description: Venerable Sāriputta gives a detailed explanation of right view in terms of the Four Noble Truths.

title: MN 9 Sammādiṭṭhi Sutta: Right View
description:
> Venerables, how does a noble disciple have right perspective?

Venerable Sāriputta gives a detailed explanation of Right View, the first factor of the noble eightfold path. At the prompting of the other mendicants, he approaches the topic from a wide range of perspectives.

title: MN 90 Kaṇṇakatthala Sutta: At Kaṇṇakatthala
description: King Pasenadi questions the Buddha on a few miscellaneous matters (omniscience, caste and the gods) showing what kinds of religious debates were current in India at the time and how the Buddha responded.

title: MN 91 Brahmāyu Sutta: With Brahmāyu
description:
A respected brahmin sends a student to closely examine the Buddha, and see if he measures up to the Brahmanical prophecies.

The most detailed description of the Buddha and his habits in the Canon.

title: MN 94 Ghoṭamukha Sutta: With Ghoṭamukha
description:
> … there is no true wandering: that is how it appears to me

Venerable Udena explains to a polite but sceptical Brahmin what makes someone a true recluse.

title: MN 95 Caṅkī Sutta: With Caṅkī
description:
> If a person has faith, they preserve truth by saying, ‘Such is my faith.’ But they don’t yet come to the definite conclusion: ‘This is the only truth, other ideas are silly.’

The Buddha instructs a Brahmin on the right way to talk about religion and how to make our way through the thicket of view

title: MN 96: Esukārī Sutta: With Esukārī
description:
> Brahmin, I don’t say that coming from an eminent family makes you a better or worse person. I don’t say that being very beautiful makes you a better or worse person. I don’t say that being very wealthy makes you a better or worse person.

The Buddha strongly rejects the caste system and the "prosp

title: MN 97 Dhanañjāni Sutta: With Dhanañjāni
description:
> … why did you get up from your seat and leave while there was still more left to do?

A corrupt tax-collector is (partially) redeemed by an encounter with Venerable Sāriputta.

title: MN 98: Vāseṭṭha Sutta: With Vāseṭṭha
description:
> is one a brahmin due to birth,  
or else because of actions?

Two brahmin students ask the Buddha about what makes a brahmin. The Buddha points out that, while the species of animals are determined by birth, for humans what matters is not your race or caste but how you chose to live.

title: MN 99: Subha Sutta: With Subha
description:
> The lay life is like farming in that it’s work with many requirements and when it fails it’s not very fruitful; but when it succeeds it is very fruitful.

Working hard is not valuable in and of itself; what matters is the outcome. And just as in lay life, spiritual practice may or may not lead to

title: Mv 1–4 Mahākhandhako: The Great Chapter of the Vinaya
description: The opening four chapters of the Vinaya record the inspiring story of the Buddha's enlightenment and of his first, eventful year of teaching.

title: Mvu 94: From Uruvilvā to Ṛṣipatana
description: A translation from the Mahāvastu on the Buddha's first journey after the Awakening to the place where he would give his first official teaching.

title: The _Nidānakathā_:  Introduction to the Jātaka Stories
description:
The traditional, commentarial introduction to the Pāli Jātaka collection containing the most famous mythologized biography of the Buddha.

This translation by T. W. Rhys Davids also contains his own introduction to the Jātakas, which remains worth a read even a century later.

title: The Sūtra of the Heart of Transcendent Wisdom: A Tibetan Heart Sūtra Liturgy
Paṭisambhidāmagga Ānāpānasati-Kathā: The Explanation of Mindfulness of Breathing in The Path of Discrimination
description:
> These are the over two hundred kinds of knowledge that arise in one who develops concentration by mindfulness of breathing with sixteen grounds

> … the earliest extant, detailed
 commentary on Buddhist meditation available in an Indic language
 
The Path of Discrimination was a key influence on l

title: Paccayuddesaniddesa: The Enumeration and Explanation of the Conditions
description: The introduction to the _Paṭṭhānapāḷi_,  the last book of the Abhidhamma, which sets out the conditions of the mind.

title: The First Sanghādisesa Rule for Bhikkhus: The Vinaya Pitaka Text and its Commentarial Exegesis
description: > The present compilation brings together in English translation the most important Pali Vinaya texts dealing with the first rule in the *Sanghādisesa* section of the Bhikkhu Pātimokkha: the training rule on intentional emission of semen — one of the disciplinary rules most fundamental to [a bhikkhu

title: The Mahākhandhaka
description: The canonical account of the Buddha's first days and the story of how the religion was founded.

title: The Story of Jīvaka
description:
> "It would not be appropriate for me to give the Buddha a powerful laxative."

The first few sections of the Robe Chapter tells of the origins and exploits of the Buddha's personal physician.

Note that, while the treatments described in this tale remain undeniably dramatic, they nevertheless prese

title: Pv 1.12 Uraga Sutta: The Snake
description:
> I do not cry over my dead son. He went to another life according to his karma.

A family explains their lack of tears.

title: Pv 1.3 Pūtimukha Sutta: Stinky Mouth
description: > … your mouth is being eaten by worms

title: Pv 1.5 Tirokudda Kanda: Hungry Shades Outside the Walls
description: > Offerings should be given for the dead

title: Pv 1.9 Mahāpesakāra Sutta: The Master Weaver
description: > When I offered gifts to monks, she would insult me.

title: Noble Sūtra of Recalling the Three Jewels
description: A simple, daily recitation of the qualities of the triple gem from the Tibetan Tradition.

title: The Lament of Rudra: From the Immaculate Confession Tantra
description:
> Kind protector, compassionate deity, heed me now, I pray

Notice how the self-empowering doctrine of Karma leaves a strong yearning for an external source of grace. A common trend in later Buddhisms was to provide such a God: either as a tantric Buddha, like here, or as a "distant" Buddha, like in

title: On the Six Sense Spheres 3: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 250 to 255
description: > In this article I translate the second half of the ninth fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 250 to 255.

title: SA 18: The Discourse on Not Belonging to Another
description:
> Whatever does not belong to you and does not belong to others, these things should quickly be eradicated and relinquished.

A clever Bhikkhu quickly understands a pithy teaching.

title: On the Five Aggregates 1: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 1 to 32
description: > This article translates the first fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 1 to 32.

title: SA 267: The Second Discourse on Not Knowing
description:
> Have you seen the variegated and different colours of a _caraṇa_ bird?

The Buddha encourages the monks to investigate the five aggregates, giving a few colorful similes to illustrate their nature.

title: On the Five Aggregates 2: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 256 to 272
description:
> This article translates the second fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 256 to 272.

> The translated section comprises the tenth fascicle of the Taishō edition of the Saṃyukta-āgama

title: SA 301: The Discourse on the Middle Way
description: > It is wrong perception that leads to the concepts of being and nonbeing.

title: On the Five Aggregates 3: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 59 to 87
description: > This article translates the third fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 59 to 87.

title: On the Five Aggregates 4: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 33 to 58
description: > This article translates the fourth fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 33 to 58.

title: On the Five Aggregates 5: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 103 to 110
description: > This article translates the fifth fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 103 to 110.

title: On Rādha and Views: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 111 to 138
description: > This article translates the sixth fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 111 to 138.

title: On Views and Penetrative Knowledge: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 139 to 187
description: > This article translates the seventh fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 139 to 187.

title: On the Six Sense Spheres 1: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 188 to 229
description: > This article translates the eighth fascicle of the Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 188 to 229.

title: On the Six Sense Spheres 2: A Translation of Saṃyukta-āgama Discourses 230 to 249
description:
> In this article I translate the first half of the ninth fascicle of the 
Saṃyukta-āgama, which contains discourses 230 to 249.

title: SF 238 Pratītyasamutpādā Di-vibhaṅga Nirdeśa Sūtra
description: An individual, Sanskrit text in the *Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṁgrahaḥ* showing the remarkable similarity between the Pali Canon and the early texts of the Mahayana.

title: SN 1.1 Oghataraṇa Sutta: Crossing the Flood
description:
> By not halting, friend, and by not straining I crossed the flood.

How the Buddha crossed the flood of suffering.

title: SN 1.11 Nandana Sutta: Nandana
description:
> They do not know bliss  
Who have not seen Nandana

A deva thinks his pleasures are supreme.

title: SN 1.12 Nandati Sutta: Delight
description:
> Standing to one side, that deity recited this verse...

We think our attachments bring us happiness, but they really bring sorrow.

title: SN 1.20 Samiddhi Sutta: Samiddhi
description:
> … sensual pleasures are time-consuming, full of suffering and despair, and the danger in them is greater still

A deva tried to convince a young monk to enjoy sensual pleasures and the Buddha rebukes the angel with a series of verses explaining that this young monk is already an arahant.

title: SN 1.23 Jaṭā Sutta: The Tangle
description:
> Where name-and-form ceases,  
Stops without remainder,  
And also impingement and perception of form:  
It is here this tangle is cut.

This generation is all tangled up like a nest of matted hair. Who can untangle this mess and how?

title: SN 1.24 Manonivāraṇa Sutta: Reining in the Mind
description:
> Should one rein in the mind from everything...

Or only from what is unwholesome?

title: SN 1.33 Sādhu Sutta: Good
description: A series of deities give verses praising generosity and the Buddha gives his own response.

title: SN 1.36 Saddhā Sutta: Faith
description:
> No ties torment one who has nothing

A series of Satullapa gods address the Buddha in verse.

title: SN 1.40 Dutiya Pajjunna Dhītu Sutta: Pajjunna’s Daughter (2)
description:
> One should not pursue a course  
That is painful and harmful.

Kokanada gives a pithy teaching in verse.

title: SN 1.41 Āditta Sutta: Ablaze
description:
> When one’s house is ablaze  
The vessel taken out  
Is the one that is useful,  
Not the one left burnt inside.

A deity recites some verses to the Buddha.

title: SN 1.42 Kiṁdada Sutta: Giving What
description:
> Giving food you give strength.  
Giving clothes you give beauty.

A few verses on the results of giving particularly praising the giving of shelter.

title: SN 1.46 Accharā Sutta: Nymphs
description:
> ‘The straight way’ that path is called,  
And ‘fearless’ is its destination.

To escape from the Forest of Delusion, one needs the vehicle of the Dhamma.

title: SN 1.49 Macchari Sutta: Samiddhi
description:
> These brighten up the heavens  
Where they’ve been reborn.

The Buddha is asked about the future destiny of people who are generous—and not.

title: SN 1.51 Jarā Sutta: Old Age
description:
> What is good      all the way through old age?

<!---->

title: SN 1.66 Attahata Sutta: Afflicted
description:
> By what is the world afflicted?  
By what is it enveloped?

The world is burning with desire.

title: SN 1.71 Chetvā Sutta: Having Slain
description:
> What is the one thing, O Gotama,
Whose killing you approve?

<!---->

title: SN 1.73 Vitta Sutta: Treasure
description:
> What here is a man’s best treasure?

A series of questions on what is best.

title: SN 1.78 Kāma Sutta: Desire
description:
> What should one who desires the good
not give away?  
What should a mortal not reject?

<!---->

title: SN 1.79 Pātheyya Sutta: Provisions for a Journey
description:
> What is the abode of wealth?  
What drags a person around?

<!---->

title: SN 10.12 Āḷavaka Sutta: With Āḷavaka
description:
> If you don’t answer me, I’ll drive you insane...

The famous story of the spirit Āḷavaka, who tries to bully the Buddha, but is soon converted through a surprisingly insightful series of questions and answers. This discourse provided the background for several elaborate legends in the later tradit

title: SN 10.4 Maṇibhadda Sutta: With Maṇibhadda
description:
> The mindful one grows better each day  
but isn’t totally freed from animosity.

The spirit Maṇibhadda speaks in praise of mindfulness, opining that a mindful one is free of hate. The Buddha responds that yes, mindfulness is wonderful, but only through developing love is one free of hate.

title: SN 10.7 Punabbasu Sutta: With Punabbasu
description: A female spirit hushes her children as she listens to the Dhamma.

title: SN 10.8 Sudatta Sutta: With Sudatta
description: When Anāthapiṇḍika heard that a Buddha had arisen in the world, he rose first thing in the morning to go and visit him. But a mysterious darkness causes him to hesitate...

title: SN 11.11 Vatapada Sutta: Vows
description:
> … when Sakka, lord of the devas, was a human being, he adopted and undertook seven vows

<!---->

title: SN 11.14 Dalidda Sutta: Poor
description:
> Formerly, when this young deva was a human being, he undertook faith, virtue, learning, generosity, and wisdom...

The *deva*s complained when a poor man was reborn in heaven, even outshining them!

title: SN 11.15 Rāmaṇeyyaka Sutta: A Delightful Place
description:
> Shrines in parks and woodland shrines,  
Well-constructed lotus ponds:  
These are not worth a sixteenth part  
Of a delightful human being.  
>  
> Whether in a village or forest,  
In a valley or on the plain--  
Wherever the *arahants* dwell  
Is truly a delightful place.

Sakka asks what place

title: SN 11.18 Gahaṭṭha Vandanā Sutta: Who Sakka Worships
description: When Sakka lifts his joined palms to the four quarters, his charioteer Mātali points out that Sakka is venerated by gods and men, and asks who he venerates.

title: SN 11.22 Dubbaṇṇiya Sutta: Ugly
description:
> Then, dear sirs, he must be an anger-eating yakkha.

When an ugly spirit takes Sakka’s throne, the gods were outraged. But the more they complained, the prettier he became. Sakka realized this was the so-called “anger-eating demon”, and defeated him by treating him with kindness and respect instea

title: SN 11.3 Dhajagga Sutta: The Banner’s Crest
description: The Buddha encourages the mendicants to recollect the Triple Gem to abandon any fear that may arise on the path.

title: SN 11.5 Subhāsitajaya Sutta: Victory by Good Speech
description:
> Nothing better
than patience
is found.

The demon lord Vepacitti proposes to Sakka that they engage in a battle of wits rather than war.

title: SN 11.6 Kulāvaka Sutta: Bird Nests
description:
> Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the demons...

Fleeing the demon host, Sakka’s chariot risks endangering the nests of little birds in the forest. Rather than render the birds homeless, Sakka instructs his charioteer to turn back, even at the cost of his life.

title: SN 12.10 Gotama Sutta: Gotama
description: The current Buddha Gotama, reflecting on how the world had fallen into suffering, became awakened by understanding dependent origination.

title: SN 12.15 Kaccanagotta Sutta: Kaccanagotta
description:
> … for one who sees the origin of the world as it really is with correct wisdom, there is no notion of nonexistence in regard to the world. And for one who sees the cessation of the world as it really is with correct wisdom, there is no notion of existence in regard to the world.

Venerable Kaccāna

title: SN 12.17 Acelakassapa Sutta: With Kassapa, the Naked Ascetic
description:
> Kassapa, if one thinks, ‘The one who acts is the same as the one who experiences the result,’ then one asserts with reference to one existing from the beginning: ‘Suffering is created by oneself.’ When one asserts thus, this amounts to eternalism.

A naked ascetic named Kassapa approaches the Budd

title: SN 12.18 Timbaruka Sutta: With Timbaruka
description:
> How is it, Master Gotama: are pleasure and pain created by oneself?

The Buddha wins over a wanderer by giving a more nuanced understanding of karma.

title: SN 12.19 Bālapaṇḍita Sutta: The Astute and the Foolish
description: Both the wise and the foolish have been reborn in this life due to their deeds conditioned by ignorance in past lives. But a fool continues to make the same mistakes and is reborn yet again, whereas a wise person lives the holy life and is not.

title: SN 12.2 Vibhaṅga Sutta: The Analysis Sutta
description: The canonical analysis of dependent origination as given in the Theravāda, parallel to [this Tibetan](/content/canon/toh211) and [this Mahayana](/content/canon/sf238) version.

title: SN 12.20 Paccaya Sutta: Conditions
description:
The Buddha distinguishes between “dependently originated phenomena”—the twelve factors—and “dependent origination”—the principle of conditionality.

> When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple has clearly seen with correct wisdom as it really is this dependent origination and these dependently arisen phenome

title: SN 12.23 Upanisa Sutta: Proximate Cause
description:
> … with liberation as proximate cause, the knowledge of destruction.

A "tremendously important" sutta showing how liberation is _also_ governed by the law of Dependent Origination.

For Bhikkhu Bodhi's commentary on this sutta, see [*Transcendental Dependent Arising*](/content/booklets/transcendan

title: SN 12.26 Upavāṇa Sutta: With Upavāna
description: Rather than saying "who" creates our suffering, the Buddha says "what" suffering (and views about it) depend on.

title: SN 12.27 Paccaya Sutta: Conditions
description: The insight that leads to stream entry is the direct knowledge of dependent origination.

title: SN 12.37 Natumha Sutta: Not Yours
description:
> Bhikkhus, this body is not yours, nor does it belong to others. It is old kamma, to be seen as generated and fashioned by volition, as something to be felt.

<!---->

title: SN 12.38 Cetanā Sutta: Intention
description:
> Bhikkhus, what one intends, and what one plans, and whatever one has a tendency towards: this becomes a basis for the maintenance of consciousness.

<!---->

title: SN 12.40 Tatiyacetanā Sutta: Volition (3)
