description: A pithy and deep sutta on the true difference between the ordinary and the enlightened mind.

title: SN 12.41 Pañcaverabhaya Sutta: Five Feaful Animosities
description: A noble disciple has eliminated the fear that comes from breaking precepts, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and understands dependent origination.

title: SN 12.43 Dukkha Sutta: Suffering
description:
> Bhikkhus, I will teach you the origin and the passing away of suffering. Listen to that and attend closely, I will speak.

Sense contact gives rise to craving... or to cessation.

title: SN 12.45 Ñātika Sutta: At Ñātika
description: A monk overhears the Buddha talking to himself...

title: SN 12.46 Aññatara Brāhmaṇa Sutta: A Certain Brahmin
description:
> Is the one who acts the same as the one who experiences the result?

<!---->

title: SN 12.48 Lokāyatika Sutta: A Cosmologist
description:
> ‘All exists’: this is the oldest cosmology, brahmin.

The Buddha rejects all such views as too extreme.

title: SN 12.49 Ariyasāvaka Sutta: A Noble Disciple
description: A noble disciple does not think about the links of dependent origination, as they see them directly and know them for themselves.

title: SN 12.51 Parivīmaṁsana Sutta: Inquiry
description:
> ... he understands: ‘I feel a feeling terminating with life.’
He understands:
‘With the breakup of the body, following the exhaustion of life, all that is felt, not being delighted in, will become cool right here; mere bodily remains will be left.’

A mendicant (whether enlightened or not!) should

title: SN 12.52 Upādāna Sutta: Grasping
description:
> Bhikkhus, when one dwells contemplating gratification in things that can be clung to, craving increases.

Illustrated with the simile of a bonfire.

title: SN 12.53 Saṁyojana Sutta: Fetters
description:
> Thus, sustained by that oil, fuelled by it, that oil lamp would burn for a very long time. So too, when one lives contemplating gratification in things that can fetter, craving increases…

Craving increases when you linger on pleasing things that stimulate fetters, illustrated with the simile of a

title: SN 12.55 Mahārukkha Sutta: A Great Tree
description:
> Suppose, bhikkhus, there was a great tree. Then a man would come along bringing a shovel and a basket. He would cut down the tree at its foot, dig it up, and pull out the roots…

Craving increases when you linger on pleasing things that stimulate grasping.

title: SN 12.57 Taruṇarukkha Sutta: A Sapling
description:
> Sustained by that care, nourished by it, that sapling would attain to growth, increase, and expansion. So too, when one dwells contemplating gratification in things that can fetter, craving increases.

Illustrated with the simile of a sapling.

title: SN 12.58 Nāmarūpa Sutta: Name and Form
description:
> Bhikkhus, when one dwells contemplating gratification in things that can fetter, there is a descent of name-and-form.

The mental and physical organism is reborn when you linger on pleasing things which stimulate the fetters, illustrated with the simile of a tree.

title: SN 12.59 Viññāṇa Sutta: Consciousness
description:
> …when one dwells contemplating danger in things that can fetter, there is no descent of consciousness…

Rebirth illustrated with the simile of a tree.

title: SN 12.60 Nidāna Sutta: Sources
description:
> Fed and fuelled by that, the great tree would stand for a long time.

When Ānanda suggests that dependent origination is simple, the Buddha rebukes him and explains how stable and hard to eradicate it is with the simile of a great tree.

title: SN 12.61 Assutavā Sutta: Uninstructed
description:
> But that which is called ‘mind’ and ‘sentience’ and ‘consciousness’ arises as one thing and ceases as another by day and by night.

An ignorant person might become free of attachment to their body, but not their mind. Still, it would be better to attach to the body, as it is at less changeable tha

title: SN 12.63 Puttamaṁsa Sutta: A Child’s Flesh
description:
> ... by eating their son’s flesh they would cross the rest of the desert.

> It is in such a way, bhikkhus, that I say nutriment should be seen.

The Buddha defines the four kinds of “food” or “nutriment”, which include edible food, contact, intention, and consciousness. He illustrates them with a

title: SN 12.64 Atthi Rāga Sutta: If There Is Desire
description:
> If, bhikkhus, there is lust for contact...

The Buddha defines the four kinds of “food” or “nutriment”, which include edible food, contact, intention, and consciousness, showing how they lead to suffering according to dependent origination.

title: SN 12.65 Nagara Sutta: The City
description:
The analogy of Nibbana as a lost city is given its earliest expression in this sutta, which beautifully tells of the Buddha's discovery of the Noble Path, and connects dependent arising to the Four Noble Truths, tying together all the Buddha's core teachings.

It is interesting to compare this sutta

title: SN 12.67 Naḷakalāpī Sutta: The Sheaves of Reeds
description:
> Just as two sheaves of reeds might stand leaning against each other, so too, with name-and-form as condition, consciousness comes to be; with consciousness as condition, name-and-form comes to be.

Venerables Mahākoṭṭhita and Sāriputta discuss whether the factors of dependent origination are creat

title: SN 12.68 Kosambī Sutta: Kosambī
description:
> … though I have clearly seen as it really is with correct wisdom, ‘Nibbāna is the cessation of existence,’ I am not an arahant

Venerable Saviṭṭha questions Venerable Musīla about his attainments, and mistakenly concludes his answer implies he's an arahant. Venerable Nārada steps in to explain for

title: SN 12.69 Upayanti Sutta: Surge
description:
> …the lakes surging cause the pools to surge.
> So too, ignorance surging causes volitional formations to surge…

<!---->

title: SN 13.10 Dutiyapabbata Sutta: The Second Discourse on the Mountains
description:
> ... the suffering that’s over and done with is more, what’s left is tiny.

For someone who has seen the truth (i.e. attained Stream Entry), the suffering eliminated is comparable to the Himalayas; what remains is just seven bits of gravel.

title: SN 14.12 Sanidāna Sutta: With a Cause
description:
> Mendicants, sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts arise for a reason…

<!---->

title: SN 14.15 Caṅkama Sutta: Walking Together
description:
> Mendicants, do you see Sāriputta walking together with several mendicants? ...
All of those mendicants have great wisdom.

Beings come together because of a common element.

title: SN 14.17 Assaddha Saṁsandana Sutta: The Faithless Converge
description: Birds of a feather flock together.

title: SN 15.1 Tiṇakaṭṭha Sutta: Grass and Sticks
description:
> The grass, sticks, branches, and leaves of India would run out before that person’s mothers and grandmothers.

Saṃsāra has been going round for a long, long time.

title: SN 15.9 Daṇḍa Sutta: A Stick
description:
> Suppose a stick was tossed up in the air. Sometimes it’d fall on its bottom, sometimes the middle, and sometimes the end. It’s the same for sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving.

<!---->

title: SN 16.11 Cīvara Sutta: Robes
description: > ‘Your outer robe of patches is soft, Kassapa.’–‘Venerable sir, let the Blessed One accept my outer robe of patches, out of compassion.’–‘Then will you wear my worn-out hempen rag-robes? ’–‘I will, venerable sir.’ Thus I offered the Blessed One my outer robe of patches and received from him his wor

title: SN 16.13 Saddhammappatirūpaka Sutta: The Counterfeit of the True Teaching
description: > Just as, Kassapa, gold does not disappear so long as counterfeit gold has not arisen in the world, but when counterfeit gold arises then true gold disappears, so the true Dhamma does not disappear so long as a counterfeit of the true Dhamma has not arisen in the world, but when a counterfeit of th

title: SN 16.2 Anottappī Sutta: Unafraid of Wrongdoing
description:
> If evil unwholesome states that have arisen in me are not abandoned, this may lead to my harm.

Sāriputta approaches Kassapa and asks how it is that only someone who is keen and conscientious can realize freedom.

title: SN 16.3 Candūpamā Sutta: Like the Moon
description: Kassapa approaches families like the moon, with humility, keeping his distance, and not getting involved. And when he teaches, it is with pure intentions.

title: SN 16.7 Dutiyaovāda Sutta: Advice (2nd)
description:
> When there are no bhikkhus who are exhorters: this is a case of decline.

The Buddha invites Kassapa to teach the mendicants, but he is reluctant, saying that the monks have become stubborn and their good qualities are in decline.

title: SN 17.2 Baḷisa Sutta: The  Hook
description:
> ‘Fisherman’ is a term for Māra the Wicked. ‘Hook’ is a term for possessions, honor, and popularity.

title: SN 17.23 Ekaputtaka Sutta: An Only Son
description:
> A faithful laywoman with a dear and beloved only son would rightly appeal to him: ...

<!---->

title: SN 17.24 Ekadhītu Sutta: An Only Daughter
description:
> A faithful laywoman with a dear and beloved only daughter would rightly appeal to her, ‘My darling, please be like the laywomen Khujjuttarā and [Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother](/content/canon/an7.53).’

Neither laywomen nor nuns should wish for possessions, honor, or fame.

title: SN 17.3 Kumma Sutta: A Turtle
description: The Buddha tells a short fable about a turtle to warn the monks about infatuation with fame.

title: SN 17.37 Mātu Sutta: Mother
description:
> Possessions, honor, and popularity are brutal, bitter, and harsh.

Even someone who would not lie for the sake of their mother could do so when corrupted by material possessions.

title: SN 17.5 Mīḷhaka Sutta: A Dung Beetle
description:
> "Possessions, honor, and popularity are brutal, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary.  
So you should train like this: ‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’

In which the Buddha compares attachment t

title: SN 17.8 Siṅgāla Sutta: A Jackal
description:
> Wherever he goes, stands, sits, or lies down he meets with tragedy 

Obsession with wealth, fame, and honor is like being a jackal with mange.

title: SN 18.21 Anusaya Sutta: Tendency
description:
> One truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: all form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’

Rāhula asks how to see so that conceit no longer occurs. The Buddha

title: SN 19.1 Aṭṭhi Sutta: A Skeleton
description:
> Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a skeleton flying through the air.

While walking for alms down Vulture’s Peak, Venerable Moggallāna smiled at something invisible.
The Buddha confirmed that the man he had seen had been a butcher in his past life.

title: SN 2.20 Anāthapiṇḍika SUtta: With Anāthapiṇḍika
description:
> This is indeed that Jeta’s Grove,  
frequented by the Saṅgha of hermits...

A deity who had been the Buddha’s supporter Anāthapiṇḍika in his former life comes to the Buddha and speaks verses in celebration of the Jeta’s Grove, good deeds, the Dhamma, and Venerable Sāriputta.

title: SN 2.22 Khema Sutta: With Khema
description:
> Witless fools behave  
like their own worst enemies,  
doing wicked deeds...

The deity Khema utters a series of verses in praise of good deeds. The Buddha responds with a simile for someone who departs the path of the good.

title: SN 2.25 Jantu Sutta: With Jantu
description: A deva gently encourages a group of wayward monks.

title: SN 2.26 Rohitassa Sutta: With Rohitassa
description:
> Once upon a time, I was a seer called Rohitassa of the Bhoja people. I was a sky-walker with psychic powers. I was as fast as a light arrow easily shot across the shadow of a palm tree...

> ... there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world. For it is in this fathom-l

title: SN 2.3 Māgha Sutta: With Māgha
description:
> What is the one thing
whose killing you approve?

The god Māgha asks the Buddha about what one should slay in order to sleep well.

title: SN 2.5 Dāmali Sutta: With Dāmali
description:
> someone who has gained a footing  
and stands on dry land  
need not strive

Dāmali the god suggests that a true brahmin must strive to abandon desire. The Buddha disagrees, saying that a true brahmin already has.

title: SN 2.6 Kāmada Sutta: With Kāmada
description:
> … the god Kāmada said to the Buddha, “It’s too hard, Blessed One! It’s just too hard!”

The deity Kāmada addresses the Buddha with a series of cryptic statements lamenting the difficulty of spiritual practice. The Buddha agrees, but points out that true practitioners do it even though it’s hard.

title: SN 2.7 Pañcālacaṇḍa Sutta: In Judgement
description:
> Even in a confining place they find it,  
the Dhamma for the attainment of unbinding.

Pañcālacaṇḍa praises the Buddha for finding the opening amid the confinement of the world. But the Buddha affirms that anyone with mindfulness and stillness may find such an escape.

title: SN 20.4 Okkhā Sutta: Rice Pots
description: Love is more fruitful than generosity.

title: SN 20.5 Satti Sutta: A Spear
description:
> Should any non-human think to overthrow their mind, they’ll eventually get weary and frustrated.

As it is not possible to bend back a spear, it is not possible to overthrow a mendicant who has developed love.

title: SN 20.7 Āṇi Sutta: The Drum Peg
description:
> … in a future time there will be mendicants who won’t want to listen when discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited.

> … that is how the discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—wi

title: SN 20.8 Kaliṅgara Sutta: Wood Blocks
description: Warriors who sleep on wooden pillows remain vigilant, and so it is for a spiritual seeker.

title: SN 21.1 Kolita Sutta: With Kolita
description:
> Here, with the subsiding of thought and examination, a bhikkhu enters and dwells in the second jhana, which has internal confidence and unification of mind, is without thought and examination, and has rapture and happiness born of concentration. This is called noble silence.

Moggallāna reflects t

title: SN 21.10 Theranāmaka Sutta: A Mendicant Named Senior
description:
> It is in such a way, Elder, that dwelling alone is fulfilled...

A monk named “Senior” likes to live alone, but the Buddha questions whether it is true solitude.

title: SN 21.3 Ghaṭa Sutta: The Barrel
description:
> I cleared my divine eye and divine ear element to communicate with the Blessed One.

Moggallāna tells Sāriputta about his day's practice.

title: SN 21.8 Nanda Sutta: With Nanda
description: When the Venerable Nanda wore pretty robes, a fancy bowl, and makeup, the Buddha lamented.

title: SN 22.1 Nakulapitu Sutta: Nakula’s Father
description:
> It was with the ambrosia of such a Dhamma talk, venerable sir, that the Blessed One anointed me.

The householder Nakulapitā asks the Buddha for help in coping with old age. The Buddha says to reflect: “Even though I am afflicted in body, my mind will be unafflicted.” Later Sāriputta explains this

title: SN 22.101 Vāsijaṭa Sutta: The Adze Handle Simile
description: > When, bhikkhus, a carpenter or a carpenter’s apprentice looks at the handle of his adze, he sees the impressions of his fingers and his thumb, but he does not know: ‘So much of the adze handle has been worn away today, so much yesterday, so much earlier.’ But when it has worn away, the knowledge o

title: SN 22.102 Aniccasaññā Sutta: The Perception of Impermanence
description:
> … when the perception of impermanence is developed and cultivated it eliminates all desire for sensual pleasures, for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, and for rebirth in a future life. It eliminates all ignorance and eradicates the conceit ‘I am’

The perception of impermanence eliminates lu

title: SN 22.109 Sotāpanna Sutta: A Stream-Enterer
description: One who truly understand these five aggregates is a stream-enterer.

title: SN 22.122 Sīlavanta Sutta: An Ethical Mendicant
description:
> Reverend Sāriputta, what things should an ethical mendicant properly attend to?

Mahākoṭṭhita asks and Sāriputta replies that if they focus on the aggregates as impermanent, etc. they may become a stream-enterer.

title: SN 22.2 Devadaha Sutta: At Devadaha
description:
> ‘What does your teacher say, what does he teach?’ Being asked thus, friends, you should answer: ‘Our teacher, friends, teaches the removal of desire and lust.’

A number of mendicants are heading for lands West, but the Buddha advises them to speak with Sāriputta before they go. Sāriputta teaches

title: SN 22.22 Bhāra Sutta: The Burden
description:
> And who is the bearer of the burden [of the five aggregates]? The individual (*puggalo*), it should be said;

title: SN 22.26 Assāda Sutta: Gratification
description:
> What is the gratification, what is the danger, what is the escape in the case of feeling … perception … volitional formations … consciousness?

How the Buddha investigated the aggregates.

title: SN 22.29 Abhinandana Sutta: Taking Pleasure
description:
> If you take pleasure in consciousness, you take pleasure in suffering.


<!---->

title: SN 22.36 Dutiya Aññatara Bhikkhu Sutta: A Certain Bhikkhu (2)
description:
> … if one has an underlying tendency towards something, then one is measured in accordance with it; if one is measured in accordance with something, then one is reckoned in terms of it.

The Buddha teaches a pithy discourse to a mendicant who wants to go on retreat.

title: SN 22.46 Dutiya Anicca Sutta: The Second Discourse on Impermanence
description:
> When one holds no more views concerning the past, one holds no more views concerning the future.

How penetrating the aggregates leads to liberation.

description:
> With the fading away of ignorance and the arising of true knowledge, ‘I am’ does not occur to him; ‘I am this’ does not occur to him; ‘I will be’ and ‘I will not be’...

When you identify anything as self, you always identify one or another of the five aggregates.

title: SN 22.48 Khandha Sutta: Aggregates
description: The distinction between “the five aggregates” and “the five grasping aggregates”.

title: SN 22.49 Soṇa Sutta: With Soṇa
description:
> ... what is that due to apart from seeing things as they really are?

The Buddha teaches a householder named Soṇa about the nature of the five aggregates and conceit.

title: SN 22.5 Samādhi Sutta: Concentration
description:
> Bhikkhus, develop concentration. A bhikkhu who is concentrated understands things as they really are.

<!---->

description:
> Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. When relishing ends, greed ends.

Right view is seeing the aggregates as they are: impermanent.

title: SN 22.53 Upaya Sutta: Involvement
description: > Bhikkhus, one who is engaged is unliberated.

description:
> Consciousness together with its nutriment should be seen as like the five kinds of seeds.

They are watered by craving.

title: SN 22.56 Upādāna Paripavatta Sutta: Circling Around Clinging
description:
> ... freed by not grasping: they are well freed. Those who are well freed are consummate ones. For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found.

How the Four Noble Truths illuminate the Five Aggregates.

title: SN 22.57 Sattaṭṭhāna Sutta: Seven Cases
description:
> Here, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu understands form, its origin, its cessation, and the way leading to its cessation; he understands the gratification, the danger, and the escape in the case of form.

To be fully accomplished, a mendicant should investigate these Dhammas.

description:
> This is the difference between a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, and a mendicant freed by wisdom.

title: SN 22.59 Anattalakkhaṇa Sutta: Not Self
description:
> "Is what is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change fit to be regarded thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’?"--"No, venerable sir."

In the Deer Park at Varanasi the Buddha teaches the famous second discourse, on not-self with regard to the aggregates, to the group of five monks.

title: SN 22.60 Mahāli Sutta: With Mahāli
description:
> But because consciousness is painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they are purified. This is a cause and reason for the purification of sentient beings.

title: SN 22.61 Āditta Sutta: Burning
description:
> Experiencing revulsion, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion his mind is liberated.

The five aggregates are burning.

title: SN 22.79 Khajjanīya Sutta: Being Devoured
description:
> And why, bhikkhus, do you call it form? ‘It is deformed,’ bhikkhus, therefore it is called form.

The Buddha explains how to view rebirth.

title: SN 22.80 Piṇḍolya Sutta: Beggars
description:
> Bhikkhus, this is the lowest form of livelihood, that is, gathering alms. In the world this is a term of abuse: ‘You alms-gatherer; you roam about with a begging bowl in your hand!’ And yet, bhikkhus, clansmen intent on the good take up that way of life for a valid reason.

description:
> That perplexity, doubtfulness, indecisiveness in regard to the true Dhamma is a formation. That formation—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born and produced?

A beautiful and deep sutta which gives some insight into how to see—and untangle—Dependent Arising.

description:
> They’d look because of grasping, not by not grasping. In the same way, the notion “I am” occurs because of grasping form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness, not by not grasping.

Ānanda praises Venerable Puṇṇa Mantāniputta, and says that it was when hearing his teaching on the aggreg

title: SN 22.84 Tissa Sutta: With Tissa
description:
> That’s how it is for one who is not without passion for fabrications.

The Venerable Tissa is roused by an interview with the Buddha.

title: SN 22.85 Yamaka Sutta: With Yamaka
description:
> Now on that occasion the following pernicious view had arisen in a bhikkhu named Yamaka: “As I understand the Dhamma taught by the Blessed One, a bhikkhu whose taints are destroyed is annihilated and perishes with the breakup of the body and does not exist after death.”

Convinced by the Venerable

description:
When Venerable Vakkali is ill, he asks the Buddha to visit him. The Buddha does so, but says there is no point in seeing his physical body, as [one who sees the Dhamma sees him](/content/canon/iti92).

In a dramatic continuation of the story, Ven. Vakkali is then taken to the Black Rock on Isigili,

title: SN 22.89 Khemaka Sutta: With Khemaka
description:
> Friends, even though a noble disciple has abandoned the five lower fetters, still, in relation to the five aggregates subject to clinging, there lingers in him a residual conceit ‘I am,’ a desire ‘I am,’ an underlying tendency ‘I am’ that has not yet been uprooted.

Venerable Khemaka is ill, and s

title: SN 22.93 Nadī Sutta: A River
description:
> And if a person who was being swept along by the current grabbed the wild sugarcane, kusa grass, reeds, vetiver, or trees, it’d break off, and they’d come to ruin because of that.

If you grasp at the aggregates as a self, you will meet with calamity, like a man swept down by a mountain river.

title: SN 22.94 Puppha Sutta: Flowers
description:
> I do not dispute with the world; rather, it is the world that disputes with me.

The Buddha explains that he doesn't teach that nothing exists.

title: SN 22.95 Pheṇapiṇḍūpama Sutta: A Lump of Foam

To him it would appear empty, void, without substance: for what substance could there be in a bubble? In the same way, a man with wi

title: SN 22.95 Pheṇapiṇḍūpama Sutta: A Lump of Foam
description: > Now suppose that in the autumn---when it's raining in fat, heavy drops---a water bubble were to appear & disappear on the water, and a man with sight were to see it. To him it would appear empty, void, without substance: for what substance could there be in a bubble? In the same way, a man with wi

title: SN 23.1 Māra Sutta: About Māra
description:
> How is Māra defined?

> Those who see it like this see rightly.

description:
> How is a sentient being defined?

Rādha asks the Buddha, who compares craving and rebirth to a child playing with sandcastles.

description:
> Form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. These are called the things that should be completely understood.

Even the Stream Enterer and Anāgāmīn understanding of the Aggregates is provisional.
Only the Araha

description:
> Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ‘The one who acts does nothing wrong...’

Clinging to different aggregates yields very different philosophies, but all trend towards moral nihilism.

title: SN 25.1 Cakkhu Sutta: The Eye
description:
> ... body and mind are impermanent, decaying, and perishing.
Someone who has faith and confidence in these teachings is called a follower by faith.

> They can’t die without realizing the fruit of stream-entry.

The Buddha defines the two types of "little stream winners": the faith follower and the

title: SN 27.8 Taṇhā Sutta: Craving
description: What are the different types of craving?

description: How to be reborn as a Nāga.

title: SN 3.1 Dahara Sutta: Young
description:
> A prince or princess in the royal family, a snake, a fire, and a monk. These four things should not be looked down on or belittled because they are young.

King Pasenadi meets the Buddha for the first time. He wonders how the Buddha can claim to be awakened when he is still so young. The Buddha te

title: SN 3.11 Sattajaṭila Sutta: Seven Matted-Hair Ascetics
description:
> You can get to know a person’s ethics by living with them. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not by the witless.

A diverse group of ascetics passes by, and Pasenadi asks the Buddha if any of them are perfected.

title: SN 3.17 Appamāda Sutta: Diligence
description:
> The astute praise diligence in making merit.

Pasenadi asks the Buddha if there is one thing that secures benefit both in this life and the next.

description:
> When you live with good friends, good companions, and good associates, you should live supported by one thing: diligence in wholesome qualities.

description:
> … people collected it and drank it and bathed in it and used it for their own purpose. Since that water was properly utilized, it’s used, not wasted.

In which the Buddha encourages us to take advantage of the abundance we've received.

title: SN 3.2 Purisa Sutta: A Person
description:
> … as a reed is destroyed by its own fruit.

Pasenadi asks of the things that cause suffering when they arise from within.

description: A wealthy man dies childless, having not enjoyed his riches. The Buddha tells what past karma led tohis present life.

title: SN 3.22 Ayyakā Sutta: Grandmother
description:
> … all beings are subject to death. Death is their end

Pasenadi laments the death of his aged grandmother.

title: SN 3.24 Issatta Sutta: Archery
description:
> Wherever the mind feels confidence, great king.

“Where should I give” and “Where is a gift very fruitful” are two different questions.

title: SN 3.25 Pabbatūpama Sutta: The Mountains Simile
description:
> Suppose a trustworthy and reliable man were to come from the east. He’d approach you and say: ‘Please sir, you should know this. I come from the east. There I saw a huge mountain that reached the clouds. And it was coming this way, crushing all creatures.’

Old age and death roll in upon all like

title: SN 3.7 Atthakarana Sutta: In Judgement
description: King Pasenadi realizes how silly it is, the things that make people lie.

title: SN 33.4 Saṅkhāraaññāṇa Sutta: Not Knowing Choices
description:
> … what is the reason why these various misconceptions arise in the world?

The wanderer Vacchagotta asks the Buddha why the various speculative views come to be. The Buddha replies that it is because of not knowing activity.

title: SN 34.2 Samādhi Mūla Kaṭhiti Sutta: Remaining in Immersion
description: > The meditator skilled in immersion and in remaining in it is the foremost...

title: SN 35.1 Ajjhattānicca Sutta: The Interior is Impermanent
description: The six sense fields are impermanent, suffering, and not-self.

title: SN 35.100 Paṭisallāna Sutta: Retreat
description:
> Mendicants, meditate in retreat. A mendicant in retreat truly understands.

<!---->

title: SN 35.101 Paṭhama Natumhāka Sutta: The First Discourse on What’s Not Yours
description: Viewing the six senses as *anattā* leads to peace.

title: SN 35.108 Seyyohamasmi Sutta: I’m Better
description:
> Not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would people think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’?

Conceit stems from clinging to the senses and their impressions.

title: SN 35.116 Lokantagamana Sutta: Traveling to the End of the World
description:
> I say it's not possible to know, see or reach the end of the world by traveling. But I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world.

The mendicants ask Ānanda to explain this enigmatic statement derived from [the famous story of Rohitassa](/content/canon/sn

title: SN 35.120 Sāriputta Saddhi Vihārika Sutta: Sāriputta and the Pupil
description:
A mendicant informs Sāriputta that one of his friends had disrobed.
Sāriputta attributes this to a lack of sense restraint, eating too much, and not being wakeful.
He then explains the meaning of sense restraint, moderation in eating, and the devotion to wakefulness.

title: SN 35.131 Nakulapitu Sutta: Nakula’s Father
description:
> What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings aren’t fully extinguished in the present life?

> If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, their consciousness relies on that and grasps it. A mendicant with grasping does not become extinguished.

<!---->

title: SN 35.135 Khaṇa Sutta: Opportunity
description:
> I have seen, bhikkhus, the hell named ‘Contact’s Sixfold Base.’

<!---->

title: SN 35.136 Rūpārāma Sutta: Delight in Forms
description:
> What others say is happiness  
the noble ones say is suffering.  
What others say is suffering  
the noble ones know as happiness.

> … consciousness exists dependent on duality

title: SN 35.145 Bāhirānattahetu Sutta: Exterior and Cause Are Not-Self
description:
> Since thoughts are produced by what is not-self, how could they be self?

<!---->

title: SN 35.146 Kamma Nirodha Sutta: The Cessation of Karma
description:
> What, bhikkhus, is old kamma? The eye is old kamma, to be seen as generated and fashioned by volition...

<!---->

title: SN 35.155 Dhamma Kathikapuccha Sutta: A Dhamma Speaker
description:
> Bhikkhu, if one teaches the Dhamma for the purpose of revulsion towards the eye, for its fading away and cessation, one can be called a bhikkhu who is a speaker on the Dhamma.

title: SN 35.17 Paṭhama Noce Assāda Sutta: The First Discourse on No Gratification Inside
description: Beings are attached to the six sense fields due to gratification, repelled due to drawbacks, and find escape because there is one.

description:

title: SN 35.21 Paṭhama Dukkhuppāda Sutta: The First Discource on the Arising of Suffering

title: SN 35.228 Paṭhamasamudda Sutta: The Ocean (1)
description: The Buddha says that the real ocean is the eye, full of sights crashing into us.

title: SN 35.23 Sabba Sutta: The All

> If anyone, bhikkhus, should speak thus: ‘Having rejected this all, I shall make known another all’—that would be a mere empty boast on his part.

The Buddha makes clear that the senses are really "all" there is.

title: SN 35.230 Bāḷisikopama Sutta: The Fisherman Simile
description:
> If a bhikkhu seeks delight in [the senses], welcomes them, and remains holding to them, he is called a bhikkhu who has swallowed Mara’s hook. He has met with calamity and disaster, and the Evil One can do with him as he wishes.

Sense pleasures are like a baited hook.

title: SN 35.231 Khīrarukkhopama Sutta: The Simile of the Latex-Producing Tree
description:
> ... even trifling forms that enter into range of the eye obsess the mind, not to speak of those that are prominent.
For what reason? Because lust still exists

Like a tree that yields sap when cut, so long as desire is present it can be activated by the senses.

title: SN 35.232 Koṭṭhika Sutta: With Koṭṭhita
description:
> There exists in the Blessed One the eye, the Blessed One sees a form with the eye, yet there is no desire and lust in the Blessed One; the Blessed One is well liberated in mind.

> Is the eye the fetter of forms or are forms the fetter of the eye?
