title: SN 35.238 Āsīvisopama Sutta: The Simile of the Vipers
description: Gives some vivid imagery to illustrate the Buddhist outlook on life. While explicitly couched as similes, the images in this sutta demonstrate that even the earliest texts were no strangers to literary style.

title: SN 35.239 Rathopama Sutta: The Simile of the Chariot
description: Explains the three primary duties of a monk: guarding the senses, moderation in eating, and the devotion to wakefulness.

title: SN 35.24 Pahāna Sutta: Abandonment
description: > the Dhamma for abandoning all

title: SN 35.240 Kummopama Sutta: The Simile of the Tortoise
description:
> A mendicant should collect their thoughts  
as a tortoise draws its limbs into its shell.

A jackal who fails to eat a turtle.

title: SN 35.241 Paṭhama Dārukkhandhopama Sutta: The First Simile of the Tree Trunk
description: > If, bhikkhus, that log does not veer towards the near shore, does not veer towards the far shore, does not sink in mid-stream, does not get cast up on high ground, does not get caught by human beings, does not get caught by nonhuman beings, does not get caught in a whirlpool, and does not become i

title: SN 35.243 Avassutapariyāya Sutta: The Explanation on the Corrupt
description:
> When a bhikkhu dwells thus, he overwhelms forms; forms do not overwhelm him. He overwhelms sounds; sounds do not overwhelm him...

The Buddha is invited to teach in a new hall in Kapilavatthu. Late at night, after teaching the Sakyans, the Buddha invites Moggallāna to teach the monks, so he explai

title: SN 35.244 Dukkha Dhamma Sutta: Entailing Suffering
description:
> if occasionally, due to a lapse of mindfulness, evil unwholesome memories and intentions connected with the fetters arise in him, slow might be the arising of his mindfulness, but then he quickly abandons them, dispels them, puts an end to them

The Buddha urges mendicants to be free of desire for

title: SN 35.245 Kiṁsukopama Sutta: The Simile of the Parrot Tree
course: imagery # also in Hecker... maybe move out?
description:
> Suppose a person was to catch six animals, with diverse territories and feeding grounds, and tie them up with a strong rope.

A mendicant goes to a series of teachers and asks how vision is purified. Dissatisfied with all their answers, he complains to the Buddha, who illustrates his quandary with

title: SN 35.246 Vīṇopama Sutta: The Simile of the Lute
description:
> One should rein in the mind thus

One should restrain the senses like a farmer watching over a field. The Buddha gives the parable of a man bewitched when he first hears a lute. He takes apart the instrument in search of the sound, but is disillusioned when no sound is found.

title: SN 35.247 Chappāṇakopama Sutta: Six Animals
description:
> Suppose a person was to catch six animals, with diverse territories and feeding grounds, and tie them up with a strong rope...

The senses are like a snake, a crocodile, a bird, a dog, a jackal, and a monkey all tied up together, pulling this way and that. Mindfulness is like a post that keeps the

title: SN 35.248 Yavakalāpi Sutta: The Sheaf of Barley
description:
> dwell with a mind in which conceit has been struck down

The six senses are like a sheaf of barley struck with six flails; and the desire for rebirth is a seventh. The Buddha goes on to speak of a cunning trap set by the gods; but the trap of Māra is even more subtle still.

title: SN 35.28 Adittapariyaya Sutta: The Fire Sermon
description:
> Monks! All is aflame!

The “all” consisting of the six interior and exterior sense fields, that is. This is the famous “third sermon” taught at Gayā’s Head to the followers of the three Kassapa brothers.

title: SN 35.63 Paṭhama Migajāla Sutta: The First Discourse With Migajāla
tags: renunciation # probably move here when it exists
description:
> A monk disjoined from the fetter of delight is said to be a person who is living alone.

The Buddha defines "living alone" as living detached from relishing the six senses and their objects.

title: SN 35.65 Paṭhama Samiddhi Māra Pañhā Sutta: Samiddhi’s First Question About Māra
description: Venerable Samiddhi asks the Buddha what Māra is.

title: SN 35.7 Ajjhattāniccātītānāgata Sutta: The Interior as Impermanent in the Three Times
description: > Mendicants, the eye of the past and future is impermanent, let alone the present.

title: SN 35.80 Dutiya Avijjā Pahāna Sutta: The Second Discourse on Abandoning Ignorance
description:
> All dhammas are unworthy of attachment.

> He sees forms as something separate.
He sees eye-consciousness as something separate.
He sees eye-contact as something separate.
And whatever arises in dependence on eye-contact—experienced either as pleasure, as pain, or as neither-pleasure-nor-pain—that

title: SN 35.82 Loka Sutta: The World
description:
> Insofar as it disintegrates, it is called the 'world.'

<!---->

title: SN 35.83 Phaggunapañhā Sutta: Phagguna’s Question
description:
> There is no eye, Phagguna, by means of which one describing the Buddhas of the past could describe them...

Worth keeping in mind that the suttas (like Iti 61) enumerate three kinds of eye: "The flesh eye, the divine eye, and the eye of wisdom."
The Buddha here says that not even the heavenly eye

title: SN 35.85 Suññata Loka Sutta: Empty is the World
description:
> It is, Ānanda, because it is empty of self and of what belongs to self that it is said, ‘Empty is the world.’

<!---->

title: SN 35.88 Puṇṇa Sutta: With Puṇṇa
description:
> The people of Sunāparanta are wild and rough, Puṇṇa. If they abuse and insult you, what will you think of them?

Venerable Puṇṇa goes to the Buddha and asks for a teaching before he immigrates to a foreign land. The Buddha warns him that folk there are fierce, and questions whether he is ready for

title: SN 35.90 Paṭhamaejā Sutta: The First Discourse on Turbulence
description:
> He should not conceive [I am] the all, should not conceive [I am] in all, should not conceive [I come] from the all, should not conceive, ‘All is mine.’

Being stirred by craving is painful, so the Realized One lives unstirred, not identifying with any aspect of sense experience.

title: SN 35.93 Dutiyadvayasutta: The Second Discourse on Duality
description:
> … consciousness exists dependent on duality

Consciousness arises from the dyad of the interior sense organ with its corresponding exterior sense stimulus. Both are conditioned, impermanent, and falling apart.

title: SN 35.94 Adanta Agutta Sutta: Untamed, Unguarded
description:
> Bhikkhus, these six bases for contact—if untamed, unguarded, unprotected, unrestrained—are bringers of suffering.

A series of verses encouraging us to guard well our senses.

title: SN 35.95 Māluṅkyaputta Suttaṁ: To Māluṅkyaputta
description:
> For one reducing suffering like this *nibbāna* is said to be near.

Venerable Māluṅkyaputta asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha wonders how to teach an old monk like him, then questions him on his desire for sense experiences that have been or might be, and encourages him to simply

title: SN 36.11 Rahogata Sutta: Alone
description: One somewhat confusing point of Buddhist philosophy is that the three feelings (painful, neutral *and pleasant*) are all included under “*dukkha*.” Thankfully for us, a monk at the time of the Buddha decided to ask him about it.

title: SN 36.19 Pañcakaṅga Sutta: Pañcakaṅga
description:
> Though some may say, ‘[Sensual pleasure] is the supreme pleasure and joy that beings experience,’ I would not concede this to them. Why is that? Because there is another kind of happiness more excellent and sublime than that

The carpenter Pañcakaṅga disagreed with Venerable Udāyī about how many k

title: SN 36.21 Sīvaka Sutta: With Sīvaka
description:
In this controversial sutta, the Buddha declares that everything an individual experiences is **not** necessarily the result of past karma.

See also [AN 5.197](/content/canon/an5.197) for a discussion on the causes of the weather!

title: SN 36.23 The Aññatarabhikkhu Sutta: A Certain Bhikkhu
description:
> … what now is feeling?

A mendicant asks the Buddha to explain how feelings relate to the four noble truths.

title: SN 36.31 Nirāmisa Sutta: Spiritual
description:
> There is carnal happiness, there is spiritual happiness, and there is happiness more spiritual than the spiritual.

<!---->

title: SN 36.6 Salla Sutta: The Dart
description: This famous simile compares physical pain and mental anguish to two arrows: the second of which is optional.

title: SN 38.1 Nibbāna Pañhā Sutta: A Question About *Nibbāna*
description: The basic definition of *nibbāna*.

title: SN 38.2 Arahatta Pañhā Sutta: A Question About Perfection
description: Sāriputta defines perfection.

title: SN 39.16 Dukkara Sutta: Hard to Do
description: The wanderer Sāmaṇḍaka asks Sāriputta what is difficult to do.

title: SN 4.1 Tapokamma Sutta: Austere Practice
description: Māra accuses the Buddha of having abandoned the path of true austerity.

title: SN 4.13 Sakalika Sutta: The Splinter
description:
> The nights and days do not afflict me,  
I see for myself no decline in the world.

The Buddha rests after being struck by stone splinters, and though Māra criticizes him for being lazy, the Buddha rests easy.

title: SN 4.16 Patta Sutta: The Alms Bowls
description:
> Mara the Evil One manifested himself in the form of an ox and approached those almsbowls.

Which is more valuable? An almsbowl, or a teaching from the Buddha?

title: SN 4.18 Piṇḍa Sutta: Alms Food
description: Māra ensures that the Buddha fails to get alms, but the Buddha is happy either way.

title: SN 4.19 Kassaka Sutta: The Farmer
description:
> The eye is yours, Evil One, forms are yours, eye-contact and its base of consciousness are yours; but, Evil One, where there is no eye, no forms, no eye-contact and its base of consciousness—there is no place for you there

While the mendicants are listening to the teachings, Māra takes the form o

title: SN 4.20 Rajja Sutta: Ruling
description:
> Take a golden mountain,  
made entirely of gold, and double it—  
it’s still not enough for one!

The Buddha wonders whether it is possible to rule justly, without violence. Māra appears and encourages the Buddha to try it.

title: SN 4.22 Samiddhi Sutta: With Samiddhi
description:
> That was not the earth splitting open, Samiddhi. That was Mara...

When Māra repeatedly pesters the monk Samiddhi when he is on retreat, the Buddha encourages him to press on in his practice.

title: SN 4.24 Satta Vassānubandha Sutta: Seven Years of Pursuit
description:
> Then Mara the Evil One, in the presence of the Blessed One, recited these verses of disappointment...

He laments his failure with the similes of a crab whose limbs are smashed and a crow who tried to eat a stone.

title: SN 4.25 Māradhītu Sutta: Māra’s Daughters
description:
> They had come to him glittering with beauty—  
Taṇha, Arati, and Raga—  
But the Teacher swept them away right there  
As the wind, a fallen cotton tuft.

Depressed, Māra laments to his three daughters of his failure to distract the Buddha.
So they take on the task themselves and assume a variety

title: SN 4.6 Sappa Sutta: A Serpent
description:
> Though many creatures crawl about,  
Many terrors, flies, serpents,  
The great sage gone to his empty hut  
Stirs not a hair because of them.

Māra manifests as a huge serpent, but the Buddha remains unshaken.

title: SN 4.9 Paṭhama Āyu Sutta: The First Discourse on the Life Span
description:
> Live like a suckling babe,  
for Death has not come for you.

The Buddha says that life is short.

title: SN 41.10 Gilānadassana Sutta: Seeing the Sick
description: When Citta was on his deathbed, rather than receiving comfort, he gave comfort and teaching to those present: human and divine.

title: SN 41.3 Dutiyaisidatta Sutta: The Second Sutta with Isidatta
description:
> Allow me, venerable sir, to answer Citta the householder’s question.

Discussion questions:
1. What does this sutta reveal about (Theravāda) monastic etiquette?
2. Why do you think the sutta ends the way it does?

title: SN 41.4 Mahakapāṭihāriya Sutta: Mahaka's Demonstration
description: Citta the householder invites some mendicants to his home for a meal. When they left he followed them, and witnessed the junior monk Venerable Mahaka performing a psychic feat.

title: SN 41.5 Paṭhamakāmabhū Sutta: With Kāmabhū
description: Kāmabhū asks Citta the Householder to explain an enigmatic, symbolic poem spoken by the Buddha.

title: SN 41.7 Godatta Sutta: With Godatta
description: > Householder, the limitless release of the heart, and the release of the heart through nothingness, and the release of the heart through emptiness, and the signless release of the heart: do these things differ in both meaning and phrasing? Or do they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phra

title: SN 41.8 Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta Sutta: The Jain Ascetic of the Ñātika Clan
description: The Jain leader Mahāvīra, known as Nigaṇṭha Nātaputa in the Buddhist tradition, is visited by Citta the Householder. Mahāvīra asks him whether he believes in a state of immersion free from thought. When Citta replies that he doesn’t, Mahāvīra is (prematurely) delighted. Citta goes on to explain that

title: SN 42.13 Pāṭaliya Sutta: With Pāṭaliya
description:
> Mister, that man attacked the king’s enemy and killed them. The king was delighted and gave him this reward.

The Buddha clears a layman's doubts and confusion about the correct way to understand the law of karma.

Also includes a fascinating description of the Koliyan police — apparently known fo

title: SN 42.6 Asibandhaka Putta Sutta: With Asibandhaka’s Son
description:
> What do you think, chief? Could a broad rock rise up or float because of prayers?

The Buddha excoriates a chief for believing that prayers can send someone to heaven.

title: SN 42.7 Khettūpama Sutta: Simile of the Field
description:
> "Why, exactly, do you teach some people thoroughly and others less thoroughly?"

The chief Asibandhakaputta asks the Buddha why, if he has equal compassion for all, he teaches some more than others. The Buddha answers with a simile of a field: a farmer knows to put most of their effort into the fe

title: SN 42.8 Saṅkha Dhama Sutta: A Horn Blower
description:
> Take a person who kills living creatures.
If we compare periods of time during the day and night, which is more frequent: the occasions when they’re killing or when they’re not killing?

The Buddha rejects the poorly phrased fatalism of a Jain follower and gives an alternative method for overcomin

title: SN 42.9 Kula Sutta: Families
description:
> I recollect ninety eons back but I’m not aware of any family that’s been ruined merely by offering some cooked almsfood.

Mahāvīra asks Asibandhakaputta to refute the Buddha on behalf of the Jains. He suggests to try to trap the Buddha with a dilemma: he claims to have compassion for householders,

title: SN 43: Connected Discourses on the Unconditioned
course: nibbana # duplicate with *Island* but I'll allow it
description: The Buddha defines *nibbāna* and gives 44 synonyms for it.

title: SN 44.1 Khemā Sutta: With Khemā
description:
> The Tathagata, great king, is liberated from reckoning in terms of consciousness; he is deep, immeasurable, hard to fathom like the great ocean.

While staying in Toraṇavatthu, King Pasenadi wishes to visit a spiritual teacher, and the nun Khemā is highly recommended to him.
He asks her about whet

title: SN 44.10 Ānanda Sutta: With Ānanda
description: The Buddha refuses to say that there is no self.

title: SN 44.2 Anurādha Sutta: With Anurādha
description:
> Formerly, Anurādha, and also now, I teach just suffering and the cessation of suffering.

Venerable Anurādha is questioned by a number of ascetics, and ends up by saying that the Realized One is described in terms other than “existing after death” and so on. The wanderers say he’s a fool, so he ch

title: SN 44.9 Kutūhalasālā Sutta: The Debating Hall
description:
> I declare, Vaccha, rebirth for one with fuel, not for one without fuel.

The Buddha explains his position on rebirth, including how the state between rebirths is possible.

title: SN 45.139 Tathāgata Sutta: The Realized One
description:
> ... the Perfectly Enlightened One is declared to be the chief among them. So too, whatever wholesome states there are, they are all rooted in diligence

This is the first sutta in a repetition series  continuing with:
  - [SN 45.140](/content/canon/sn45.140)
  - [SN 45.141–5](/content/canon/sn45.1

title: SN 45.14 Paṭhamauppāda Sutta: Arising (1st)
description:
> These eight things don’t arise to be developed and cultivated except when a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has appeared.

An important sutta in which the Buddha reiterates the uniqueness of his discovery.

title: SN 45.140 Pada Sutta: Footprints
description:
> ... the elephant’s footprint is declared to be the chief among them, that is, with respect to size, so too whatever wholesome states there are, they are all rooted in diligence...

See [the previous sutta](/content/canon/sn45.139) for how to expand the ellipses in this one,
and see [the next sutta

title: SN 45.141-145 Kūṭādi Sutta: A Roof Peak
description:
> Of all kinds of fragrant flower, jasmine is said to be the best...

See [the previous sutta](/content/canon/sn45.140) and [the one before that](/content/canon/sn45.139) for how to expand the ellipses in this text, and see [the next sutta](/content/canon/sn45.146-148) for the continuation of this r

title: SN 45.146-148 Candimādi Sutta: The Moon
description:
> ... in the autumn, when the sky is clear and cloudless, the sun, ascending in the sky, dispels all darkness from space as it shines and beams...

See [the first sutta in this repetition series](/content/canon/sn45.139) for how to expand the ellipses in this sutta.

title: SN 45.149 Bala Sutta: Hard Work
description: > And how, bhikkhus, does a bhikkhu, based upon virtue, established upon virtue, develop and cultivate the Noble Eightfold Path?

title: SN 45.150 Bīja Sutta: Seeds
description: > … based upon virtue, established upon virtue, a bhikkhu develops and cultivates the Noble Eightfold Path

title: SN 45.151 Nāga Sutta: Dragons
description: > Bhikkhus, based upon the Himalayas, the king of mountains, the nagas nurture their bodies and acquire strength.

title: SN 45.152 Rukkha Sutta: Trees
description: > A bhikkhu who develops and cultivates the Noble Eightfold Path slants, slopes, and inclines towards Nibbāna.

title: SN 45.153 Kumbha Sutta: Pots
description: > Bhikkhus, just as a pot that has been turned upside down gives up its water and does not take it back...

title: SN 45.154 Sūka Sutta: A Spike
description: > So too, bhikkhus, that a bhikkhu with a rightly directed view, with a rightly directed development of the path, could pierce ignorance...

title: SN 45.155 Ākāsa Sutta: The Sky
description: > …the seven factors of enlightenment go to fulfilment by development.

title: SN 45.156 Paṭhamamegha Sutta: Storms (1st)
description: > ...when a bhikkhu develops and cultivates the Noble Eightfold Path, whenever evil unwholesome states arise, he disperses them and quells them on the spot.

title: SN 45.157 Dutiyamegha Sutta: Storms (2nd)
description: > ... when a bhikkhu develops and cultivates the Noble Eightfold Path, whenever evil unwholesome states have arisen, he intercedes to disperse and quell them.

title: SN 45.158 Nāvā Sutta: A Ship
description: > When a bhikkhu develops and cultivates the Noble Eightfold Path, his fetters easily collapse and rot away.

title: SN 45.159 Āgantuka Sutta: A Guest House
description:
> Bhikkhus, suppose there is a guest house.
People come from the east, west, north, and south and lodge there;
khattiyas, brahmins, vessas...

Compare and contrast this sutta with [the famous Rumi poem (translated by Coleman Barks) of the same title](https://www.scottishpoetrylibrary.org.uk/poem/gue

title: SN 45.160 Nadī Sutta: A River
description: > … when that bhikkhu is developing and cultivating the Noble Eightfold Path, it is impossible that he will give up the training and return to the lower life.

title: SN 45.161 Esanā Sutta: Searches
description: > Mendicants, there are these three searches. What three? The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a holy life.

title: SN 45.2 Upaḍḍha Sutta: Half the Spiritual Life
description:
Good, spiritual friendship is the whole of the holy life.

See [SN 45.49](/content/canon/sn45.49) for _how_ to use a good friend to advance on the path.

title: SN 45.24 Dutiya Paṭipadā Sutta: The Second Discourse on the Way
description:
> Bhikkhus, whether for a layperson or one gone forth, I praise the right way.

The wrong eightfold path is the wrong way; the right eightfold path is the right way.

title: SN 45.27 Kumbha Sutta: Pots
description:
> What is the mind’s stand?

Someone without the eightfold path is easily knocked over, like a pot without a stand.

title: SN 45.34 Pāraṅgama Sutta: Going to the Far Shore
description:
> through nonclinging find delight  
In the relinquishment of grasping

The eightfold path leads from the near to the far shore.

title: SN 45.35 Paṭhamasāmañña Sutta: The Ascetic Life
description:
> … the ascetic life and the fruits of the ascetic life

The eightfold path is the ascetic life. Its fruits are stream-entry, once-return, non-return, and perfection.

title: SN 45.4 Jāṇussoṇi Brāhmaṇa Sutta: The Brahmin Jānussoṇi
description:
> This divine vehicle unsurpassed  
Originates from within oneself.  
The wise depart from the world in it,  
Inevitably winning the victory.

Ānanda sees the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi resplendent on his all-white chariot. He asks the Buddha whether there is a similarly divine vehicle in Buddhism.

title: SN 45.49 Kalyāṇamitta Sutta: Good Friends
description:
> And how does a bhikkhu who has a good friend develop and cultivate the Noble Eightfold Path?

<!---->

title: SN 45.5 Kimatthiya Sutta: For What Purpose
description:
> For what purpose, friends, is the holy life lived under the ascetic Gotama?

<!---->

title: SN 45.8 Vibhaṅga Sutta: Analysis
description:
The Canonical definition of the Noble Eightfold Path.

For an even more detailed analysis, see [MN 117](/content/canon/mn117).

title: SN 45.9 Sūka Sutta: A Spike
description:
> … a bhikkhu develops right view, which is based upon seclusion, dispassion, and cessation, maturing in release

On the kind of right view necessary to attain Nibbāna.

title: SN 46.1 Himavanta Sutta: The Himalaya
description:
> ... based upon virtue, established upon virtue, a bhikkhu develops and cultivates the seven factors of enlightenment...

Dragons nurture their strength in the Himalayas, then enter the rivers and reach the ocean. So too, a mendicant nurtures ethics and then develops the seven awakening factors to

title: SN 46.11 Pāṇa Sutta: Living Beings
description: Just as living creatures are based on the earth, the awakening factors are based on ethics.

title: SN 46.16 Tatiya Gilāna Sutta: The Third Discourse on Illness
description: When the Buddha was sick, Mahācunda recited for him the awakening factors.

title: SN 46.2 Kāya Sutta: The Body
description:
> ...the sign of the beautiful: frequently giving careless attention to it is the nutriment for the arising of unarisen sensual desire...

Just as the body depends on food, the hindrances and the awakening factors feed on specific nutriments.
In this sutta, the Buddha gives the specific condition fo

title: SN 46.24 Ayonisomanasikāra Sutta: Careless Attention
description: A sutta on how *samādhi* is squandered by unwise attention.

title: SN 46.29 Ekadhamma Sutta: One Thing
description:
> Bhikkhus, I do not see even one other thing that, when developed and cultivated, leads to the abandoning of the things that fetter so effectively as this

What fetters one? And what leads to release?

title: SN 46.3 Sīla Sutta: Ethics
description:
> Those bhikkhus who are accomplished in virtue, accomplished in concentration, accomplished in wisdom, accomplished in liberation, accomplished in the knowledge and vision of liberation: even the sight of those bhikkhus is helpful

A description of the path, from hearing the good teachings up to en

title: SN 46.33 Upakkilesa Sutta: Corruptions
description:
> Iron is a corruption of gold, corrupted by which gold is neither malleable nor wieldy nor radiant but brittle and not properly fit for work.

Hindrances sully the mind like impurities in gold.

title: SN 46.39 Rukkha Sutta: Trees
description:
> Bhikkhus, there are huge trees with tiny seeds and huge bodies, encirclers of other trees, and the trees which they encircle become bent, twisted, and split.

> ... when some clansman here has left behind sensual pleasures and gone forth from the household life into homelessness, he becomes bent,

title: SN 46.51 Āhārasutta Sutta: Nourishing
description:
> … what fuels and what starves the five hindrances and the seven awakening factors

<!---->

title: SN 46.53 Aggi Sutta: Fire
description: > Bhikkhus, when the mind becomes excited, it is timely to develop the enlightenment factor of tranquillity...

title: SN 46.54 Mettāsahagata Sutta: Accompanied by Lovingkindness
description:
> … how is the liberation of the mind by lovingkindness developed? What does it have as its destination, its culmination, its fruit, its final goal?

Some wanderers tell some Buddhist mendicants that they, too, teach the five hindrances and the four Brahmā meditations, so what is the difference? The

title: SN 46.55 Saṅgārava Sutta: Saṅgarava
description: The Buddha compares the five hindrances to a bowl of water in various conditions.

title: SN 46.6 Kuṇḍaliya Sutta: Kuṇḍaliya
description:
> Restraint of the sense faculties, Kuṇḍaliya, when developed and cultivated, fulfils the three kinds of good conduct.

On the pivotal role of sense restraint in establishing both virtuous conduct and mindfulness.

title: SN 47.10 Bhikkhunupassaya Sutta: The Nuns’ Quarters
description: When Ānanda visits the nuns’s quarters they tell him that their meditation is prospering to higher and higher levels. Ānanda reports the good news to the Buddha, who speaks of two ways of developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation: directed and undirected.

title: SN 47.12 Nālanda Sutta: At Nāḷandā
description: > Whatever Arahants, Perfectly Enlightened Ones arose in the past, all those Blessed Ones had first abandoned the five hindrances, corruptions of the mind and weakeners of wisdom; and then, with their minds well established in the four establishments of mindfulness, they had developed correctly the

title: SN 47.13 Cunda Sutta: Cunda
description:
> … dwell with yourselves as your own island, with yourselves as your own refuge

For the conclusion, read the very next sutta: [SN 47.14](/content/canon/sn47.14).

title: SN 47.14 Ukkacelā Sutta: At Ukkacelā
description:
> … mendicants, live as your own island, your own refuge, with no other refuge. Let the teaching be your island and your refuge

After the passing of Sāriputta and Moggallāna (whose actual death is unrecorded in the canon), the Buddha says the Saṅgha looks empty; yet he is not sad.

title: SN 47.15 Bāhiya Sutta: With Bāhiya
description: When Venerable Bāhiya asks for a teaching to take on retreat, the Buddha teaches the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, well grounded on ethics.

title: SN 47.18 Brahma Sutta: With Brahmā
description:
> The compassionate one, who sees the ending of rebirth,  
understands the one-way path.

Just after the Buddha’s awakening, Brahmā Sahampati supports the Buddha’s reflection that the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are the way to nibbāna.

title: SN 47.19 Sedaka Sutta: The Acrobat Simile
description: > Protecting oneself, bhikkhus, one protects others; protecting others, one protects oneself. For an audio recording of this sutta, see [this entry](/content/canon/sn47.19_candasiri).

title: SN 47.19 Sedaka Sutta: The Acrobat Simile (recording)
description:
> Protecting oneself, bhikkhus, one protects others; protecting others, one protects oneself.

A reading of [SN 47.19](/content/canon/sn47.19).

title: SN 47.20 Janapada Kalyāṇī Sutta: The Most Beautiful Girl of the Land
description:
> You must carry around this bowl of oil filled to the brim between the crowd and the most beautiful girl of the land. A man with a drawn sword will be following right behind you, and wherever you spill even a little of it, right there he will fell your head.

Now that’s mindfulness!

title: SN 47.3 Bhikkhu Sutta: A Bhikkhu
description: The Buddha summarizes the path as the four foundations of mindfulness based on virtue.

title: SN 47.31 Ananussuta Sutta: Unheard Before
description: The Buddha explains how his unique insights were gained by practicing the four satipaṭṭhānā meditations.

title: SN 47.4 Sālā Sutta: At Sālā
description: Even Arahants remain focused on the four *satipaṭṭhāna*—how much more so should the new monks.

title: SN 47.42 Samudaya Sutta: Origin
description:
> Bhikkhus, I will teach you the origination and the passing away of the four establishments of mindfulness. Listen...

A profound sutta helping us understand what the Buddha meant by the four satipaṭṭhāna.

title: SN 47.43 Magga Sutta: The Path
description:
> The seer of the destruction of birth,  
Compassionate, knows the one-way path

Brahma Sahampati praises the Buddha's reflections on the power of Satipaṭṭhāna Meditation.

title: SN 47.48 Mitta Sutta: Friends
description: You should encourage your friends to practice the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

title: SN 47.6 Sakuṇagghi Sutta: The Hawk
description:
> Move in your own resort, bhikkhus, in your own ancestral domain. Mara will not gain access to those who move in their own resort.

The parable of the quail and the hawk.

title: SN 47.7 Makkaṭa Sutta: A Monkey
description:
> Thinking, ‘I will free both hands,’ he seizes it with his foot; he gets caught there.

The parable of the foolish monkey who gets trapped in tar when it ventures outside its ancestral territory. And what is a mendicant’s ancestral territory? The four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

title: SN 47.8 Sūda Sutta: The Cook
description:
> ... that wise, competent, skilful bhikkhu picks up the sign of his own mind.

Like a cook, a meditator must attend to the signs of their success.

title: SN 47.9 Gilāna Sutta: Sick
description:
> I have taught the Dhamma, Ānanda, without making a distinction between inside and outside. The Tathagata has no closed fist of a teacher in regard to the teachings.

The Buddha overcomes an illness and gives Ānanda a sermon on how he leads the Saṅgha—and how the Saṅgha should function after he's g

title: SN 48.10 Indriya Vibhaṅga Sutta: The Second Discourse Giving an Analysis of the Faculties
description:
> The Faculty of Faith, the Faculty of Energy, the Faculty of Mindfulness, the Faculty of Concentration, the Faculty of Wisdom.

A sutta good to contemplate or chant, the analysis of the five spiritual faculties provides a fascinating alternative perspective on the path to awakening.

title: SN 48.12 Paṭhama Saṁkhitta Sutta: The First Brief Discourse
description:
> The faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom are the five faculties.

One who has developed the five faculties fully is a perfected one. Developing them to a lesser degree, one reaches lesser attainments.

title: SN 48.17 Tatiya Vitthāra Sutta: The Third in Detail
description:
> if you practice fully you succeed fully. If you practice partially you succeed partially. These five faculties are not a waste...

<!---->

title: SN 48.3 Dutiyasotāpanna Sutta: A Stream-Enterer (2nd)
description: One who understands the origin, the passing, the gratification, the danger, and the escape regarding the five faculties is a stream-enterer.

title: SN 48.40 The Uppaṭipāṭika Sutta: Irregular Order
description: A fascinating description of the four jhānas and nirodha as the cessation of pain, sadness, pleasure, happiness, and equanimity respectively.

title: SN 48.41 Jarādhamma Sutta: Old Age
description:
> … the limbs are flabby & wrinkled; the back, bent forward

When Ānanda sees the Buddha’s sense faculties fading, the Buddha speaks on the decrepitude of old age.

title: SN 48.45 Paṭhamapubbārāma Sutta: At the Eastern Monastery (1st)
description:
> When a noble disciple has wisdom, the faith, energy, mindfulness, and immersion that follow along with that become stabilized.

Having developed wisdom a mendicant is awakened.

title: SN 48.50 Āpaṇa Sutta: At Āpaṇa on Faith
description: Venerable Sāriputta explains how a faithful disciple uses their faith to develop the path.

title: SN 48.54 Pada Sutta: Footprints
description: Just as all footprints fit into that of an elephant, wisdom is the chief of qualities that lead to awakening.

title: SN 48.8 Daṭṭhabbaṁ Sutta: To Be Seen
description:
> The faculty of conviction, the faculty of persistence, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, & the faculty of discernment.

The Buddha defines the Five Spiritual Faculties (*indriya*).

title: SN 48.9 Paṭhama Vibhaṅga Sutta: The First Analysis
description: The Buddha defines the five spiritual faculties.

title: SN 49.23-34 Balakaraṇīya Vagga: Hard Work
description: > Mendicants, all the hard work that gets done depends on the earth...

title: SN 5.10 Vajirā Sutta: Vajira
description:
> Why now do you assume ‘a being’?
Mara, is that your speculative view?
This is a heap of sheer formations:
Here no being is found.

Māra asks the nun Vajirā about who has created this being. Recognizing him, she points out that the word “being” is nothing more than a convention used to designate th

title: SN 5 Bhikkhuni-samyutta: Discourses (to Māra) of the Ancient Nuns
description:
> One to whom it might occur,  
‘I'm a woman’ or ‘I'm a man’  
Or ‘I'm anything at all’--  
Is fit for Māra to address.

<!---->

title: SN 51.10 Cetiya Sutta: At the Shrine
description:
> But even though the Buddha dropped such an obvious hint, such a clear sign, Ānanda didn’t beg the Buddha, ‘Sir, may the Blessed One please remain for the eon!’

<!---->

title: SN 51.11 Pubba Sutta: Before
description:
> What’s the cause for the development of the bases of psychic power?

<!---->

title: SN 51.15 Uṇṇābhabrāhmaṇa Sutta: The Brahmin Uṇṇābha
description:
> They formerly had the desire to attain perfection, but when they attained perfection the corresponding desire faded away.

The Venerable Ānanda explains to the Brahmin Uṇṇābha how the right kind of desire leads to the end of desire.

title: SN 51.20 Iddhipāda-Vibhaṅga Sutta: Analysis of the Bases of Power
description:
> [He dwells] by night as by day, and by day as by night. By means of an awareness thus open & unhampered, he develops a brightened mind.

The Buddha teaches the bases for psychic power and analyzes them in detail.

title: SN 52.10 Bāḷhagilāna Sutta: Gravely Ill
description:
> "What meditation does Venerable Anuruddha practice so that physical pain doesn’t occupy his mind?"

<!---->

title: SN 52.8 Salaḷāgāra Sutta: The Frankincense-Tree Hut
description:
> The Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. It’s not easy to make it slant, slope, and incline to the west.

Even if kings beg them to disrobe, a mendicant who has developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation is unmoved. Their mind flows to Nibbāna.

title: SN 52.9 Ambapālivana Sutta: In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
description:
> [He] who is released through right gnosis often dwells with a mind well-established in these four establishings of mindfulness.

Sāriputta asks why Anuruddha looks so bright, and he replies that it is due to developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, explaining that (and demonstrating wh

title: SN 54.11 Icchānaṅgala Sutta: At Icchānaṅgala
description: The Buddha describes how he meditated during a three-month retreat.

title: SN 54.13 Ānanda Sutta: To Ananda
description: > Concentration through mindfulness of in-&-out breathing, when developed & pursued, brings the four establishings of mindfulness to completion. The four establishings of mindfulness, when developed & pursued, bring the seven factors for awakening to completion. The seven factors for awakening, when

title: SN 54.9 Vesālī Sutta: At Vesālī
description: The Buddha taught the meditation on the ugliness of the body, then left to go on retreat. However, many monks, misconstruing the teachings, ended up killing themselves. The Buddha taught breath meditation as a peaceful and pleasant alternative.

title: SN 55.1 Cakkavatti Rāja Sutta: A Wheel-Turning Monarch
description:
> … mendicants, gaining these four continents is not worth a sixteenth part of gaining these four things.

Even a universal monarch may have a bad rebirth, but someone who has attained Stream Entry is freed from such unfortunate destinies.

title: SN 55.11 Sahassa Bhikkhunisaṁgha Sutta: A Thousand Nuns
description:
> A noble disciple who has these four things is a stream-enterer

One of the few suttas in the Canon where the Buddha directly teaches Bhikkhunīs.

title: SN 55.17 Dutiya Mittā Macca Sutta: The Second Sutta on Friends and Relatives
description:
> If they listen to your advice, you should establish them in the four factors of stream-entry.

You should encourage your friends in the Dhamma.

title: SN 55.21 Paṭhamamahānāma Sutta: The First Sutta With Mahānāma
description: In which the Buddha reassures a devout follower that it's their habits of mind, not the mind's exact state at the moment of death, which will determine their rebirth.

title: SN 55.22 Mahānāma Sutta: The Second Sutta With Mahānāma
description:
> Suppose a tree were leaning toward the east... When its root is cut, which way would it fall?

Mahānāma the Sakyan expresses his fear that if he dies unmindful he may be reborn into a lower realm. The Buddha tells him not to worry, as he will definitely go to a good place, having established the f

title: SN 55.24 Paṭhamasaraṇānisakka Sutta: Sarakāni (1)
description:
> "It’s incredible, it’s amazing! Who can’t become a stream-enterer these days?"

It's never too late to practice.

title: SN 55.38 Vassa Sutta: Rain
description:
> In the same way, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha...

Like rain falling on the mountain top, the four factors of stream-entry flow on to the ending of defilements.

title: SN 55.40 The Nandiya Sutta: To Nandiya
description:
> This is how a disciple of the noble ones dwells

> … the person in whom the factors of stream entry are altogether and in every way lacking I call an outsider

<!---->

title: SN 55.41 Paṭhama Abhisanda Sutta: The First Discourse on Overflowing Merit
description: The four factors of stream-entry—with ethics as the fourth—are streams of merit, and like the ocean cannot be fathomed.

title: SN 55.43 The Tatiya Asaṇkheyya Sutta: Incalculable 3
description:
> When a noble disciple has these four streams of merit [...] his merit simply is incalculable

The four factors of stream-entry—with wisdom as the fourth—are called streams of merit.

title: SN 55.44 Paṭhamamahaddhana Sutta: Rich (1st)
description:
> … a noble disciple who has four things is said to be rich, prosperous, and wealthy.

The four factors of stream-entry are said to be true prosperity.

title: SN 55.5 The Sāriputta Sutta: To Sāriputta
description:
> Association with people of integrity, lord, is a factor for stream entry.

The Buddha asks Sāriputta about the four factors for stream-entry: association with good people, hearing the teaching, proper attention, and right practice. He also defines the “stream” and the “Sotāpanna” in this omnibus s

title: SN 55.51 Sagāthaka Sutta: With Verses
description:
> who has confidence in the Saṅgha,  
and correct view:  
they’re said to be prosperous,  
their life is not in vain.

The four factors of stream entry.

title: SN 55.52 Vassaṁvuttha Sutta: One Who Completed the Rains
description: A monk reports that the Buddha said that increasingly high levels of attainment are increasingly rare.

title: SN 55.54 Gilāna Sutta: Sick
description:
> … how a wise lay follower should advise another wise lay follower who is sick

Ending with a rather unusual description of the path as turning the mind progressively higher.

title: SN 55.7 Veḷudvāreyya Sutta: The People of Bamboo Gate
description: The Buddha explains "The Golden Rule" to a group of Brahmin householders.

title: SN 56.10 Tiracchānakathā Sutta: Unworthy Talk
description:
> Mendicants, don’t engage in all kinds of low talk, such as...

<!---->

title: SN 56.11 Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta: The Discourse on Setting in Motion the Wheel of the Dhamma
description:
The Buddha's first discourse.

Note: The PDF linked above is from [Bhikkhu Bodhi's translation, courtesy of SuttaCentral](https://suttacentral.net/sn56.11/en/bodhi).

title: SN 56.21 Koṭigāma Sutta: At the Village of Koṭi 1
description:
> … not understanding and not comprehending the Noble Truth of suffering, both you and I have wandered and journeyed in this cycle of birth and death for a very long time

<!---->

title: SN 56.23 Sammāsambuddha Sutta: A Fully Awakened Buddha
description: An Arahant is one who understands the Four Noble Truths.

title: SN 56.27 Tatha Sutta: Real
description:
> These four noble truths are real, not unreal, with no alteration. That is why they are called ‘noble truths.’

<!---->

title: SN 56.29 Pariññeyya Sutta: Should Be Completely Understood
description:
> Of these Four Noble Truths, there is one to be completely understood, one to be abandoned, one to be realized, and one to be developed.

<!---->

title: SN 56.31 Sīsapāvana Sutta: In the Rosewood Forest
description:
> What do you think, bhikkhus, which is more numerous: these few siṁsapa leaves that I have taken up in my hand or those in the siṁsapa grove overhead?

The Buddha taught only a fraction of what he knows.

title: SN 56.32 Khadirapatta Sutta: Acacia Leaves
description:
> Having made a basket of acacia leaves or of pine needles or of myrobalan leaves, I will bring water...

<!---->

title: SN 56.33 Daṇḍa Sutta: A Stick
description:
> Bhikkhus, just as a stick thrown up into the air falls now on its bottom, now on its top, so too beings roam and wander on...

Beings who have not seen the four noble truths roam on from one birth to another, like a stick thrown end over end.

title: SN 56.34 Cela Sutta: Clothes
description:
> Bhikkhus, if one’s clothes or head were ablaze, what should be done about it?

Even more than if your clothes are on fire, you should make an effort to understand the four noble truths.

title: SN 56.35 Sattisata Sutta: A Hundred Spears
description:
> Rather, the breakthrough to the Four Noble Truths is accompanied only by happiness and joy.

Even more than if you’re being tortured with spikes, you should make an effort to realize Nibbāna.

title: SN 56.36 Pāṇa Sutta: Living Creatures
description:
> Still, bhikkhus, the gross creatures in the ocean would not be exhausted even after all the grass, sticks, branches, and foliage in Jambudipa had been used up and exhausted.
[...] So vast, bhikkhus, is the plane of misery.

Your rebirths are more numerous than the leaves in India.

title: SN 56.37 Paṭhamasūriya Sutta: The First Simile of the Sun
description:
> Therefore, bhikkhus, an exertion should be made to understand...

As the dawn precedes the sunrise, right view precedes the penetration of the four noble truths.

title: SN 56.38 Dutiyasūriya Sutta: The Second Simile of the Sun
description:
> But, bhikkhus, when a Tathagata arises in the world, an Arahant, a Perfectly Enlightened One, then there is the manifestation of great light and radiance; then no blinding darkness prevails, no dense mass of darkness...

<!---->

title: SN 56.39 Indakhīla Sutta: A Boundary Pillar
description:
> … they do not look up at the face of another ascetic or brahmin, thinking: ‘This worthy is surely one who really knows, who really sees.’ For what reason? Because, bhikkhus, they have clearly seen the Four Noble Truths.

One who has not seen the Dhamma is fickle and easily misled.

title: SN 56.40 Vādatthika Sutta: Seeking an Argument
description:
> ... seeking an argument, searching for an argument, thinking: ‘I will refute his thesis,’ it is impossible that he could make that bhikkhu shake...

No-one can refute you if you are well grounded in the four noble truths.

title: SN 56.48 Dutiyachiggaḷayuga Sutta: A Yoke With a Hole (2)
description: In this famous simile, the Buddha explains how rare it is to receive a human rebirth in the time of a Buddha and encourages us to use the opportunity well.

title: SN 56.51 Nakhasikhā Sutta: A Fingernail
description:
> What do you think, mendicants? Which is more: the little bit of dirt under my fingernail, or this great earth?

The Buddha explains the fruit of Stream Entry.

title: SN 56.9 Viggāhika Kathā Sutta: Arguments
description:
> Bhikkhus, do not engage in disputatious talk

Don’t argue. Instead, converse on the four noble truths.

title: SN 6.1 Brahmāyācana Sutta: The Appeal of Brahmā
description:
> Open are the doors to the deathless!  
Let those with ears show their faith  

After his awakening, the Buddha hesitated to teach, thinking that the Dhamma is too subtle for people to understand. But Brahmā Sahampati appears and encourages him to teach, pointing out that there are those with “litt

title: SN 6.13 Andhakavinda Sutta: At Andhakavinda
description:
> Where dreadful serpents slither,  
where the lightning flashes and the sky thunders  
in the dark of the night;  
there meditates a mendicant

Brahmā Sahampati appears to the Buddha and speaks in praise of the renunciates staying fearless in the deep forest, and celebrates the many who have found

title: SN 6.2 Gārava Sutta: Respect
description:
> What if I were to dwell in dependence on this very Dhamma to which I have fully awakened, honoring & respecting it?

What a Buddha bows to.

title: SN 6.9: Turū Brahma Sutta: With the Brahmā Tudu
description:
> Man is born  
with an axe in his mouth.

Brahmā Tudu tries to persuade Kokālika to have faith in Sāriputta and Moggallāna.

title: SN 7.10 Bahudhītara Sutta: Many Daughters
description:
> You’re right, brahmin, I don’t have  
fourteen oxen  
missing ...

A brahmin is searching for his lost oxen when he sees the Buddha meditating peacefully in the forest. He laments the many sorrows of his life, celebrating the Buddha’s happiness and freedom from worldly sorrows.

title: SN 7.11 Kasi Bhāradvāja Sutta: With Bhāradvāja the Farmer
description:
> Tell me how you’re a farmer when asked:  
how am I to recognize your farming?

A brahmin farmer criticizes the Buddha for failing to be productive, merely living off the work of others, so the Buddha explains his line of work.

title: SN 7.12 Udaya Sutta: With Udaya
description:
> Again & again one wearies & trembles.  
Again & again the dullard goes to the womb.

A brahmin complains when the Buddha visits for alms many days in a row.
The Buddha takes the chance to point out that all natural phenomena repeat in cycles, and only an awakened one escapes the cycle of rebirth.

title: SN 7.14 Mahāsāla Sutta: A Well-to-do Brahmin Father
description:
> Master Gotama, I have four sons. At their wives’ order my sons chased me out from my house.

The Buddha gives him a verse to recite in the village council, contrasting his faithless sons to his trusty wooden staff. Ashamed, the sons take back their father.

title: SN 7.15 Mānatthaddha Sutta: Big-headed One
description: A stuck-up brahmin listening to the Buddha is awestruck when the Buddha appears to read his mind.

title: SN 7.19 Mātuposaka Sutta: Supporting One's Mother
description:
> ... praised in this life by the astute,  
they depart to rejoice in heaven.

The Buddha gives encouragement to a brahmin seeking alms for his parents.

title: SN 7.2 Akkosa Sutta: The Abuser
description: The Buddha is confronted by an angry and rude Brahmin.

title: SN 7.3 Asundarika Sutta: Asundarika
description:
> if you’re patient, mindful and calm,  
then you act for the good of both  
for yourself and the other person

A brahmin visits the Buddha and abuses him, but the Buddha responds with patience.

title: SN 7.6 Jata Sutta: The Tangle
description:
> … who can untangle this tangle?

A brahmin with matted hair asks the Buddha how we can become disentangled. This short set of verses became one of the most important in all of Theravāda Buddhism when it was used as the cornerstone of Buddhaghosa's [*Visuddhimagga*](/content/canon/vsm_buddhaghosa).

title: SN 8.5 Subhāsita Sutta: Well-Spoken Words
description:
> … speech that has four factors is well spoken

For Thanissaro Bhikkhu's translation of this sutta, see [Snp 3.3](/content/canon/snp3.3).

title: SN 8.6 Sāriputta Sutta: With Sāriputta
description:
> Venerable Vaṅgīsa thought, 'This Venerable Sāriputta is educating the mendicants...'

<!---->

title: SN 9.1 Viveka Sutta: Seclusion
description:
> You, a person:  
subdue your desire for people.

A monk in the forest lets his mind drift to thoughts of the lay life, and is exhorted by a local deity.

title: SN 9.14 Gandhatthena Sutta: The Scent Thief
description: In which a _deva_ chastises a monk for sniffing a flower!

title: SN 9.2 Upaṭṭhāna Sutta: Getting Up
description:
> … why bother a renunciate?

When a mendicant falls asleep in the middle of the day, a deity tries to rouse them. But not all is at it seems.

title: SN 9.6 Anuruddha Sutta: With Anuruddha
description: A former partner of Venerable Anuruddha, now a deity named Jālinī, tries to tempt him with heavenly pleasures. But he has seen a higher happiness.

title: Snp 1.1 Uraga Sutta: The Serpent
description:
> such a monk gives up the here and the beyond,  
just as a serpent sheds its worn-out skin

As we advance along the path, we shed our old attachments.

title: Snp 1.10 Āḷavaka Sutta: Āḷavaka the Demon
description:
> How does one cross over the flood?  
How does one cross over the ocean?

A *yakkha* demon challenges the Buddha with riddles and threatens to “hurl out his mind, rip open his heart, or hurl him across the River Ganges” if he doesn’t solve the riddles to the *yakkha*’s satisfaction.

title: Snp 1.11 Vijaya Sutta: Victory
description:
> Linked together by bones and sinews,  
plastered over with flesh and hide,  
and covered by the skin …

Gain victory over the defilements with this one weird trick (contemplation of the unattractiveness of the body).

> Then there is the hollow head  
all filled with brains.  
Governed by ignoranc

title: Snp 1.2 Dhaniya Sutta: With the Cattle-owner Dhaniya
description:
> Whoso has boys, has sorrow of his boys,  
Whoso has kine, by kine come his annoys.  
Man’s assets, these of all his woes are chief.  
Who has no more, no more has grief.

In this dramatic poem, the Buddha and a cowherd debate who is more prepared for a coming storm.

title: Snp 1.3 Khaggavisāṇa Sutta: The Rhinceros Horn Sutta
description:
> … seeing this danger in association,  
fare singly as the rhino’s horn.

If you can’t find a good teacher, it’s better to wander alone than to consort with fools.

title: Snp 1.4 Kasi Bharadvaja Sutta:: To the Plowing Bharadvaja
description:
> Faith is the seed, practice the rain,  
And wisdom is my yoke and plow.

A simile on the work of the practice as farming.

title: Snp 1.6 Parābhava Sutta: Downfalls for a Lay Follower
description: The various actions and attitudes that lead to spiritual decline.

title: Snp 1.8 Karaniya Metta Sutta: The Buddha's Words on Loving-Kindness
description:
> One should sustain this recollection

The Buddha describes, in this much beloved poem from the Sutta Nipata, how to cultivate loving-kindness.

title: Snp 1.9 Hemavata Sutta: The Buddha Teaches Sātāgira and Hemavata, the Native Spirits
description:
> Abstaining from perceptions of sensuality,  
overcoming all fetters,  
having totally ended delight in becoming,  
one doesn’t sink  
into the deep.

The Buddha explains to a *yakkha* how one crosses over the flood.

title: Snp 2.1 Ratana Sutta: Gems
description: An annotated, line-by-line translation of this famous Pāli chant.

title: Snp 2.10 Uṭṭhāna Sutta: Get up!
description:
> Get up and meditate!  
What’s the point in your sleeping?

Don’t let an opportunity for practice pass you by.

title: Snp 2.14 Dhammika Sutta: With Dhammika
description:
> A diligent layperson observing these duties  
Ascends to the gods called Self-luminous.

The proper code of conduct for followers of the Dhamma.

title: Snp 2.4 Maṅgala Sutta: Greatest Blessings
description:
> Those who thus abide, ever remain invincible, in happiness established. These are the greatest blessings.

<!---->

title: Snp 2.6 Kapila/Dhammacariya Sutta: A Righteous Life
description:
> One such as that is  
like a sewer  
brimful with years of filth  
for it’s hard to clean one full of grime.

The Buddha encourages the monks to just expell those who are wicked and stubborn.

title: Snp 2.8 The Dhamma Nāvā Sutta: The Boat
description:
> a knowledge master,  
evolved, learned, and unflappable—  
can help others to contemplate,  
so long as they are prepared to listen carefully.

A good teacher, like a good boatman, is one who knows firsthand how to cross to the further shore.

title: Snp 3.1 Pabbajjā Sutta: Going Forth
description:
A rare glimpse into the Bodhisattva's journey.

Make sure to also read [Bhante's translation notes](https://discourse.suttacentral.net/t/snp-3-1-pabbajjasutta-the-going-forth/26844?u=khemarato.bhikkhu){:ga-event-value="0.8" target="_blank"} as well.

title: Snp 3.10 Kokālika Sutta: With Kokālika
description:
> Kokālika has been reborn in the Pink Lotus hell because of his resentment for Sāriputta and Moggallāna.

A follower of Devadatta slanders Ven. Sāriputta and Ven. Moggallāna and, after suffering a painful disease, dies. The sutta then gives a graphic description of the sufferings awaiting him in he

title: Snp 3.11 Nālaka Sutta: The Sages Asita and Nālaka and the Buddha’s advice
description:
> Knowledge of Silence I’ll convey,  
hard to do, to master difficult,  
so be both firm and resolute  
and I’ll speak upon this thing.

> Understand this by the way streams move  
in clefts and crevices:  
the little creeks flow on babbling,  
while silent flow the great rivers.  
>  
> What is unf

title: Snp 3.12 Dvayatānupassanā Sutta: Contemplating Pairs
description:
> When a mendicant meditates rightly contemplating a pair of teachings in this way—diligent, keen, and resolute—they can expect one of two results: enlightenment in the present life or, if there’s something left over, non-return.

Not all dualities are misleading. This sutta teaches ways to contempl

title: Snp 3.2 Padhāna Sutta: Striving
description:
> As I strove to subdue myself  
beside the broad Nerañjarā...

Māra attempts to dissuade the Bodhisatta from his path.

title: Snp 3.3 Subhasita Sutta: Well-Spoken
description:
> Truth, indeed, is deathless speech

A short sutta on Right Speech, equivalent to [SN 8.5](/content/canon/sn8.5).

title: Snp 3.5 Māgha Sutta: With Māgha on Giving
description: What are the qualities of a recipient that produce the most merit from a gift?

title: Snp 3.6 Sabhiya Sutta: Sabhiya’s Questions
description: A wanderer, disappointed in the teachings he has received from other teachers, approaches the Buddha with his questions on the goal of the holy life.

title: Snp 3.8 Salla Sutta: The Arrow
description:
> not by weeping & grief  
do you gain peace

A poem about facing death squarely and realistically.

title: Snp 3.9 Vāseṭṭha Sutta: The Discourse to Vāseṭṭha
description:
> We ask Gotama, the Eye that has arisen in the world:  
Is one a brahmin by birth, or by action?  
Explain to us what we do not understand –  
how to know a brahmin.

What makes someone respectable?

title: Snp 4.1 Kāma Sutta: Objects, Desires, Pleasures
description:
> let a mindful one avoid at every turn  
these sense-desires,  
with them abandoned,  
cross the flood

<!---->

title: Snp 4.11 Kalahavivāda Sutta: Quarrels and Disputes
description:
> Whenever there are arguments and quarrels, tears and anguish, arrogance and pride, and grudges and insults to go with them, can you explain how these things come about?

The Buddha is questioned on the source of quarrels and disputes, and on the highest level of spiritual attainment.

title: Snp 4.15 Attadaṇḍa Sutta: Taking Up Arms
description:
> I saw this population flounder,  
like a fish in a little puddle.  
Seeing them fight each other,  
fear came upon me.

The Buddha speaks in poignant terms of the saṁvega that led him to leave the household life. He concludes with recommendations for practice and a description of the person who ha

title: Snp 4.2 Guhaṭṭhaka Sutta: The Eight on the Body as a Cave
description:
> The person who’s to their body-cave  
Clouded by many moods...

Those who remain attached to the body, to sensuality, and to their sense of “mine” will have a hard time freeing themselves from fear of death and from further rebirths.

title: Snp 4.5 Paramaṭṭhaka Sutta: Eight on the Ultimate
description:
> Whoever should take to himself certain views,
thinking them the best...

The conceit that comes from clinging to practices or views—even if they’re supreme—is a fetter.

title: Snp 4.6 Jara Sutta: On Decay
description:
> A wise man is not deluded by what is perceived by the senses. He does not expect purity by any other way.

Life is short. Possessiveness brings grief. Freedom comes from abandoning any sense of “mine.”

title: Snp 4.7 Tissametteyya Sutta: To Tissametteyya (on the Dangers of Sex)
description:
> Someone who formerly lived alone  
and then resorts to sex  
is like a chariot careening off-track;

The drawbacks of falling away from the celibate life.

title: Snp 4.9 Māgaṇḍiya Sutta: With Māgaṇḍiya
description:
> What is this body full of piss and shit?  
I wouldn’t even want to touch it with my foot.

Māgandiya offers the Buddha his daughter in marriage. The Buddha refuses and further subdues Māgandiya’s pride by describing a state of peace Māgandiya doesn’t even understand.

title: Snp 5.10 Todeyyamāṇavapucchā: The Questions of Kappa
description:
> a perilous flood has arisen,  
for those oppressed by old age and death,  
let me declare an island to you.  
Owning nothing, taking nothing:  
this is the island with nothing further.  
I call this [island] ‘*nibbāna*,’  
the extinction of old age and death.

How to recognize an emancipated perso

title: Snp 5.15 Posālamāṇavapucchā: Posāla's Question
description:
> One who has entirely given up the body, ...
how should one like that be guided?

How to develop insight after mastering the perception of nothingness.

title: Snp 5.19 Pārāyanānugītigāthā: Preserving the Way to the Beyond
description:
> I shall keep reciting the Way to the Beyond

Perhaps the last sutta of the early Pāli Canon, the *Pārāyanānugītigāthā* extols the virtues of the Buddha and of those who preserve, and realize, his teachings.

title: Snp 5.4 Puṇṇakamāṇavapucchā: The Questions of Mettagū
description:
> The ignorant dullard who creates acquisition  
encounters suffering again and again.  
Therefore, understanding, one should not create acquisition,  of
contemplating it as the genesis and origin of suffering.

Ordinary and Enlightened beings contrasted, with intimations of the path between the two

title: Snp 5.6 Dhotakamāṇavapucchā: The Questions of the Student Dhotaka
description:
> I rejoice, great seer,  
in that supreme peace

How can one become freed of all doubts?
How does one continue to advance even after stream-entry?

title: Snp 5.7 Upasīvamāṇavapucchā: The Questions of Upasīva
course: nibbana # duplicate with *The Island* but that's okay...
description: The Buddha gives pithy answers to Upasīva about the path to liberation and the status of anāgāmīs and arahants.

title: T0203 雜寶藏經: The Storehouse of Sundry Valuables
description:
A collection of 121 stories ostensibly from the Sarvāstivāda spanning from the time of Śākyamuni and his disciples to the era of King Kaniṣka and Aśvaghoṣa in the second century C.E.

The collection notably includes a northern version of Ven. Nāgasena’s conversion of King Milinda (111, Fascicle 9) a

title: T0389 佛垂般涅槃略說敎誡經: The Sutra of the Teachings Left Behind by the Buddha
description:
> Now he lay among the Sal trees, about to enter Nirvana. The time was the middle of the night, calm and noiseless. For the sake of all the disciples, he briefly spoke of the most important doctrines...

An English translation of a popular Zen liturgical sutra.

title: T0600 十善業道經: The Ten Wholesome Courses of Action
description:
The Buddha enumerates the ten meritorious deeds and their lavish results to a great Naga king.

This sutra builds on themes found in Early Buddhist texts such as the Natha Sutta (AN 10.17) giving an example of how later Buddhists texts evolved from earlier ones.

title: The Sutra on the Eight Realizations of Great Beings
translator: Diem Thanh Truong and Carole Melkonian
description: An English translation of a Vietnamese translation of (and commentary on) ["The Eight Great Awakenings Sutra" (佛說八大人覺經, T0779)](https://web.archive.org/web/20200413134557/https://fotuozhengfa.com/archives/35339){:target="_blank"} which breaks down right view into eight components.

title: The Śūraṅgama Sūtra (T. 945)
description:

description: This is a Pāli-English translation of ten stories from the commentary to Apadāna 39.10 on the unwholesome actions undertaken by the Bodhisatta in past lives and their karmic repercussions in his final life.

title: Ud 4.4 Yakkhapahāra Sutta: The Discourse about Moonlight
description: Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahāmoggallāna meditate together in peace not even a _yakkha_ could disturb.

title: Ud 4.5 Nāga Sutta: The Discourse about the Nāga Elephant
description: In this sutta we see the Buddha exemplifying the two uses of nature on the path: as a site for seclusion and as an opportunity for reflection.

title: Ud 4.8 Sundarī Sutta: The Discourse about Sundarī
description:
> having heard that rough speech broadcast around,  
A monk should bear it with an uncorrupt mind.

After wanderers of other sects attempt to frame the Buddhist monks for the murder of Sundarī, the Buddha teaches the monks how to respond to false accusations.

title: Ud 5.1 Piyatara Sutta: The Discourse about the King
description: A Queen gives her King an honest answer, and the Buddha gives us the very pith of ethics.

title: Ud 5.2 Appāyuka Sutta: Short-lived
description: Ven. Ānanda comments on how the Buddha’s mother died shortly after his birth and the Buddha says this is true of all Bodhisattas.

title: Ud 5.3 Suppabuddha Kuṭṭhi Sutta: With Suppabuddha the Leper
description:
> A wise man in the world of the living should avoid bad deeds.

Reflection Questions:
  1. What was the cause of this man's leprosy?
  2. How does the Buddha treat him?
  3. What attitude towards lepers does this sutta encourage us to have?

title: Ud 5.5 Uposatha Sutta: Sabbath
description:
> Then the Venerable Mahamoggllana took that person by the arm, pulled him outside the gate, and bolted it.

<!---->

title: Ud 5.6 Soṇa Sutta: With Soṇa
description:
A young man in a remote part of India is able to ordain only after many delays.
Eventually he meets the Buddha, who rejoices in his erudition.

title: Ud 5.7 Kaṅkhārevata Sutta: Revata
description:
> Whatever doubts there are...  
The meditators give up all these

The Buddha rejoices in Ven. Revata's diligent meditation.

title: Ud 5.8 Saṁghabheda Sutta: A Schism in the Saṅgha
description:
> Evil, for the evil, is easy to do.

Devadatta announces that he will cause a split in the Sangha.

title: Ud 5.9 Sadhāyamāna Sutta: Jeering
description: > False pundits, totally muddled...

title: Ud 6.2 Satta Jaṭila Sutta: Seven Matted-Hair Ascetics
description:
> ..accepting gold and money, it’s hard for you to know who is perfected or on the path to perfection.

How to judge another person’s spiritual character.

title: Ud 6.3 Paccavekkhaṇa Sutta: The Buddha’s Reviewing
description:
> Then at that time the Gracious One was sitting reflecting on his own abandonment of countless bad, unwholesome things, and how through development countless wholesome things had come to fulfilment.

<!---->

title: Ud 6.4 Paṭhamanānātitthiya Sutta: Various Sectarians (1)
description: The famous simile of the blind men and the elephant.

title: Ud 6.9 Upātidhāvanti Sutta: Hastening By
description:
> Then at that time many moths rushing and falling down into those oil lamps, were coming to grief, were coming to ruin.

Like moths to the flame, living beings are draw to appearances at their own peril.

title: Ud 7.4 Dutiya Satta Sutta: The Second Discourse on Clinging
description: > ... most of the people in Sāvatthī were excessively attached to sensual pleasures...

title: Ud 7.7 Papañcakhaya Sutta: The Ending of Proliferations
description:
> Then the Blessed One, realizing his own abandoning of the perceptions & categories of objectification, on that occasion exclaimed...

<!---->

title: Ud 7.9 Udapāna Sutta: The Well
description:
> What need for a well  
if there were waters always?

Wanderers of other sects try to keep the Buddha from drinking the water in a well.

title: Ud 8.1 Paṭhama Nibbānapaṭisaṁyutta Sutta: The First Discourse About Nibbāna
description:
> ... no coming or going or remaining or passing away or reappearing. It is not established, does not proceed, and has no support. Just this is the end of suffering.

The nature of Nibbāna especially as differentiated from the (other) attainments of Samādhi.

title: Ud 8.2 Dutiya Nibbāna Paṭisaṁyutta Sutta: The Second Exclamation About Nibbāna
description:
> It’s hard to see the unaffected...

<!---->

title: Ud 8.3 Tatiya Nibbāna Paṭisaṁyutta Sutta: The Third Discourse about Nibbāna
description:
> There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned.

The escape from conditions exists.

See also, [Iti 43](/content/canon/iti43).

title: Ud 8.4 Catuttha Nibbāna Paṭisaṁyutta Sutta: The Fourth Connected Discourse About Extinguishment
description:
> For the independent there’s no agitation. When there’s no agitation there is tranquility.

Nibbāna is true independence.

title: Ud 8.6 Pāṭaligāmiya Sutta: The Layfolk of Pāṭali Village
description:
> He should dedicate an offering  
To the deities there.  
Venerated, they venerate him

A few verses on how to become "beloved of the gods" get a framing narrative glorifying the Magadha Kingdom.

Many Buddhist kingdoms (down to the present day) create (or promote) apocryphal stories to justify the

title: Ud 8.7 Dvidhāpatha Sutta: A Fork in the Road
description:
> Walking together, dwelling as one,  
the knowledge master mixes with foolish folk.

One of the Buddha’s attendants learns to listen to the Buddha's advice.

title: Ud 8.8 Visākhā Sutta: The Discourse about Visākhā
description:
> For those who have one love, they have one suffering.  
For those who love nothing, they have no sorrow.

Lady Visākhā wished for many grandchildren.

title: Daily Confession (from the Vajrapañjara Tantra)
description: > In the Three Jewels, I take refuge...

description: The Theravāda Abhidhamma's Canonical analysis of Dependant Arising.

title: Vb 7 Satipaṭṭhāna Vibhaṅga: Analysis of the Ways of Attending to Mindfulness
description: The Theravāda Abhidhamma's Canonical analysis of Satipaṭṭhāna Meditation.

Ostensibly a commentary on a single verse from the Dhammapada, this classic work synthesize

title: Vv 1.1 Paṭhama Pīṭha Sutta: Throne Mansion
description:
> Raising my hands and putting my palms and fingers together, I saluted

Arahant Moggallana asks a deva about his previous good karma.

title: Vv 1.10 Tiladakkhiṇā Sutta: Sesame-Gift Mansion
description:
> I did not have valuable things to offer. But still, I offered some

Even a small offering of sesame seeds to the Buddha brought much merit.

title: Vv 1.12 Dutiya Patibbatā Sutta: Second Honest Wife Mansion
description:
> Because of these meritorious deeds, I have been born as a very beautiful devata

A deva explains how keeping the precepts and being a follower of the Supreme Buddha can bring happiness.

title: Vv 1.14 Dutiya Suṇisā Sutta: Second Daughter-in-Law Mansion
description:
> To my amazement, I was reborn in the heavenly Nandana Park as a goddess!

A deva explains how offering a honey covered cake led her to rebirth in a heavenly park.

title: Vv 1.8 Tatiya Nāvā Sutta: Third Ship Mansion
description:
> One day, I saw several monks who were very thirsty and had fallen to the ground. I got up quickly and offered them water to drink.

A deva explains the merit accumulated when she offered water to a monk who had fallen.

title: Vv 2.1 Dāsī Sutta: Servant Girl’s Mansion
description:
> I was a servant working in other people’s houses. But I was very fortunate...

Only those who do good deeds are reborn in heaven.

title: Vv 2.11 Dutiya Bhikkhadāyikā Sutta: Second Alms Giver’s Mansion
description:
> I happily offered alms to that Arahant monk with my own hands. Because of this meritorious deed, I have been born as a very beautiful devata

<!---->

title: Vv 2.8 Saddhā Sutta: Saddha’s Mansion
description:
> I had unshakable faith in the Triple Gem. I practiced the precepts and delighted in giving and sharing.

A deva explains how they came to be reborn in heaven.

title: Vv 3.6 Daddalla Sutta: Dazzling Mansion
description:
> But I offered them much more food than you did! Yet, I have been born in a lower heavenly world. Having offered very little, how did you receive more happiness than me?

Devas discuss the importance of thinking about the whole Noble Sangha when giving alms instead of individual monks.

title: Vv 3.9 Visālakkhī Sutta: Mansion of the Beautiful-Eyed Goddess
description:
> I offered all those flowers with a happy mind

Following the precepts, leading a restrained life, practicing generosity, and having faith brings much happiness.

description:
> Giving is always great.

A deva explains...

title: Vv 6.3 Phaladāyikā Sutta: Fruit Giver’s Mansion
description:
> … one who wishes happiness in the human world and the heavenly world should offer fruit

title: Vv 7.10 Serissaka Sutta: Serissaka’s Mansion
description:
> In this desert, there are no fruits, roots or any food or drink. There is no way to make a fire. There is only dust and scorching sand.

A deva named Serissaka explains how he came to enjoy a comfortable life and what we can do to achieve the same.

title: Vv 7.11 Sunikkhitta Sutta: Sunikkhitta’s Mansion
description:
> I arranged those flowers beautifully while recollecting the great qualities of the Supreme Buddha.

Arahant Moggallana asks what meritorious action a deva did to became so powerful.

title: Vv 7.2 Nandana Sutta: Nandana Mansion
description:
> Because of this meritorious deed, I have been born as a very beautiful deva and enjoy all the wonderful things that delight my heart.

A deva explains the results of taking care of one’s parents and having confidence in monks.

title: Vv 7.8 Anekavaṇṇa Sutta: Mansion of Many Colors
description:
> Although I did not have anything with which to practice generosity, I encouraged others.

A deva explains how worshiping the relics of a Buddha can bring much happiness.

title: Vv 7.9 Maṭṭakuṇḍalī Sutta: Mattakundali’s Mansion
description:
> You seek something that cannot be obtained. I am sure that you will die from sadness: it is impossible to get the sun and moon

A deva shows a grieving father the way to end his sorrow.
